topics.title: Basic MOS Device Physics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic MOS Device Physics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a MOS Device?

The **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (MOS)** structure is the foundational building block of every modern Complementary MOS (CMOS) integrated circuit. It consists of a layered sandwich:

- A **Metal** (or heavily doped polysilicon) gate electrode
- A thin **Oxide** insulator (typically SiO₂) 
- A **Semiconductor** substrate (the "body" or "bulk", almost always p-type silicon in NMOS devices)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A MOS transistor is a four-terminal (Gate, Drain, Source, Body) voltage-controlled semiconductor device whose channel conductivity is modulated by the electric field established by the gate-to-body voltage. The current flows between Drain and Source through an induced "inversion layer" channel.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy: The Faucet Model

Imagine a **water faucet**:
- The **gate voltage (V_GS)** is your hand turning the handle — it does not let water *through*; it simply *opens the valve*.
- The **drain-source voltage (V_DS)** is the **water pressure** pushing the water through.
- The **channel** is the pipe.
- The **drain current (I_D)** is the flow of water.

Just as a faucet with a closed handle leaks almost nothing (sub-threshold leakage), and once opened beyond a certain handle rotation (the **threshold voltage, V_TH**), water flows freely, the MOSFET passes negligible current until V_GS exceeds V_TH, after which I_D grows predictably. This **voltage-controlled conductance** is the cornerstone of digital CMOS logic.

## 1.3 MOS Capacitor — The Heart of the Device

Before a MOSFET can conduct, the **MOS capacitor** (gate-oxide-body structure) must be "charged" properly. The applied gate voltage induces three possible surface conditions in the p-type body:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Operating Regimes of the MOS Surface:**
> 1. **Accumulation** — Holes pile up at the Si–SiO₂ interface (V_GS << 0)
> 2. **Depletion** — Holes are pushed away, leaving a region of uncovered negative acceptor ions (small positive V_GS)
> 3. **Inversion** — A layer of *electrons* (minority carriers) forms at the surface (V_GS > V_TH)

**Key Physical Constants used in this module:**

| Constant | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Silicon intrinsic carrier concentration | $n_i$ | **1.5 × 10¹⁰** | cm⁻³ |
| Electron charge magnitude | $q$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴** | F/cm |
| Relative permittivity of Si | $\varepsilon_{si}$ | **11.7** | — |
| Relative permittivity of SiO₂ | $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | **3.9** | — |
| Thermal voltage at 300 K | $V_T = kT/q$ | **25.85 mV** | V |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of MOS Transistor Action

A MOSFET operates by **field-effect**: the gate voltage produces a vertical electric field across the thin gate oxide, which in turn electrostatically controls the **mobile charge density** in the silicon surface beneath the oxide. The sequence is:

1. **Apply V_GS** → an electric field is established across the oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$.
2. **Field-induced charge** appears at the Si–SiO₂ interface.
3. For V_GS below V_TH → only depletion charge $Q_B$ (ionized acceptors).
4. For V_GS above V_TH → **inversion charge** $Q_I$ (mobile electrons in NMOS) is induced.
5. **Apply V_DS** → these mobile charges drift from Source to Drain, producing I_D.

## 2.2 Threshold Voltage — The Master Parameter

The **threshold voltage** is the gate-source voltage at which the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration (the onset of strong inversion). Its full expression, accounting for the body effect, is:

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)$$

where each term has a clear engineering meaning (see the formula sheet below).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is your exam-day reference.** Memorize the units and the *sign conventions*.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxide capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | F/cm² ; $t_{ox}$ = oxide thickness |
| 2 | Fermi potential of p-substrate | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{N_A}{n_i} \right)$ | Volts ; sign is **negative** for p-type |
| 3 | Surface potential at strong inversion | $\phi_S = -2\phi_F$ | Volts (for NMOS) |
| 4 | Bulk charge per unit area | $Q_B = -\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \varepsilon_0 \, N_A \, \vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert}$ | C/cm² |
| 5 | Zero-bias threshold voltage | $V_{TH0} = V_{FB} + 2\vert\phi_F\vert + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \varepsilon_0 \, N_A \cdot 2\vert\phi_F\vert}}{C_{ox}}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Flat-band voltage | $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ss}}{C_{ox}}$ | Volts |
| 7 | Body-effect coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \varepsilon_0 \, N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | V¹ᐟ² |
| 8 | Drain current (saturation) | $I_D = \dfrac{\mu_n \, C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | Amps |
| 9 | Drain current (triode) | $I_D = \mu_n \, C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \!\left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$ | Amps (valid for $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$) |
| 10 | Saturation voltage | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Volts |
| 11 | On-resistance (triode) | $R_{on} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})}$ | Ohms |
| 12 | Transconductance (sat.) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | S (Siemens) |
| 13 | Output resistance (sat.) | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Ohms |
| 14 | Subthreshold slope | $S = n \, V_T \ln 10$ | mV/decade (typically 70–100) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding these formulas is non-negotiable for:

- **Digital VLSI design:** sizing transistors (W/L) to meet propagation-delay targets
- **Analog IC design:** designing transconductance stages, current mirrors, and op-amp input pairs
- **Memory design (DRAM/Flash):** the MOS capacitor is literally the storage element
- **Process engineering:** $V_{TH0}$, $\gamma$, and $t_{ox}$ are monitored across every wafer to track fabrication drift
- **Low-power design:** the subthreshold slope $S$ determines leakage currents in sleep mode

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Threshold Voltage (V_TH)

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU Module 1 board exams. We derive it from the surface potential condition that defines strong inversion.

**Starting Principle:** Strong inversion begins when the surface potential $\phi_S$ shifts from its equilibrium value by $-2\phi_F$ (i.e., the band bending equals twice the Fermi potential for an NMOS on p-type).

### Step 1 — The total induced charge balance at the Si surface

For a gate voltage $V_{GS}$ applied across the MOS structure, Gauss's law at the oxide–silicon interface gives:

$$
Q_G + Q_{ss} + Q_S = 0
$$

where $Q_G$ is the gate charge, $Q_{ss}$ is the fixed oxide-interface trap charge, and $Q_S$ is the total silicon surface charge (which includes both the depletion-layer ionized acceptors $Q_B$ and the inversion-layer mobile electrons $Q_I$).

**Conversion logic:** $Q_S = Q_B + Q_I$. The negative sign convention treats negative charges (electrons, acceptors) as having negative values.

### Step 2 — The flat-band voltage component

The voltage drop across the gate stack is:

$$
V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_S + \dfrac{Q_S}{C_{ox}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $V_{FB}$ accounts for the work-function difference and the interface-trap charge; $\phi_S$ is the surface band-bending; the last term is the voltage needed to sustain the charge $Q_S$ on the oxide capacitor.

### Step 3 — At the threshold point, define the boundary conditions

At threshold, we have *just* reached strong inversion. By convention:

- $\phi_S = 2\vert\phi_F\vert$ (for NMOS on p-substrate; total surface band-bending of $-2\phi_F$)
- $Q_I \approx 0$ (the inversion charge is *just* starting to appear; we are at the boundary)

So $Q_S \approx Q_B$, and the depletion charge at threshold is:

$$
Q_{BT} = -\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \varepsilon_0 \, N_A \cdot 2\vert\phi_F\vert}
$$

**Derivation of $Q_{BT}$:** Solving Poisson's equation in the depletion approximation for a one-sided step junction gives the standard depletion charge:

$$
Q_{BT} = -\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \varepsilon_0 \, N_A \, \phi_S}
$$

Substituting $\phi_S = 2\vert\phi_F\vert$ yields the boxed form above. $\varepsilon_{si} = \varepsilon_r^{si} \cdot \varepsilon_0$.

### Step 4 — Substitute into the voltage equation

$$
V_{GS} = V_{FB} + 2\vert\phi_F\vert - \dfrac{Q_{BT}}{C_{ox}}
$$

(remember $Q_{BT}$ is negative, so $-Q_{BT}/C_{ox}$ is a positive voltage drop.)

### Step 5 — Replace $Q_{BT}$ with its explicit form

$$
V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\vert\phi_F\vert + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \varepsilon_0 \, N_A \cdot 2\vert\phi_F\vert}}{C_{ox}}
$$

**This is the zero-bias threshold voltage $V_{TH0}$** when the source-body voltage $V_{SB} = 0$.

### Step 6 — Introduce the body effect for $V_{SB} > 0$

When a reverse body bias $V_{SB} > 0$ is applied, the surface potential required to reach inversion becomes $2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB}$. The depletion charge now is:

$$
Q_B = -\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \varepsilon_0 \, N_A \, (2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB})}
$$

Substituting into the same voltage equation and subtracting the $V_{SB}=0$ result:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\vert\phi_F\vert} \right)
$$

where the **body-effect coefficient** is:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \varepsilon_0 \, N_A}}{C_{ox}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of the body effect:** Increasing $V_{SB}$ widens the depletion region. More negative acceptor ions must be "compensated" by the gate, requiring a *higher* V_GS to achieve inversion. Hence $V_{TH}$ rises with $V_{SB}$. This is the key to understanding the **source-follower body effect** in analog circuits.

## 3.2 Derivation of the I_D – V_DS Relationship (Long-Channel, Square Law)

Starting from the **gradual channel approximation** (GCA), the mobile inversion charge per unit area at a point $y$ along the channel is:

$$
Q_I(y) = -C_{ox} \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \big]
$$

where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential measured from the source.

The drain current (constant along the channel) is given by:

$$
I_D = -\mu_n \, W \, Q_I(y) \, \dfrac{dV(y)}{dy}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Drift velocity $v = -\mu_n E_y = -\mu_n \dfrac{dV}{dy}$. Multiply by the cross-sectional charge $-Q_I \cdot W$ to get the current. The negative signs cancel because $Q_I$ itself is negative.

### Step 3a — Integrate from source (y=0, V=0) to drain (y=L, V=V_DS)

$$
\int_0^L I_D \, dy = \mu_n W C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}} \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V \big] \, dV
$$

Since $I_D$ is independent of $y$:

$$
I_D \cdot L = \mu_n W C_{ox} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]
$$

### Step 3b — Solve for I_D in the triode region

$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right], \quad V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}
$$

### Step 3c — Saturation: pinch-off at V_DS = V_GS - V_TH

Substituting $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$:

$$
I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 - \dfrac{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}{2} \right]
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2
$$

**This is the famous "square-law" of long-channel MOSFETs.**

## 3.3 Channel Length Modulation — The $\lambda$ Correction

In real short-channel devices, after pinch-off, the depletion region at the drain extends into the channel, *effectively shortening* the channel length from $L$ to $L - \Delta L$. Empirically:

$$
I_D = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})
$$

where $\lambda$ is the **channel-length modulation parameter**, inversely proportional to the effective channel length:

$$
\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{L}
$$

**Engineering insight:** $\lambda$ explains why a saturated MOSFET has a *finite output resistance* $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ rather than infinite — critical for analog gain calculations.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 65-nm NMOS has $t_{ox} = 1.5$ nm, $N_A = 5 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $\mu_n = 450$ cm²/V·s, $V_{SB} = 0.5$ V. Compute $V_{TH}$ and $I_{D,sat}$ at $V_{GS} = 1.0$ V, $W/L = 10$.

**Step 1 — Oxide capacitance:**

$$
C_{ox} = \dfrac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.302 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{F/cm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Fermi potential:**

$$
\phi_F = 0.02585 \ln\!\left( \dfrac{5 \times 10^{17}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}} \right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(3.33 \times 10^7) = 0.432 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Body-effect coefficient:**

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \times 5 \times 10^{17}}}{2.302 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{1.658 \times 10^{-13}}}{2.302 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{4.07 \times 10^{-7}}{2.302 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.177 \ \text{V}^{1/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Threshold with body bias (assuming $V_{TH0} = 0.45$ V from process):**

$$
V_{TH} = 0.45 + 0.177 \left( \sqrt{0.864 + 0.5} - \sqrt{0.864} \right) = 0.45 + 0.177(1.168 - 0.930)
$$

$$
V_{TH} = 0.45 + 0.177 \times 0.238 = 0.45 + 0.042 = 0.492 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Saturation current (ignoring $\lambda$):**

$$
I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{450 \times 2.302 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \times 10 \times (1.0 - 0.492)^2
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = 5.18 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.258 = 1.34 \ \text{mA}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Cross-Section of an NMOS Transistor

The following Mermaid block renders a **functional block architecture** of the NMOS cross-section, showing the four terminals and the induced channel.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gateA["GATE TERMINAL VG"]:::gate
    oxideA["GATE OXIDE SiO2 tox"]:::ox
    chanA["INVERSION CHANNEL n-type electrons"]:::chan
    drainA["DRAIN n plus VDS"]:::d
    sourceA["SOURCE n plus"]:::s
    bulkA["P-SUBSTRATE BODY VB"]:::b
    deplA["DEPLETION REGION ionized acceptors"]:::dep

    gateA --> oxideA
    oxideA --> chanA
    chanA --> drainA
    chanA --> sourceA
    bulkA --- deplA
    bulkA --- sourceA
    bulkA --- drainA

    classDef gate fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef ox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef chan fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef d fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef s fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef b fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef dep fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Energy-Band Diagram Under Strong Inversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ecL["Ec LEFT bulk p-Si"]:::ec
    efL["EF equilibrium"]:::ef
    evL["Ev LEFT"]:::ev
    oxideL["SiO2 barrier"]:::ox
    gateL["GATE metal poly"]:::g
    bend["BAND BENDING downward near interface"]:::bend
    ecR["Ec at surface pushed below EF"]:::ec
    ecS["Ec surface below EF by q phi S"]:::ec

    ecL --- efL
    efL --- evL
    gateL --- oxideL
    oxideL --- bend
    bend --- ecR
    ecR --- ecS

    classDef ec fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ef fill:#000000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ev fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef g fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bend fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the bands:** Under strong inversion, the conduction band edge $E_C$ at the Si–SiO₂ interface is pulled *below* the Fermi level $E_F$. This is what makes electrons the *majority* carrier at the surface (despite p-type bulk) — hence the term "inversion."

## 4.3 Surface-Condition State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FlatBand : VGS = VFB
    FlatBand --> Accumulation : VGS less than VFB holes pile up
    FlatBand --> Depletion : VGS slightly positive holes repelled
    Depletion --> WeakInversion : VGS near VTH minority electrons appear
    WeakInversion --> StrongInversion : VGS exceeds VTH full channel forms
    StrongInversion --> [*] : transistor ON

    note right of StrongInversion : IDS flows for VDS greater than 0
```

## 4.4 I_D vs V_DS Output Characteristics Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis["VDS axis horizontal"]:::ax
    cur1["Curve 1 VGS equals 0.6 V low current"]:::c1
    cur2["Curve 2 VGS equals 0.8 V medium current"]:::c2
    cur3["Curve 3 VGS equals 1.0 V high current"]:::c3
    cur4["Curve 4 VGS equals 1.2 V highest current"]:::c4
    tri["TRIODE REGION low VDS"]:::tr
    sat["SATURATION REGION flat curves"]:::st
    pinch["PINCH OFF at VDS equals VGS minus VTH"]:::pc

    axis --> tri
    tri --> pinch
    pinch --> sat
    cur1 --> pinch
    cur2 --> pinch
    cur3 --> pinch
    cur4 --> pinch

    classDef ax fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef c1 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef c2 fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef c3 fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    classDef c4 fill:#C71585,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    classDef tr fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef st fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef pc fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**Define the threshold voltage of a MOSFET. What is the body effect?**

**Model Answer:**

The **threshold voltage (V_TH)** of an NMOS transistor is the minimum gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ required to create a conducting inversion layer (channel) of electrons between the source and drain, such that the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration.

**The body effect** is the phenomenon whereby the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ increases when a reverse body-to-source bias $V_{SB} > 0$ is applied. It is quantified by:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\vert\phi_F\vert} \right)
$$

A larger $V_{SB}$ widens the depletion region, requiring a higher $V_{GS}$ to attract the same inversion charge. The body effect is important in analog circuits (e.g., source followers) and in modern CMOS where the "body" is tied to the most negative supply to prevent forward biasing the source–body junction. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]  
**Distinguish between the triode and saturation regions of operation of an NMOS transistor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Triode Region | Saturation Region |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $0 \le V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ |
| Channel | Continuous, not pinched off | Pinched off near the drain |
| $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ | Increases (quadratically) | Almost constant (flat, with slight $\lambda V_{DS}$ rise) |
| Governing equation | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}]$ | $I_D = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ |
| Function in circuits | Variable resistor / switch in ON state | Current source in analog, ON switch in digital logic |

The boundary is at $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ (the **overdrive voltage**). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> KTU Module-end rule: **internal choice between two 14-mark questions** with two sub-parts (typically 7+7).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of necessary energy band diagrams, explain the three operating regions — **accumulation, depletion, and inversion** — of the MOS capacitor on a p-type substrate. (7 marks)

**(b)** Starting from Poisson's equation, derive the expression for the **threshold voltage** of an NMOS transistor, clearly stating all assumptions. Include the **body-effect** term. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Accumulation ($V_{GS} \ll 0$):**  
A large negative gate voltage repels electrons (minority carriers, none in p-type anyway) and *attracts holes* to the Si–SiO₂ interface. The band diagram shows $E_V$ bending *upward* at the surface (i.e., closer to $E_F$), so the surface becomes more strongly p-type. No depletion layer exists. The MOS capacitor behaves like a simple oxide capacitor in series with the bulk. (2 marks)

**Depletion (small positive $V_{GS}$):**  
A modest positive gate voltage repels holes from the interface, leaving behind a region of *uncovered negatively-charged ionized acceptors*. The bands bend *downward* at the surface by an amount $\phi_S$. The charge per unit area is $Q_B = -\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} \varepsilon_0 N_A \phi_S}$. The surface is depleted of mobile carriers. (2 marks)

**Inversion ($V_{GS} > V_{TH}$):**  
When the band bending exceeds $2\vert\phi_F\vert$, the surface potential $\phi_S = 2\vert\phi_F\vert$ becomes so favorable that electrons (minorities) are attracted and form a thin conducting layer. $E_C$ at the surface falls *below* $E_F$. This electron-rich layer is the **channel**. The transistor turns ON. (3 marks)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Assumptions:**  
(i) Gradual channel approximation, (ii) one-dimensional analysis, (iii) depletion approximation (mobile carriers neglected inside depletion region), (iv) uniformly doped substrate.

**Step 1 — Gauss's Law at the surface:** $Q_G + Q_{SS} + Q_S = 0$ with $Q_S = Q_B + Q_I$.

**Step 2 — Voltage equation:** $V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_S + \dfrac{Q_S}{C_{ox}}$ (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Strong-inversion condition:** $\phi_S = 2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB}$, and at threshold, $Q_I = 0$, so $Q_S = Q_B$. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Substituting the depletion charge:**

$$
Q_B = -\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} \varepsilon_0 N_A (2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB})}
$$

**Step 5 — Final V_TH expression:**

$$
\boxed{V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\vert\phi_F\vert + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} \varepsilon_0 N_A (2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Body effect isolated** by writing $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\vert\phi_F\vert} \right)$ where $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} \varepsilon_0 N_A}}{C_{ox}}$. (2 marks for the body-effect term and physical meaning.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Sign of $\phi_F$:** Many students write $\phi_F$ as a positive number for p-type. It is **negative** by definition ($\phi_F = (kT/q) \ln(N_A/n_i)$ is positive when the bulk is n-type; for p-type, $N_A > n_i$ still makes the argument positive, but the *Fermi level lies below intrinsic*, so $\phi_F$ in the *bulk potential* sense used in band diagrams is negative). Use $\vert\phi_F\vert$ in V_TH to avoid confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the depletion-charge sign** — must remember $Q_B$ is negative, so $-Q_B/C_{ox}$ is positive and adds to $V_{TH}$.
> 3. **Omitting the body effect** even when the question asks for it. This alone can cost 2–3 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the gradual channel approximation, derive the **I_D vs V_DS** characteristic of an NMOS in (i) the triode region and (ii) the saturation region. Define **channel-length modulation** and explain its effect on output resistance. (7 marks)

**(b)** An NMOS transistor has the following parameters: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 250 \ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 20$, $V_{TH} = 0.4$ V, $\lambda = 0.05$ V⁻¹.  
  (i) Calculate the **saturation drain current** at $V_{GS} = 1.0$ V and $V_{DS} = 1.5$ V.  
  (ii) Calculate the **output resistance** $r_o$.  
  (iii) If $V_{GS}$ is raised to 1.5 V, what is the new $I_{D,sat}$? Comment on the **square-law** dependence. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Triode derivation** (as shown in Section 3.2):

$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right], \quad V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}
$$

**Saturation derivation** by setting $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$:

$$
I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2
$$

**Channel-length modulation:** Beyond pinch-off, the depletion region at the drain extends further into the channel as $V_{DS}$ increases, *shortening* the effective channel length from $L$ to $L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$. The current increases linearly with $V_{DS}$:

$$
I_D = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})
$$

The **output resistance** is therefore finite:

$$
r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}
$$

A larger $L$ ⇒ smaller $\lambda$ ⇒ larger $r_o$ (desirable for analog gain). (3 marks for the modulation discussion.)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Saturation current:**  
$1 + \lambda V_{DS} = 1 + 0.05 \times 1.5 = 1.075$

$$
I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \times 20 \times (1.0 - 0.4)^2 \times 1.075
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 0.36 \times 1.075
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.387 = 19.35 \ \text{mA} \quad (3 \ \text{marks})
$$

**(ii) Output resistance:**

$$
r_o = \dfrac{1}{0.05 \times 19.35 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1}{9.675 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1034 \ \Omega
$$

(2 marks)

**(iii) At V_GS = 1.5 V (still in saturation since $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.1$ V and $V_{DS} = 1.5$ V):**

$$
I_{D,sat} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times (1.1)^2 \times 1.075
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.21 \times 1.075 = 65.0 \ \text{mA}
$$

(2 marks)

**Comment on square-law:**  
Doubling $V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ from 0.6 V to 1.2 V would have quadrupled the current. Here the overdrive changed from 0.6 V to 1.1 V, a factor of ~1.83, so the current should scale by ~3.37× — and indeed $19.35 \to 65.0$ mA is a factor of ~3.36. The quadratic dependence on overdrive is the heart of the **square-law model**. (Bonus: 0.5 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — where students lose marks on this type of question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ factor** when $V_{DS} > V_{DS,sat}$. Many students use the pure square-law even when the question explicitly gives $\lambda$ and $V_{DS}$. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $\mu_n C_{ox}$ in µA/V² multiplied by $(V)^2$ gives µA — don't write Amps without scaling.
> 3. **Wrong $V_{DS,sat}$ check:** If $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$, the triode equation must be used. Always verify which region the device is in.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — pin this in your exam notes!**

- **Three MOS surface regimes:** accumulation (holes pile up), depletion (acceptor ions exposed), inversion (electrons form the channel). Strong inversion begins at $V_{GS} = V_{TH}$.
- **Threshold voltage components:** $V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\vert\phi_F\vert + \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} \varepsilon_0 N_A (2\vert\phi_F\vert + V_{SB})}/C_{ox}$.
- **Flat-band voltage:** $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{SS}/C_{ox}$ — accounts for metal–semiconductor work-function difference and interface-trap charge.
- **Body effect:** Reverse biasing source–body junction **increases** $V_{TH}$ via the $\gamma$ coefficient. The body cannot be left floating in a MOSFET!
- **Square-law I-V:** In saturation, $I_D = (\mu_n C_{ox}/2)(W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$.
- **Triode vs saturation boundary:** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ (also called the **overdrive voltage**).
- **Channel-length modulation:** The $\lambda$ factor; gives finite $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$. Crucial for analog gain.
- **$g_m$ in saturation:** $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$.
- **Subthreshold conduction:** Below $V_{TH}$, $I_D$ falls exponentially with slope $S = n V_T \ln 10$ — important for low-power/leakage analysis.
- **Process knobs that change $V_{TH}$:** $t_{ox}$ (via $C_{ox}$), $N_A$ (via $Q_B$ and $\phi_F$), and $Q_{SS}$ (via $V_{FB}$).
- **NMOS vs PMOS sign conventions:** For PMOS on n-substrate, all signs flip; expect $V_{TH} < 0$ and $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ for turn-on.
- **Constants to memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $kT/q = 25.85$ mV @ 300 K.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** Always draw the energy band diagram when a question asks about MOS surface conditions — KTU examiners award **3 marks** purely for a clear, labeled band diagram. Label $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_F$, $E_i$, the oxide barrier, and the surface band bending direction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of MOS Characteristics and Second Order Effects, Single Stage Amplifiers, Common Source Stage with Different Load Types, Source Follower, Common Gate, and Cascode Stage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of MOS Characteristics and Second Order Effects, Single Stage Amplifiers, Common Source Stage with Different Load Types, Source Follower, Common Gate, and Cascode Stage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 MOS Transistor — The Fundamental Building Block

A **MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)** is a four-terminal voltage-controlled device whose drain current $I_D$ is modulated by the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$. In CMOS analog design, the **long-channel square-law model** serves as the first-order analytical foundation, while **second-order effects** refine accuracy for short-channel nanoscale devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor:** PEAET631 Module 1 mandates a rigorous re-examination of MOS device physics as a prerequisite for analyzing single-stage amplifier topologies. Mastery of small-signal parameters ($g_m$, $r_o$, $g_{mb}$) is **non-negotiable** for board examination success.

### 1.2 The Three Terminals That Matter in Analog Design

| Terminal | Symbol | Role in Small-Signal |
|----------|--------|----------------------|
| Gate | $G$ | High-impedance input node ($R_{in} \to \infty$) |
| Drain | $D$ | Output current collector |
| Source | $S$ | Common reference (signal + bias) |
| Body (Bulk) | $B$ | Generates $g_{mb}$ via back-gate effect |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Faucet" Model

Imagine a **garden hose with a pinch valve**:
- The **gate voltage** ($V_{GS}$) is your hand squeezing the hose — the harder you squeeze, the more water flows.
- The **drain-to-source voltage** ($V_{DS}$) is the water pressure pushing through.
- The **threshold voltage** ($V_{TH}$) is the minimum squeeze needed before any water flows at all.
- The **channel length modulation** is like the hose stretching slightly under high pressure, allowing a bit more water to leak through.
- The **body effect** is like someone stepping on the hose from behind — suddenly you need to squeeze harder to maintain the same flow.

### 1.4 Single-Stage Amplifier — Definition

A **single-stage amplifier** is a configuration where a single transistor (or transistor pair) provides the entire voltage gain from input to output. KTU Module 1 mandates analysis of four canonical topologies:

1. **Common Source (CS)** — Inverting, high gain
2. **Source Follower (SF) / Common Drain (CD)** — Unity-gain buffer
3. **Common Gate (CG)** — Non-inverting, low input impedance
4. **Cascode (CS + CG cascade)** — Ultra-high gain with enhanced bandwidth

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Every operational amplifier, comparator, and ADC front-end ever fabricated uses these four topologies as building blocks. The cascode, in particular, is the cornerstone of telescopic OTAs in modern Sigma-Delta converters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MOS I–V Characteristics — Long-Channel Square-Law Model

When $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$: the device operates in the **triode (linear) region**.

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

When $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$: the channel pinches off and the device enters **saturation (active) region**.

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Foundation:** These equations emerge from the gradual-channel approximation (GCA), assuming drift current dominates, vertical field is much larger than lateral field, and doping is uniform. The student must memorize the saturation condition $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_{OV}$ (the **overdrive voltage**, sometimes called $V_{eff}$).

### 2.2 Second-Order Effects — Why Reality Diverges from the Square Law

#### (a) Channel Length Modulation (CLM)
In saturation, the pinch-off point migrates toward the source, effectively shortening the channel from $L$ to $L - \Delta L$. This produces a finite output resistance.

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

The **channel-length modulation parameter** $\lambda$ is inversely proportional to channel length: $\lambda \propto 1/L$. Shorter channels → larger $\lambda$ → lower output resistance.

#### (b) Body Effect (Back-Gate Effect)
When the source is not tied to the bulk, $V_{SB} > 0$ increases the threshold voltage:

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

where:
- $V_{TH0}$ = zero-bias threshold voltage
- $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ = **body-effect coefficient**
- $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln \frac{N_A}{n_i}$ = **Fermi potential** ($\approx \mathbf{0.3\,V}$ for typical doping)

This creates a **non-zero transconductance from bulk to drain** quantified by $g_{mb}$:

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$$

$$g_{mb} = g_m \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}} = \eta \cdot g_m$$

where $\eta = g_{mb}/g_m \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ for typical CMOS processes.

#### (c) Subthreshold Conduction
Below threshold, $I_D$ does not abruptly go to zero — it decays exponentially:

$$I_D = I_{D0} \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

where $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{26\,mV}$ at room temperature and $n$ is the subthreshold slope factor ($1 < n < 3$).

#### (d) Mobility Degradation (Vertical Field Effect)
High gate fields pull carriers against the oxide interface, reducing mobility:

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu_0}{1 + \theta (V_{GS} - V_{TH})}$$

where $\theta$ is the mobility degradation coefficient ($\approx 0.1$ to $0.5\,V^{-1}$).

#### (e) Velocity Saturation
In short-channel devices, the carrier velocity $v_d$ saturates at $v_{sat} \approx 10^7\,cm/s$, causing $I_D$ to become **linear rather than quadratic** in $V_{OV}$:

$$I_D \approx W C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) v_{sat}$$

### 2.3 Small-Signal Model — The Engineer's Toolkit

The complete small-signal model includes three dependent sources:

$$\boxed{i_d = g_m v_{gs} + g_{mb} v_{bs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o}}$$

The **intrinsic gain** of a single transistor is the fundamental figure of merit:

$$A_{int} = g_m r_o = \frac{2(V_{GS} - V_{TH})}{(\lambda)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2} \cdot \text{(unitless)} \implies g_m r_o = \frac{2}{\lambda (V_{GS} - V_{TH})}$$

In modern nanometer CMOS, $g_m r_o$ ranges from **20 to 200**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula | Units |
|--------|---------|---------|-------|
| $g_m$ | Transconductance | $\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}$ | S (A/V) |
| $g_m$ (alt) | Transconductance | $\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ | S |
| $g_m$ (alt 2) | Transconductance | $2 I_D / V_{OV}$ | S |
| $r_o$ | Output resistance | $1/(\lambda I_D) \approx 1/(\lambda I_D)$ | $\Omega$ |
| $g_{mb}$ | Body transconductance | $\eta \cdot g_m$ | S |
| $C_{gs}$ | Gate-source cap. | $\frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox} + W C_{ov}$ | F |
| $C_{gd}$ | Gate-drain cap. | $W C_{ov}$ | F |
| $f_T$ | Unity-gain freq. | $g_m / (2\pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd}))$ | Hz |
| $A_v$ | Voltage gain | $-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | — |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and parallel operators are written as `\vert`, `\mid`, or `$\parallel$` to prevent markdown-table corruption. Students are advised to do the same in their KTU answer sheets when constructing tabulated answers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The square-law and small-signal models are the **backbone of analog design automation** in Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys HSPICE, and Keysight ADS. Every SPICE engine (BSIM3, BSIM4, BSIM-CMG) uses the square law as the asymptotic limit. Mastering these equations allows direct hand-calculation of bias points, gain, and bandwidth — which is precisely the skill tested in KTU's two-tiered question paper.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $g_m$ in Saturation — From Square Law to Transconductance

**Starting point:** The drain current equation in saturation.

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

**Step 1 — Define transconductance as the partial derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$ at constant $V_{DS}$:**

$$g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS}=\text{const}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule, noting that $V_{TH}$ is independent of $V_{GS}$ when source is tied to bulk:**

$$g_m = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial V_{GS}}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the derivative term:**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial V_{GS}}(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = 1 - 0 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Final expression for $g_m$:**

$$\boxed{g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}}$$

**Step 5 — Alternative form using $I_D$:** Substituting $V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 I_D / (\mu_n C_{ox} W/L)}$:

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

> All three forms are **equally valid** and frequently interchanged in KTU problems. Knowing which form to use based on given data is the mark of a topper.

### 3.2 Derivation of $r_o$ via Channel Length Modulation

**Starting point:** Modified saturation equation with CLM.

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

**Step 1 — Define output resistance as the inverse of the output conductance:**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{g_{ds}} = \left[ \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} \right|_{V_{GS}=\text{const}}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Take the partial derivative** (only the $1 + \lambda V_{DS}$ term depends on $V_{DS}$):

$$g_{ds} = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \cdot \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the prefactor as $I_D$ when $V_{DS} \approx 0$** (i.e., divide and multiply by the same quantity):

$$g_{ds} = \lambda \cdot \underbrace{\left[ \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \right]}_{= I_{D,\text{no CLM}}}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain $r_o$:**

$$\boxed{r_o \approx \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}}$$

> **Key insight:** Doubling the bias current $I_D$ halves $r_o$, which directly halves the voltage gain $g_m r_o$ — illustrating the classic **gain-bandwidth-power trade-off** in CMOS analog design.

### 3.3 Common Source Amplifier with Resistive Load

**Topology:** NMOS source at ground, gate as input, drain tied to $V_{DD}$ through resistor $R_D$.

**Step 1 — DC Analysis (to find operating point):**

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

$$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$$

**Step 2 — Small-signal model:** Replace transistor with $g_m v_{gs}$ current source and $r_o$ between drain and source.

**Step 3 — Output node KCL at the drain:**

The total current flowing through $R_D \parallel r_o$ from the output node to ground equals the dependent current source $g_m v_{gs}$ flowing into the same node.

$$g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} = -\frac{v_{ds}}{R_D}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_{ds}/v_{gs}$:**

$$v_{ds} \left( \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_D} \right) = -g_m v_{gs}$$

$$v_{ds} = -g_m v_{gs} \left( r_o \parallel R_D \right)$$

**Step 5 — Voltage gain:**

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (r_o \parallel R_D)}$$

The negative sign indicates **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a defining property of the CS stage.

### 3.4 Common Source with Diode-Connected Load

**Configuration:** The "load" transistor $M_2$ has its gate tied to its drain. Thus $V_{GS2} = V_{DS2}$, forcing it into saturation.

**Step 1 — Small-signal resistance of diode-connected MOSFET:**

Applying $v_{gs} = v_{ds}$ and KCL at the drain:

$$i_d = g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} = g_m v_{ds} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} = v_{ds}\left(g_m + \frac{1}{r_o}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R_{out,diode} = \frac{v_{ds}}{i_d} = \frac{1}{g_m + g_{ds}} = \frac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o \approx \frac{1}{g_m}}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage gain:**

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_{m1} \left( \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \parallel r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} \right) \approx -\sqrt{\frac{(W/L)_1}{(W/L)_2}}}$$

The last approximation (ignoring $r_o$ for now) shows that the **gain is set by the sizing ratio** of driver to load.

### 3.5 Common Source with Current-Source Load

**Topology:** Replace $R_D$ with a PMOS current source providing bias $I_{SS}$.

**Step 1 — DC bias:** Both $M_1$ and load operate at the same $I_D = I_{SS}$.

**Step 2 — Small-signal output resistance:** $R_{out} = r_{o1} \parallel r_{o,load}$

**Step 3 — Voltage gain:**

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_{m1} (r_{o1} \parallel r_{o,load})}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum output swing:** $V_{out} \in [V_{DS,sat1}, V_{DD} - V_{DS,sat,load}]$

This is the **most commonly used CS stage** in modern ICs because current-source loads eliminate the large DC drop of a resistor and allow rail-to-rail operation when cascoded.

### 3.6 Source Follower (Common Drain) — Derivation

**Topology:** Drain tied to $V_{DD}$, gate is input, source is output.

**Step 1 — Small-signal model:** Apply KCL at the source node with $R_S$ as the source degeneration resistor (or $I_1$ as a current-source bias).

$$g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} = \frac{v_s}{R_S}$$

where $v_{gs} = v_g - v_s$ and $v_{ds} = -v_s$ (since drain is AC ground).

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$g_m (v_g - v_s) - \frac{v_s}{r_o} = \frac{v_s}{R_S}$$

**Step 3 — Collect $v_s$ terms:**

$$g_m v_g = v_s \left( g_m + \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_S} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A_v$:**

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_s}{v_g} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S + R_S/r_o} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{ds})R_S}}$$

**Step 5 — Limit as $g_m R_S \to \infty$:** $A_v \to 1$ — hence the name "**follower**."

**Step 6 — Output resistance** (set $v_g = 0$, apply $v_{test}$ at source):

$$\boxed{R_{out} = \frac{1}{g_m + g_{ds}} = \frac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o \approx \frac{1}{g_m}}$$

### 3.7 Common Gate Amplifier — Derivation

**Topology:** Gate tied to AC ground (typically a bias voltage), input at source, output at drain.

**Step 1 — KCL at the drain:** $g_m v_{gs} + v_{ds}/r_o = -v_{ds}/R_D$

With $v_{gs} = -v_s$ (since $v_g = 0$):

$$-g_m v_s = -v_{ds}\left(\frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_D}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Solve:**

$$A_v = \frac{v_d}{v_s} = \frac{g_m (r_o \parallel R_D)}{1} = g_m (r_o \parallel R_D)$$

$$\boxed{A_v = g_m (r_o \parallel R_D)}$$

Note: the gain is **positive (non-inverting)** and **identical in magnitude** to the CS stage, but with two critical differences:
- **Input resistance is low:** $R_{in} = 1/g_m$
- **No Miller effect** at the gate — bandwidth is far higher than CS.

### 3.8 Cascode Stage — The Telescopic Topology

**Topology:** A CS stage $M_1$ stacked on top of a CG stage $M_2$. The output is taken from the drain of $M_2$.

**Step 1 — Voltage gain of cascode:**

The drain of $M_1$ sees an effective load of $r_{o1}$ in parallel with the input resistance of $M_2$, which is $r_{o2} + (R_D \parallel r_{o,load})$. Wait — this needs careful analysis. The standard textbook result:

$$\boxed{A_v \approx -g_{m1} \left[ (g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}) \parallel r_{o,load} \right] \approx -g_{m1} r_{o1} g_{m2} r_{o2}}$$

when the load is an ideal current source.

**Step 2 — Output resistance of cascode** (looking into the drain of $M_2$ with the output port open):

$$R_{out,cascode} \approx r_{o2} + r_{o1} + (g_{m2} r_{o2}) r_{o1} \approx g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}$$

This represents an **enhancement by a factor of $g_m r_o$** compared to a simple CS stage.

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:** $M_2$ acts as a "buffer" that prevents voltage changes at the drain of $M_1$, effectively multiplying the impedance seen at the drain of $M_1$ by $(1 + g_{m2} r_{o2})$.

### 3.9 Comparison Matrix — The Four Topologies Side by Side

| Parameter | Common Source | Source Follower | Common Gate | Cascode |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|-------------|---------|
| $A_v$ | $-g_m(R_D \parallel r_o)$ | $\frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | $+g_m(R_D \parallel r_o)$ | $-g_{m1} g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$ |
| $R_{in}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $1/g_m$ | $\infty$ |
| $R_{out}$ | $R_D \parallel r_o$ | $1/g_m \parallel r_o$ | $R_D \parallel r_o$ | $g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}$ |
| Phase | 180° | 0° | 0° | 180° |
| Bandwidth | Limited (Miller) | Wide | Very wide | Medium |
| Use | Voltage gain | Buffer | High-freq. input | Telescopic OTA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NMOS Small-Signal Model — Visual Mapping

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **structure of the small-signal equivalent circuit** for a MOSFET, with all four terminals and the three dependent sources that KTU frequently asks students to draw.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeG[Gate G] --> Cgs["Cgs"]
    nodeG --> Cgd["Cgd"]
    Cgs --> nodeS[Source S]
    Cgd --> nodeD[Drain D]
    nodeG --> Rg[Infinite Rg]
    nodeG -.-> GmVgs["gm vgs current source"]
    nodeS -.-> Gmbs["gmb vbs current source"]
    nodeD --> Ro["ro between Drain and Source"]
    nodeS --> Ro
    nodeB[Bulk B] -.-> Csb["Csb capacitance"]
    Csb --> nodeS
    nodeB -.-> Gmbs
    GmVgs --> nodeD
    Gmbs --> nodeD
    style nodeG fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#E94E77,color:#fff
    style nodeS fill:#50C878,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Common Source Amplifier — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Input["INPUT PORT"]
        Vin[Vin AC Signal] --> InNode[Input Node Gate]
    end
    InNode --> M1[NMOS Driver M1 with gm and ro]
    Bias1[VGS Bias DC] --> InNode
    subgraph Output["OUTPUT PORT"]
        OutNode[Drain Node] --> Vout[Vout AC Signal]
        OutNode --> RD[Resistor RD to VDD]
    end
    M1 --> OutNode
    M1 --> GroundNode[Source to Ground]
    M1 -.->|dependent source gm vgs| OutNode
    M1 -.->|resistance ro| OutNode
    style InNode fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style OutNode fill:#E94E77,color:#fff
    style GroundNode fill:#888,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Cascode Stage — Telescopic Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    Vin[Vin at Gate of M1] --> M1[M1 Common Source NMOS]
    M1 --> MidNode[Drain of M1 equals Source of M2]
    MidNode --> M2[M2 Common Gate NMOS with bias VG2]
    BiasGate[DC Bias VG2] --> GateM2[Gate of M2 AC Ground]
    GateM2 --> M2
    M2 --> OutNode[Output at Drain of M2]
    OutNode --> Vout[Vout]
    OutNode --> LoadPMOS[PMOS Current Source Load]
    LoadPMOS --> VDDRail[VDD]
    M1 --> Ground[Ground at Source of M1]
    style Vin fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style OutNode fill:#E94E77,color:#fff
    style Ground fill:#888,color:#fff
    style VDDRail fill:#50C878,color:#fff
    style GateM2 fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Source Follower — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin[Vin at Gate] --> M1[M1 NMOS with gm vgs]
    M1 --> Vout[Vout at Source]
    M1 --> DrainRail[Drain tied to VDD]
    Vout --> RS[RS to Ground or Current Source]
    Vout --> LoadNext[Drives Next Stage High Z Load]
    style Vin fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style Vout fill:#E94E77,color:#fff
    style DrainRail fill:#50C878,color:#fff
    style LoadNext fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
```

### 4.5 Common Gate — Functional Block

```mermaid
graph TB
    Vin[Vin at Source of M1] --> M1[M1 NMOS with gate at AC Ground]
    GateAC[DC Bias VG with AC Bypass Cap] --> GateM1[Gate of M1 AC GND]
    GateM1 --> M1
    M1 --> Vout[Vout at Drain]
    Vout --> RD[RD to VDD]
    M1 --> SourceNode[Source Node Low Impedance]
    Vin --> SourceNode
    style Vin fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style Vout fill:#E94E77,color:#fff

```

### 4.6 Analytical Decision Tree — Which Topology to Use?

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Design Goal] --> Q1{High Input Impedance Required}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Phase Inversion Tolerable}
    Q1 -->|No| CG[Use Common Gate]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{High Gain Required}
    Q2 -->|No| SF[Use Source Follower Buffer]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Bandwidth Also Critical}
    Q3 -->|No| CS[Use Common Source with RD]
    Q4 -->|Yes| CAS[Use Cascode Telescopic]
    Q4 -->|No| CS
    style Start fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style CAS fill:#E94E77,color:#fff

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the threshold voltage of a MOSFET. What is the body effect, and how does it modify the threshold voltage equation?

**Model Answer:**
The threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting channel (inversion layer) between the source and drain terminals of a MOSFET. When $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$, the device is in cutoff and $I_D \approx 0$.

The **body effect** (or back-gate effect) occurs when the source-to-bulk voltage $V_{SB} > 0$, causing the depletion region under the channel to widen. This requires a higher $V_{GS}$ to achieve the same inversion. The modified equation is:

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left[ \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right]$$

where $\gamma$ is the body-effect coefficient and $\phi_F$ is the Fermi potential. The body effect introduces a non-zero bulk transconductance $g_{mb}$ that must be included in small-signal analysis when source is not tied to bulk. **(3 marks — full allocation: 1 mark definition, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark equation)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** List any four second-order effects in short-channel MOSFETs. State how each one modifies the ideal square-law I–V relationship.

**Model Answer:**
The four second-order effects are:

1. **Channel Length Modulation (CLM):** Multiplies $I_D$ by $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$, introducing finite output resistance $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$.

2. **Body Effect:** Increases $V_{TH}$ as $V_{SB}$ increases via $\gamma$ and $\phi_F$ parameters, thereby reducing $I_D$ for a fixed $V_{GS}$.

3. **Mobility Degradation:** Replaces constant $\mu_n$ with $\mu_{eff} = \mu_0 / [1 + \theta(V_{GS} - V_{TH})]$, reducing $g_m$ at high $V_{GS}$.

4. **Velocity Saturation:** In sub-100 nm devices, $I_D$ becomes linear rather than quadratic in $V_{OV}$, approximated by $I_D \approx W C_{ox} V_{OV} v_{sat}$.

**(3 marks — 0.75 marks per correct effect with correct impact statement)**

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the small-signal voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance of a **common source amplifier with resistive load**, including channel length modulation. Clearly state the small-signal model and the KCL equations used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A common source stage uses an NMOS transistor with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\,\mu A/V^2$, $W/L = 50$, $V_{TH} = 0.5\,V$, and is biased at $I_D = 0.5\,mA$. The load resistor is $R_D = 2\,k\Omega$ and $\lambda = 0.1\,V^{-1}$. Compute $g_m$, $r_o$, and the small-signal voltage gain. Comment on the effect of $\lambda$ on gain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Step 1:** Draw small-signal model: $g_m v_{gs}$ current source from D to S, $r_o$ between D and S, $R_D$ from D to $V_{DD}$ (AC ground). **(1 mark)**
- **Step 2:** Apply KCL at drain node: $g_m v_{gs} + v_{ds}/r_o + v_{ds}/R_D = 0$. **(2 marks)**
- **Step 3:** Solve: $v_{ds} = -g_m v_{gs} (r_o \parallel R_D)$, so $A_v = v_{ds}/v_{gs} = -g_m (r_o \parallel R_D)$. **(2 marks)**
- **Step 4:** Input resistance is $\infty$ (gate is insulated); output resistance $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**
- $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D} = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$.
- $g_m = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.236 \times 10^{-3}\,S = 2.236\,mA/V$. **(2 marks)**
- $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D) = 1/(0.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 20\,k\Omega$. **(2 marks)**
- $R_D \parallel r_o = (2 \times 20)/(2 + 20) = 1.818\,k\Omega$. **(1 mark)**
- $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -2.236 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.818 \times 10^{3} = -4.06\,V/V$. **(1 mark)**
- **Comment:** $\lambda$ controls $r_o$. If $\lambda \to 0$ (long channel), $r_o \to \infty$ and $|A_v| \to g_m R_D = 4.47\,V/V$, an increase of about 10%. Channel length modulation therefore reduces the achievable gain — a critical trade-off in nanometer design. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks)

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the voltage gain and output resistance of a **cascode amplifier**. Compare its output resistance with that of a simple common source stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cascode stage uses $M_1$ and $M_2$ both biased at $I_D = 0.2\,mA$ with $g_m = 1\,mA/V$ and $\lambda = 0.05\,V^{-1}$ for each. If the load is an ideal current source (infinite impedance), compute the overall voltage gain. How does this compare to a single CS stage under identical bias? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- The cascode is $M_1$ (CS) feeding $M_2$ (CG) with $M_2$'s gate at AC ground. **(1 mark)**
- Looking into the drain of $M_2$ (output port), the small-signal resistance of the cascode transistor pair is obtained by applying a test voltage $v_x$ at the drain of $M_2$ and computing the resulting current $i_x$. The standard result: **(3 marks)**
$$R_{out,cascode} = r_{o2} + r_{o1} + g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1} \approx g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}$$
- For a CS alone: $R_{out,CS} = r_{o1}$. Thus cascode provides an enhancement factor of approximately $g_{m2} r_{o2}$. **(2 marks)**
- Voltage gain: $A_v = -g_{m1} R_{out,cascode} = -g_{m1} g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution (b):**
- $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D) = 1/(0.05 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}) = 100\,k\Omega$ for each transistor. **(1 mark)**
- Cascode gain: $A_{v,cascode} = -g_{m1} g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2} = -(10^{-3}) \times (10^{-3}) \times (10^5) \times (10^5) = -10^4\,V/V = -80\,dB$. **(2 marks)**
- CS gain: $A_{v,CS} = -g_{m1} (r_{o1} \parallel R_{load})$. With ideal current-source load ($R_{load} = \infty$), $A_{v,CS} = -g_{m1} r_{o1} = -10^{-3} \times 10^5 = -100\,V/V = -40\,dB$. **(2 marks)**
- **Comparison:** Cascode is $100\times$ better. The cascode is essential in high-gain op-amps and OTAs where the $g_m r_o$ of one transistor is insufficient. **(1 mark)**
- **Note:** Although cascode gain is much higher, the **headroom** is reduced because two $V_{DS,sat}$ drops now stack. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include $r_o$:** A staggering 60% of KTU scripts lose 1–2 marks by ignoring channel length modulation. The examiner expects $r_o$ in **every** modern analysis.
> 2. **Sign errors in $A_v$:** CS and cascode are **inverting** (negative $A_v$); SF and CG are **non-inverting**. Confusing these costs full marks on the gain question.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in $g_m$:** Always express $g_m$ in A/V (or mA/V) consistently. Mixing $A/V$ with $k\Omega$ yields wrong answers.
> 4. **Miller multiplication omitted:** For CS, the input capacitance becomes $C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + |A_v|)$. This is the #1 reason students get bandwidth questions wrong.
> 5. **Diode-connected load analysis:** A common error is to assume $R_{out,diode} = r_o$ — it is actually $1/g_m \parallel r_o \approx 1/g_m$.
> 6. **Cascode derivation shortcut:** Students often quote $g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}$ without deriving the impedance boost. The examiner wants at least one KCL step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MOS square law (saturation):** $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$.
- **Transconductance** has three equivalent forms: $\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV}$, $\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$, and $2 I_D / V_{OV}$.
- **Output resistance:** $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$, decreases with shorter channels and higher bias current.
- **Body effect** introduces $g_{mb} = \eta g_m$ where $\eta = 0.1$ to $0.3$ — include it whenever source is not at bulk.
- **Common Source:** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$, **inverting**, infinite $R_{in}$, moderate $R_{out}$, Miller-limited bandwidth.
- **Source Follower:** $A_v \approx 1$, **non-inverting**, $R_{out} \approx 1/g_m$, ideal for impedance buffering.
- **Common Gate:** $A_v = +g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$, **non-inverting**, $R_{in} = 1/g_m$ (low!), no Miller effect, ideal for high-frequency or low-impedance sources.
- **Cascode:** $A_v \approx -g_{m1} g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$, with output resistance boosted by $g_{m2} r_{o2}$ — the **workhorse of high-gain analog ICs**.
- **Diode-connected load** has $R_{out} \approx 1/g_m$, useful for low-voltage low-gain stages.
- **Current-source load** provides maximum DC bias stability and the highest gain for a given power budget.
- **Trade-off mantra:** Gain $\uparrow$ ↔ Bandwidth $\downarrow$ ↔ Headroom $\downarrow$ — every design decision shifts this balance.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Topper Strategy:** On the day of the exam, memorize the gain expressions in the order: **CS, CG, SF, Cascode**. Recall that CS and Cascode invert, CG and SF do not. Always include $r_o$. Always convert units of $I_D$ to **amperes** before plugging into $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$. This single habit will prevent **80% of computational errors**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple, Cascode, and Basic Concepts of Active Current Mirror
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Simple, Cascode, and Basic Concepts of Active Current Mirror # ⚡ Module 2 — Current Mirrors in CMOS Analog Design A **Current Mirror** is the fundamental analog building block of CMOS integrated circuits. It copies (mirrors) a reference current flowing through one MOSFET branch into another branch, maintaining a precise ratio. Current mirrors serve as **biasing elements**, **active loads**, and **signal processing elements** in op-amps, OTAs, ADC/DAC references, and bandgap references. --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Simple (Basic) MOSFET Current Mirror **Formal KTU Definition:** > A *simple NMOS current mirror* consists of two matched NMOS transistors M1 and M2, where M1 is **diode-connected** (gate tied to drain), and the gate of M2 is tied to the gate of M1, forcing both devices to share the same **V_GS**. Since V_GS1 = V_GS2, and assuming matched (μ_n C_ox) and equal V_TH, the drain current of M2 becomes a scaled replica of the current forced through M1. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Operating Principle:** The diode-connected device M1 establishes a V_GS that produces a specific I_REF. M2, with identical V_GS, sources the same drain current (or scaled version depending on aspect ratios). This is the cornerstone of all analog CMOS design. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **water tap system**. A main pipe (reference branch) has water flowing at a fixed rate. A **pressure regulator** (the diode-connected M1) reads the pressure and sets the valve position. A second identical pipe (mirror branch) uses the **same valve setting** to deliver exactly the same flow rate. If you make the second pipe wider (increase W/L ratio), more water flows — this is exactly what happens when we scale the W/L of M2. **Key Physical Constants/Parameters (Bolded):** - **I_REF** — Reference current (input current) - **I_OUT** — Mirrored output current - **V_DS,sat = V_GS − V_TH** — Saturation voltage (overdrive voltage, V_OV) - **μ_n C_ox** — Process transconductance parameter - **λ** — Channel-length modulation coefficient - **g_m** — Small-signal transconductance - **r_o = 1/(λI_D)** — Small-signal output resistance --- ### 1.2 Cascode Current Mirror **Formal KTU Definition:** > A *cascode current mirror* places a **cascode transistor** (M3, M4) on top of a basic mirror (M1, M2). This stacking greatly increases the **output impedance** and suppresses the channel-length modulation effect, making I_OUT nearly independent of the output voltage V_OUT. It is the workhorse precision biasing circuit. **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the simple mirror as a **single water valve** whose flow rate slightly fluctuates with downstream pressure (channel length modulation). Adding a cascode transistor is like placing a **second pressure-regulating valve** in series — now the downstream pressure variations barely affect the upstream flow. The output current stays rock-solid even as V_OUT swings. --- ### 1.3 Active Current Mirror (Active Load) **Formal KTU Definition:** > An *active current mirror* refers to the **use of a current mirror as an active load** in a differential amplifier or gain stage. Typically, a **PMOS current mirror** (M3, M4) replaces the resistive load at the output node of an NMOS differential pair (M1, M2). The mirror converts differential signal currents into a single-ended output voltage with maximum gain, since the PMOS mirror presents a very high small-signal impedance ≈ **2·r_o**. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **seesaw** (the differential pair). When you push one side down, the other side goes up. Normally, the seesaw's weight (load) limits how high it goes. Replace the weight with a **strong spring (active current mirror load)** — now even small pushes create big vertical motion (large voltage gain). The active load is the "spring" that converts tiny current differences into large voltage swings. > [!NOTE] > **Why Active Loads?** They provide high gain without needing huge resistors (which consume massive silicon area in CMOS). The small-signal gain of a differential stage with active load is **A_v = g_m · (r_on ∥ r_op)** ≈ **g_m · r_o²**, which is enormous. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Simple NMOS Current Mirror — Operation **Circuit Topology:** M1 (diode-connected, gate = drain), M2 (gate tied to gate of M1). Both have source at ground. **Operation Steps:** - **Step 1:** Reference current I_REF flows into the drain of M1. - **Step 2:** Because M1 is diode-connected, V_GS1 is forced such that I_D1 = I_REF (assuming M1 is in saturation). - **Step 3:** Gate of M2 is tied to gate of M1, so **V_GS2 = V_GS1**. - **Step 4:** Both transistors have the same V_GS. If (W/L)_1 = (W/L)_2 and matched, then **I_OUT = I_D2 = I_D1 = I_REF**. - **Step 5:** If aspect ratios differ, the currents scale: **I_OUT = I_REF × [(W/L)_2 / (W/L)_1]**. **V_GS Equation (in saturation):** $$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$ **Channel-Length Modulation Effect:** Without λ, the mirror is perfect. With λ, M1 and M2 have different V_DS, so: $$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \frac{(1 + \lambda V_{DS2})}{(1 + \lambda V_{DS1})}$$ This is the **systematic mirror error** caused by finite output impedance. --- ### 2.2 Cascode Current Mirror — Operation **Topology:** M1 (diode-connected), M2 + M3 (cascode mirror branch), M4 (cascode on reference branch). V_BIAS sets the gate voltage of cascode transistors M3, M4 so that V_DS2 ≈ V_DS1. **Why Cascode?** - **Boosts output impedance** from r_o to approximately **g_m · r_o²**. - **Suppresses λ-induced mismatch** because both branches now have similar V_DS. **Output Impedance Derivation (small-signal):** $$r_{out,cascode} \approx r_{o2} + r_{o4} + g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o4} \approx g_m r_o^2$$ For typical values (g_m ≈ 1 mA/V, r_o ≈ 10 kΩ): r_out,cascode ≈ **10 MΩ** — three orders of magnitude higher than simple mirror. **Minimum Output Voltage (Headroom):** V_OUT must be high enough to keep M2 and M4 in saturation: - M4 (cascode): V_DS4 ≥ V_GS4 − V_TH - M2 (mirror): V_DS2 ≥ V_GS2 − V_TH $$V_{OUT,min} = V_{GS2} - V_{TH} + V_{GS4} - V_{TH} = 2V_{OV} + 2V_{TH}$$ This is the **headroom penalty** of cascode mirrors. --- ### 2.3 Wide-Swing Cascode Mirror **Innovation:** Bias M4 with V_GS4 = V_OV (overdrive), so the gate of M4 is biased at **V_BIAS = V_GS1 + V_OV**. This reduces headroom. $$V_{OUT,min,wide} \approx 2V_{OV} + V_{TH}$$ This is roughly **one V_TH lower** than the standard cascode. --- ### 2.4 Active Current Mirror Load (PMOS Load on NMOS Diff Pair) **Operation:** - M1, M2 = NMOS differential pair (tail current I_SS) - M3, M4 = PMOS current mirror load - Differential input (v_in+ on M1, v_in− on M2) - When v_id > 0: I_D1 increases by Δi, I_D2 decreases by Δi - Current through M3 increases → mirrored to M4 → M4 pulls more current from output node - Net result: **output current = 2·Δi** (single-ended conversion) **Small-Signal Differential Gain:** $$A_v = g_{m1,2} \cdot (r_{o1,2} \parallel r_{o3,4}) \approx g_m \cdot \frac{r_o}{2} \cdot 2 = g_m r_o$$ Wait — more accurately, for a differential pair with active load: $$A_v = g_{mN} (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$$ For matched r_oN = r_oP = r_o: $$A_v = g_m \cdot \frac{r_o}{2}$$ If the load is a *cascode* PMOS mirror: A_v ≈ **g_m · r_o² / 2** — extremely large. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Unit | |---|---------|---------|------------------| | 1 | Drain current (sat) | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}\tfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | V (V_GS), A (I_D) | | 2 | Drain current (with λ) | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}\tfrac{W}{L}V_{OV}^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | Includes CLM | | 3 | Mirror ratio | $I_{OUT}/I_{REF} = (W/L)_2/(W/L)_1$ | Ideal, matched V_TH, μC_ox | | 4 | Mirror error (λ) | $\Delta I/I = \lambda (V_{DS2}-V_{DS1})$ | Systematic error | | 5 | Simple mirror r_out | $r_{out} = r_{o2} = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ | Ohms | | 6 | Cascode mirror r_out | $r_{out} \approx g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o4}$ | Ohms, ≈ g_m r_o² | | 7 | V_OUT,min (cascode) | $V_{OUT,min} = 2V_{OV} + 2V_{TH}$ | Volts | | 8 | V_OUT,min (wide-swing) | $V_{OUT,min} \approx 2V_{OV} + V_{TH}$ | Volts | | 9 | Active load gain | $A_v = g_m (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ | Dimensionless | | 10 | Active load gain (cascode load) | $A_v \approx \tfrac{1}{2} g_m r_o^2$ | Dimensionless | | 11 | Transconductance | $g_m = \mu C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV} = \sqrt{2\mu C_{ox}(W/L) I_D}$ | A/V | | 12 | Overdrive voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Volts | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Current mirrors are used in **bandgap references** (PTAT/CTAT summation), **bias generators**, **transimpedance amplifiers**, **sample-and-hold circuits**, **ADC/DAC bias networks**, and as **active loads in op-amp gain stages**. Without current mirrors, integrated analog circuits cannot exist. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.6 Why Channel-Length Modulation Degrades the Simple Mirror In a real MOSFET, increasing V_DS pinches the channel slightly, causing I_D to rise. The factor $(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ captures this. For M1 (diode-connected): V_DS1 = V_GS1. For M2: V_DS2 = V_OUT (whatever the load demands). If V_OUT > V_GS1, then M2 carries **more** current than M1, even with identical V_GS. This is the **mirror error**: $$I_{OUT} = I_{REF} \cdot \frac{(1+\lambda V_{OUT})}{(1+\lambda V_{GS1})}$$ For I_REF = 100 μA, λ = 0.01 V⁻¹, V_OUT = 3V, V_GS1 = 1V: $$I_{OUT} = 100\mu A \cdot \frac{1.03}{1.01} \approx 102 \mu A \quad \text{(2% error)}$$ In precision analog (e.g., 12-bit ADC bias), 2% is unacceptable. **Solution: cascode mirror.** --- ## 2.7 The Cascode Mirror — Mathematical Intuition Adding transistors M3 and M4 creates a **regulated cascode structure**. Consider M4 as a common-gate amplifier whose input is V_DS2 (the drain of M2). Any increase in V_OUT that would tend to increase I_OUT is fed back: the extra voltage raises V_DS4, but M4's gate is fixed at V_BIAS, so V_GS4 falls, which **reduces** the current. This is **negative feedback** that stabilizes I_OUT. **Mathematically**, applying KCL at the drain of M2 in small-signal: $$i_{d2} = g_{m2} v_{gs2} + \frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}}$$ But M2 is diode-connected (gate tied to drain of M1, source grounded), so v_gs2 is fixed by M1's V_GS in small-signal — actually, v_gs2 = 0 in pure small-signal because the gate of M2 is at AC ground (tied to gate of M1, both diode connected). Thus M2 acts as a **common-gate stage** for M4, with input v_ds2. The output resistance seen at the drain of M4 is the standard cascode result: $$r_{out} \approx r_{o4} (1 + g_{m4} r_{o2}) \approx g_m r_o^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key insight:** Cascode multiplies output impedance by roughly **g_m · r_o** (the intrinsic gain of a single stage). This gain ranges from 100 to 1000 in modern CMOS. --- ## 2.8 Active Current Mirror Load — Why It's Critical In a differential amplifier, the *small-signal differential current* must be converted into a *single-ended output voltage*. Without an active load, you'd need a resistor — but in CMOS, large resistors are impractical. **How the PMOS active mirror performs differential-to-single-ended conversion:** - Suppose I_D1 = I_SS/2 + i_d, I_D2 = I_SS/2 − i_d (small-signal differential currents). - M3 carries I_D1's AC component i_d. - M3 is diode-connected, so its V_GS3 changes to absorb i_d. - This V_GS3 is shared with M4 (mirror action), so M4 also tries to carry i_d. - But the total current at the output node is: I_D2 − I_D4 = (I_SS/2 − i_d) − (I_SS/2 + i_d) = **−2i_d**. The output node sees **twice** the differential current — hence the factor of 2 in active-load diff amp gain. The voltage developed across r_out = r_oN ∥ r_oP: $$v_{out} = -2 i_d \cdot (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$$ $$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{id}} = g_{mN} (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$$ > [!TIP] > **Real-world utility:** This architecture (NMOS diff pair + PMOS active mirror) is the **input stage of virtually every op-amp** — including the legendary two-stage Miller op-amp, folded-cascode OTA, and telescopic op-amp. # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Derivation 1: Current Ratio of a Simple NMOS Mirror **Given:** - M1 diode-connected, M2 with gate tied to M1's gate. - Both in saturation. - Matched: μ_n C_ox, V_TH; only (W/L) may differ. **Step 1:** Write KCL at the drain of M1. $$I_{REF} = I_{D1} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS1})$$ **Step 2:** Write I_D for M2. Since gate of M2 = gate of M1, V_GS2 = V_GS1. $$I_{OUT} = I_{D2} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS2} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS2})$$ **Step 3:** Subtract (V_GS2 − V_TH) = (V_GS1 − V_TH), and divide I_OUT by I_REF. $$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \frac{(1+\lambda V_{DS2})}{(1+\lambda V_{DS1})}$$ **Step 4:** Ignore λ for ideal case: $$\boxed{\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}}$$ --- ## Derivation 2: Output Impedance of Cascode Mirror **Circuit:** M1 (diode-connected) carries I_REF; M2 mirrors into M4's drain through M3; M4 is the cascode on top of M2. V_BIAS sets V_GS3 = V_GS4. **Step 1:** Apply a test voltage v_x at the drain of M4 (output node). Measure i_x. **Step 2:** M2 is diode-connected (small-signal: gate at AC ground, source at ground). So v_gs2 = 0 in pure small-signal — wait, M2 is NOT diode-connected here. Let me re-set up: **Corrected topology:** M1 = diode-connected reference. M2 = mirror (gate tied to M1's gate). M3 = cascode above M2 (gate at V_BIAS). M4 = cascode above M1 (gate at V_BIAS). Output node = drain of M3. **Step 3:** Now v_x is applied at drain of M3. Current i_x flows in. We need r_out = v_x / i_x. **Step 4:** M3 is in saturation. Its drain current is: $$i_{d3} = g_{m3} v_{gs3} + \frac{v_{ds3}}{r_{o3}}$$ **Step 5:** v_gs3 = v_g3 − v_s3 = V_BIAS − v_{ds2} = −v_{ds2} (since V_BIAS is AC ground). **Step 6:** KCL at drain of M2 (node v_ds2). Current into M2 from M3's source equals current into M2's drain, which flows out through M2's source (grounded): $$i_{d3} = -i_{d2} = -\frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}}$$ Therefore: $$-\frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}} = g_{m3}(-v_{ds2}) + \frac{v_{ds2} - v_x}{r_{o3}}$$ Wait — let me redo: v_ds3 = v_x − v_{ds2}. So: $$-\frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}} = -g_{m3} v_{ds2} + \frac{v_x - v_{ds2}}{r_{o3}}$$ **Step 7:** Multiply through by r_o2 · r_o3: $$-v_{ds2} r_{o3} = -g_{m3} v_{ds2} r_{o2} r_{o3} + r_{o2}(v_x - v_{ds2})$$ **Step 8:** Solve for v_ds2: $$v_{ds2} \left[-r_{o3} + g_{m3} r_{o2} r_{o3} + r_{o2}\right] = r_{o2} v_x$$ $$v_{ds2} = \frac{r_{o2} v_x}{r_{o2} + r_{o3} + g_{m3} r_{o2} r_{o3}}$$ **Step 9:** Now total i_x is the current into drain of M3 plus current through r_o3 to v_x. Actually i_x = current from v_x into the circuit. At output node: i_x = −i_{d3} (flowing into drain of M3 from v_x) plus the current through r_o3. Using v_ds3 = v_x − v_{ds2}, the small-signal drain current of M3: $$i_{d3} = g_{m3}(-v_{ds2}) + \frac{v_x - v_{ds2}}{r_{o3}}$$ **Step 10:** The current i_x delivered by v_x enters the output node and splits into M3's drain. So i_x = i_{d3}. Substitute v_ds2: $$i_x = g_{m3} \left(-\frac{r_{o2} v_x}{r_{o2} + r_{o3}(1+g_{m3}r_{o2})}\right) + \frac{v_x}{r_{o3}} - \frac{r_{o2} v_x}{r_{o3}(r_{o2}+r_{o3}(1+g_{m3}r_{o2}))}$$ **Step 11:** For g_m r_o ≫ 1, the dominant term simplifies (after algebra): $$i_x \approx \frac{v_x}{g_{m3} r_{o2} r_{o3}}$$ $$\boxed{r_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} \approx g_{m3} r_{o2} r_{o3} \approx g_m r_o^2}$$ --- ## Derivation 3: Active Load Differential Amplifier Gain **Circuit:** NMOS pair M1, M2 (sources tied to I_SS). PMOS pair M3, M4 (current mirror load). Input v_id = v_g1 − v_g2 applied to gates of M1, M2. **Step 1:** Differential half-circuit analysis. Each side sees transconductance g_mN converting v_id/2 into current g_mN · (v_id/2). **Step 2:** At the output node, the AC current from M1's drain (which is i_d1 = +g_mN · v_id/2) flows through M3 (diode-connected in PMOS load). M3 mirrors this to M4. M4's source current = i_d3 = g_mN · v_id/2. **Step 3:** The AC current at output node: - M2's drain contributes i_d2 = −g_mN · v_id/2 (sinks current, since v_id/2 < 0 for M2) - M4's drain contributes i_d4 = −g_mN · v_id/2 (PMOS sourcing current equivalent) **Step 4:** Total output current (out of the node): $$i_{out} = -i_{d2} - i_{d4} = \frac{g_{mN} v_{id}}{2} + \frac{g_{mN} v_{id}}{2} = g_{mN} v_{id}$$ Wait, careful with sign. Let's define v_id = v_g1 − v_g2 > 0. Then v_gs1 = +v_id/2, v_gs2 = −v_id/2. So: - i_d1 = g_mN · v_id/2 (increases) - i_d2 = −g_mN · v_id/2 (decreases) **Step 5:** PMOS M3 carries I_D1's AC part: i_d3 = i_d1 = g_mN · v_id/2. M4 mirrors this: i_d4 = i_d3 = g_mN · v_id/2. **Step 6:** At output node (drain of M2 = drain of M4): - Current entering from M4 (drain current flowing OUT of PMOS = into the node): +i_d4 - Wait, PMOS drain current flows from source to drain (out of drain). So M4 PUSHES current OUT of its drain = INTO output node. So +i_d4 enters. - M2 SINKS current from output node: −i_d2 (since v_gs2 < 0, i_d2 < 0, meaning current actually flows INTO drain, so it adds to output node). KCL at output node: $$i_{out} = i_{d4} + (-i_{d2}) = \frac{g_{mN} v_{id}}{2} + \frac{g_{mN} v_{id}}{2} = g_{mN} v_{id}$$ **Step 7:** This output current flows through the parallel output resistance r_oN ∥ r_oP: $$v_{out} = -i_{out} \cdot (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$$ (The negative sign because the current increases the voltage drop across the resistance.) **Step 8:** Therefore: $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{id}} = g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})}$$ For r_oN = r_oP = r_o: $$A_v = \frac{g_{mN} r_o}{2}$$ --- ## Derivation 4: Systematic Mirror Error from Channel-Length Modulation **Given:** V_DS1 = V_GS1 (M1 diode-connected, source grounded). V_DS2 = V_OUT (M2 drain tied to load). **Step 1:** With λ included: $$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS1}-V_{TH})^2 (1+\lambda V_{GS1})$$ $$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS2}-V_{TH})^2 (1+\lambda V_{OUT})$$ **Step 2:** With V_GS2 = V_GS1 and matched (W/L): $$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{1+\lambda V_{OUT}}{1+\lambda V_{GS1}}$$ **Step 3:** For λ V_OUT, λ V_GS1 ≪ 1, expand using $(1+x)(1+y)^{-1} \approx 1 + x - y$: $$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} \approx 1 + \lambda(V_{OUT} - V_{GS1})$$ $$\boxed{\text{Mirror Error: } \frac{\Delta I}{I_{REF}} = \lambda(V_{OUT}-V_{GS1})}$$ For V_OUT = 3V, V_GS1 = 1V, λ = 0.01 V⁻¹: $$\frac{\Delta I}{I_{REF}} = 0.01 \times 2 = 0.02 = 2\%$$ This 2% systematic error is **eliminated by cascode topology**, where V_DS2 ≈ V_GS1 by design. --- ## Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table | Parameter | Simple Mirror | Cascode Mirror | Wide-Swing Cascode | |-----------|---------------|----------------|---------------------| | **r_out** | $r_o$ | $g_m r_o^2$ | $g_m r_o^2$ | | **V_OUT,min** | $V_{OV}$ | $2V_{OV}+2V_{TH}$ | $2V_{OV}+V_{TH}$ | | **Mirror accuracy** | Poor (λ-dependent) | Excellent | Excellent | | **Transistor count** | 2 | 4 | 4 | | **Bias complexity** | None | 1 V_BIAS | 1 V_BIAS (modified) | | **Use case** | Biasing, low-precision | Precision bias, op-amp loads | Low-voltage precision | # 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1: Simple NMOS Current Mirror (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["VDD Supply"] --> nodeR["Reference Current Source IREF"] nodeR --> nodeM1["M1: Diode-Connected NMOS

Current Attenuation"] style nodeA fill:#cce5ff style nodeE fill:#d4edda style nodeF fill:#fff3cd style nodeG fill:#f8d7da ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is a current mirror? Why is a MOSFET used as a diode-connected device in a simple current mirror? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **current mirror** is a circuit that copies a reference current from one branch into another, maintaining a precise ratio determined by the aspect ratios (W/L) of the MOSFETs involved. A MOSFET is used as a **diode-connected** device (gate tied to drain) because: 1. It always operates in **saturation** (since V_DS = V_GS > V_GS − V_TH). 2. The V_GS developed is a well-defined function of I_D, given by the square-law: $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$. 3. It acts as a **small-signal short** (low impedance ≈ 1/g_m), allowing V_GS to adjust automatically to whatever I_REF is forced through it.
