This V_GS is then imposed on the mirror transistor M2, ensuring I_OUT tracks I_REF. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the advantages and disadvantages of a cascode current mirror over a simple current mirror. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages:** 1. **Higher output impedance** (≈ g_m r_o² vs r_o for simple), making I_OUT nearly independent of V_OUT. 2. **Better mirror accuracy** — systematic error from channel-length modulation is suppressed because V_DS of the mirror transistor is regulated. 3. **Better PSRR** (power supply rejection ratio) for biasing applications. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Reduced output voltage swing** (headroom penalty): V_OUT,min = 2V_OV + 2V_TH instead of just V_OV. 2. **More transistors** (4 instead of 2), increasing silicon area. 3. **Requires additional bias voltage** V_BIAS, complicating the design. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Draw the circuit of a simple NMOS current mirror and derive the expression for the output current I_OUT in terms of the reference current I_REF, considering channel-length modulation. Comment on the systematic mirror error. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Design a cascode current mirror to provide I_OUT = 200 μA from I_REF = 100 μA using 0.18 μm CMOS technology with μ_n C_ox = 250 μA/V², V_TH = 0.5 V, λ = 0.05 V⁻¹. Assume all transistors have L = 0.5 μm. Compute the (W/L) ratios and the output impedance. **[7 marks]** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Simple Current Mirror Analysis **Circuit:** Two NMOS transistors M1 (diode-connected) and M2 (mirror). Both have source at ground. Gate of M2 = gate of M1. I_REF enters drain of M1; I_OUT leaves drain of M2. **Derivation:** $$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1+\lambda V_{GS})$$ $$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1+\lambda V_{OUT})$$ Taking the ratio: $$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}\cdot\frac{(1+\lambda V_{OUT})}{(1+\lambda V_{GS})}$$ For matched aspect ratios (W/L)_1 = (W/L)_2: $$I_{OUT} = I_{REF}\cdot\frac{1+\lambda V_{OUT}}{1+\lambda V_{GS}}$$ **Systematic Error:** Since V_OUT typically ≠ V_GS, the mirror ratio deviates from unity. The percentage error is: $$\frac{\Delta I}{I_{REF}} \approx \lambda(V_{OUT} - V_{GS})$$ For typical values (λ = 0.05 V⁻¹, V_OUT − V_GS = 2V), the error ≈ **10%** — unacceptable for precision analog design. **Solution:** use cascode mirror to regulate V_DS of M2. --- #### Part (b) — Cascode Mirror Design **Given:** I_OUT = 200 μA, I_REF = 100 μA, μ_n C_ox = 250 μA/V², V_TH = 0.5 V, λ = 0.05 V⁻¹, L = 0.5 μm. **Step 1:** Mirror ratio = I_OUT / I_REF = 200/100 = **2**. Choose (W/L)_1 = 10/0.5 = 20, then (W/L)_2 = 2 × 20 = **40** (i.e., W_2 = 20 μm at L = 0.5 μm). **Step 2:** Compute V_OV from I_REF equation: $$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 V_{OV}^2$$ $$100 \mu A = \frac{1}{2}(250 \mu A/V^2)(20) V_{OV}^2$$ $$V_{OV}^2 = \frac{100 \mu A}{2500 \mu A/V^2} = 0.04 V^2$$ $$V_{OV} = 0.2 V$$ So V_GS = V_TH + V_OV = 0.5 + 0.2 = **0.7 V**. **Step 3:** Output impedance r_o of each transistor: $$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$$ For M2 (carrying I_OUT = 200 μA): $$r_{o2} = \frac{1}{0.05 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$ For M3 (cascode, also 200 μA): $$r_{o3} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 4:** Compute g_m of M3: $$g_{m3} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 200 \mu A}{0.2 V} = 2 \text{ mA/V}$$ **Step 5:** Cascode output impedance: $$r_{out} \approx g_{m3} r_{o2} r_{o3} = (2 \text{ mA/V})(100 \text{ k}\Omega)^2 = 20 \text{ M}\Omega$$ **Final Design Summary:** | Component | Value | |-----------|-------| | (W/L)_1 (M1) | 20 | | (W/L)_2 (M2) | 40 | | (W/L)_3, (W/L)_4 (cascode) | 40 each | | V_GS | 0.7 V | | V_OV | 0.2 V | | r_out | ≈ 20 MΩ | | V_OUT,min | 0.4 + 1.0 = **1.4 V** | --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the working of an active current mirror used as a load in a CMOS differential amplifier. Derive the small-signal differential gain. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A PMOS current mirror load is used with an NMOS differential pair (μ_n C_ox = 100 μA/V², μ_p C_ox = 50 μA/V², V_TH,N = 0.5 V, V_TH,P = −0.5 V, λ_N = λ_P = 0.04 V⁻¹, I_SS = 200 μA). For (W/L)_1,2 = 25 and (W/L)_3,4 = 50, calculate the differential gain. **[7 marks]** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Active Current Mirror as Differential Amplifier Load **Circuit Description:** A CMOS differential amplifier with active PMOS current mirror load consists of: - **M1, M2**: NMOS differential input pair (common-source configuration with tail current I_SS). - **M3, M4**: PMOS current mirror (M3 diode-connected, M4 mirrors). - **Inputs**: v_in+ to gate of M1; v_in− to gate of M2. - **Output**: taken at the common drain of M2 and M4. **Working Principle:** When v_id = v_in+ − v_in− is applied: - M1 current increases by Δi = (g_mN · v_id)/2 - M2 current decreases by the same Δi M3 (diode-connected PMOS) carries M1's increased current. Since M3 and M4 share V_GS (gate tied together), M4 mirrors this increment. **At the output node:** - M2 tries to sink LESS current (Δi less) - M4 sources MORE current (Δi more) - Net output current change = **2Δi** (differential-to-single-ended conversion) **Derivation of Gain:** $$i_{out} = 2 \cdot \Delta i = 2 \cdot \frac{g_{mN} v_{id}}{2} = g_{mN} v_{id}$$ This current flows through the parallel combination of r_oN (output resistance of M2) and r_oP (output resistance of M4): $$v_{out} = -i_{out}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$$ $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{id}} = g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})}$$ For r_oN = r_oP = r_o: $$A_v = \frac{g_{mN} r_o}{2}$$ If a cascode PMOS load is used (telescopic structure): $$A_v \approx \frac{g_{mN} r_o^2}{2}$$ This achieves gains of **1000 to 10000** in modern CMOS — far exceeding what resistive loads can provide. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Gain Calculation **Given:** μ_n C_ox = 100 μA/V², μ_p C_ox = 50 μA/V², V_TH,N = 0.5 V, V_TH,P = −0.5 V, λ_N = λ_P = 0.04 V⁻¹, I_SS = 200 μA, (W/L)_1,2 = 25, (W/L)_3,4 = 50. **Step 1:** Each NMOS carries I_SS/2 = 100 μA. **Step 2:** Compute V_OV for NMOS pair: $$I_{D1} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 V_{OV,N}^2$$ $$100 \mu A = \frac{1}{2}(100 \mu A/V^2)(25) V_{OV,N}^2$$ $$V_{OV,N}^2 = \frac{100}{1250} = 0.08 V^2 \implies V_{OV,N} = 0.283 V$$ **Step 3:** Compute g_mN: $$g_{mN} = \mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 V_{OV,N} = (100 \mu A/V^2)(25)(0.283 V) = 707 \mu A/V$$ Or equivalently: $g_{mN} = 2I_D/V_{OV,N} = 200\mu A / 0.283V = 707 \mu A/V$. ✓ **Step 4:** Compute V_OV for PMOS pair. Each PMOS also carries 100 μA: $$V_{OV,P}^2 = \frac{2 \times 100 \mu A}{50 \mu A/V^2 \times 50} = \frac{200}{2500} = 0.08 V^2$$ $$V_{OV,P} = 0.283 V \text{ (symmetric)}$$ **Step 5:** Compute r_o for NMOS and PMOS: $$r_{oN} = \frac{1}{\lambda_N I_{D1}} = \frac{1}{0.04 \times 100\mu A} = 250 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$r_{oP} = \frac{1}{\lambda_P I_{D3}} = \frac{1}{0.04 \times 100\mu A} = 250 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 6:** Compute differential gain: $$A_v = g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}) = (707 \mu A/V) \left(\frac{250 \text{ k}\Omega}{2}\right)$$ $$A_v = (707 \times 10^{-6})(125 \times 10^3) = 88.4$$ $$\boxed{A_v \approx 88.4 \text{ V/V} \approx 38.9 \text{ dB}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in differential-to-single-ended conversion: Many students write A_v = g_m · r_o (no half). The correct answer is g_m · r_o/2 for a simple active load. **Marks lost: 2-3.** > 2. **Confusing PMOS V_GS with NMOS V_GS**: PMOS V_GS is negative (V_GS,P = V_G − V_S = −V_SG). Use |V_GS,P| for square-law calculations. **Marks lost: 2.** > 3. **Ignoring λ in gain calculations**: Even when the question gives λ, students sometimes use g_m · R_D formula (resistive load), forgetting that active load has r_o instead of R_D. **Marks lost: 3-4.** > 4. **Wrong mirror ratio formula**: For cascode mirror, the output impedance is g_m · r_o², NOT 2 · r_o or r_o. **Marks lost: 2.** > 5. **Not specifying V_OUT,min** when asked to comment on headroom. Always include: $V_{OUT,min} = V_{OV,M2} + V_{OV,M3} + (V_{GS3} - V_{GS3,something})$ — show the full derivation. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ A **simple current mirror** uses a diode-connected MOSFET to set V_GS, which is then forced on a mirror transistor to replicate current. - ✅ The **mirror ratio** equals the ratio of W/L: $\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$ (assuming matched λ effects are negligible). - ✅ **Channel-length modulation** introduces systematic mirror error: $\Delta I / I = \lambda(V_{OUT} - V_{GS1})$. For V_OUT >> V_GS1, this error can be 5–10%. - ✅ **Cascode mirror** adds two transistors (M3, M4) stacked on the basic mirror, with V_BIAS regulating their gates. It boosts **r_out from r_o to ~g_m·r_o²**. - ✅ Cascode mirror has **headroom penalty**: V_OUT,min = 2V_OV + 2V_TH (standard) or 2V_OV + V_TH (wide-swing). - ✅ **Active current mirror load** = PMOS current mirror as load on NMOS diff pair. Provides single-ended output from differential input with maximum gain. - ✅ Active load **differential gain**: $A_v = g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}) = g_{mN} r_o / 2$ for matched r_o. With cascode load: ~g_m r_o²/2. - ✅ The active load converts differential to single-ended by summing M2's reduced current with M4's mirrored increased current — yielding **2× current**. - ✅ For KTU problems: always compute V_OV first using the I_D equation, then derive g_m = 2I_D/V_OV, then r_o = 1/(λI_D), then assemble the gain. - ✅ **Mirror accuracy hierarchy**: Simple (poor) < Cascode (excellent) < Regulated Cascode (precision). - ✅ **Common bias issues**: Forgetting body effect, mismatched V_TH, mobility degradation, and finite output resistance at the bias node.

topics.title: Miller Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Miller Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Miller Effect (Miller's Theorem):** In electronics, the **Miller Effect** refers to the multiplication of the effective input capacitance of an inverting voltage amplifier by a factor of $(1 - A_v)$, where $A_v$ is the voltage gain of the amplifier. This occurs because the impedance between the input and output nodes is effectively "bootstrapped" by the amplifier's gain, leading to an apparent increase in the capacitance as seen at the input terminal.

In the context of **CMOS Circuit Design**, the Miller Effect is critically important because the **gate-to-drain overlap capacitance** ($C_{gd}$) — present intrinsically in every MOSFET — gets multiplied by the large negative gain of a common-source amplifier, drastically increasing the effective input capacitance ($C_{in,eff}$).

### 1.2 Physical Mechanism

In any inverting amplifier, when the input voltage $V_{in}$ increases by $\Delta V$, the output voltage changes by approximately $A_v \cdot \Delta V$ in the opposite direction. Therefore, the total voltage change across the feedback impedance (typically a capacitor $C_f$) between the input and output nodes is:

$$\Delta V_{feedback} = V_{in} - V_{out} = \Delta V - A_v \cdot \Delta V = \Delta V(1 - A_v)$$

Since the same current flows through the feedback capacitor as into the input, the input "sees" an effective capacitance that is $(1 - A_v)$ times larger than the actual feedback capacitance.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Crowded Bus Stop" Effect:**
> Imagine a bus stop (the input node) and a destination (the output node) connected by a narrow, two-way alley (the feedback capacitor). Normally, only a small number of people (charge carriers) can move through the alley.
>
> Now imagine a giant hydraulic press (the amplifier) at the destination that **sucks people away** every time they try to enter. The pull at the destination makes the pressure difference across the alley much larger than just the input pressure alone. As a result, the alley appears to be carrying **far more flow (current)** than its actual width (capacitance) suggests. To the observer at the bus stop, it looks like the alley is much wider than it really is — that perceived extra width is the **Miller Capacitance**.

### 1.4 Significance in CMOS Design

- **Frequency Response Limitation:** The Miller Effect is the **dominant reason** why common-source amplifiers have a much lower bandwidth than common-gate or cascode configurations.
- **Dominant Pole Creation:** The multiplied input capacitance often becomes the **dominant pole** of the amplifier's transfer function.
- **Design Mitigation:** Cascode stages, miller compensation techniques, and neutralization capacitors are all design responses to the Miller Effect.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics

- The effective Miller Capacitance: $C_{M} = C_f \cdot (1 - A_v)$
- Miller's Theorem applies to any **floating impedance** between two nodes where one is an amplified version of the other.
- For an inverting amplifier with $A_v < 0$, the factor $(1 - A_v) \approx \vert A_v \vert$ for large negative gains.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Miller's Theorem — Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Miller's Theorem (1920):** A floating impedance $Z_f$ connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier with gain $A_v$ can be **replaced** by two grounded impedances:
> - $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}$ (connected from input to ground)
> - $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z_f}{1 - \dfrac{1}{A_v}} = \dfrac{Z_f \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ (connected from output to ground)

This theorem converts a "floating" two-port impedance into two single-port grounded impedances, dramatically simplifying small-signal analysis.

### 2.2 Operational Steps — Logical Breakdown

1. **Identify the floating impedance** between the input node and the output node of an inverting gain stage.
2. **Determine the small-signal voltage gain** $A_v$ from input to output (typically negative for common-source amplifiers).
3. **Apply the Miller multiplication factor** $(1 - A_v)$ to the feedback component to find the effective grounded component at the input.
4. **Compute the dominant pole** as $\omega_p = \dfrac{1}{R_{sig} \cdot C_{in,eff}}$, where $R_{sig}$ is the source resistance and $C_{in,eff}$ includes the Miller capacitance.
5. **Assess frequency response impact** by comparing the 3-dB bandwidth with and without the Miller Effect.

### 2.3 Application to Common-Source CMOS Amplifier

In a common-source MOSFET amplifier:
- The intrinsic gate-to-drain overlap capacitance is $C_{gd}$.
- The small-signal voltage gain is $A_v = -g_m \cdot R_D$ (typically large and negative).
- The Miller capacitance appearing at the input is:

$$C_{M} = C_{gd} \cdot (1 - A_v) = C_{gd} \cdot (1 + g_m R_D)$$

This is a **massive multiplicative increase** because $g_m R_D$ is the intrinsic gain of the transistor, often in the range of **10 to 100** in CMOS technologies.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Miller Multiplication Factor | $K_M$ | $1 - A_v$ | Dimensionless | Factor by which feedback C is amplified at input |
| Miller Capacitance | $C_M$ | $C_f \cdot (1 - A_v)$ | Farads (F) | Effective input capacitance due to feedback |
| Input Effective Capacitance (CS amp) | $C_{in,eff}$ | $C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$ | Farads (F) | Total capacitance at gate input |
| Dominant Pole Frequency | $\omega_p$ | $\dfrac{1}{R_{sig} C_{in,eff}}$ | rad/s | -3 dB cutoff angular frequency |
| Effective Input Impedance (Miller) | $Z_1$ | $\dfrac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Grounded equivalent at input |
| Effective Output Impedance (Miller) | $Z_2$ | $\dfrac{Z_f \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Grounded equivalent at output |
| 3-dB Bandwidth (CS amp) | $f_{3dB}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{sig} C_{in,eff}}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Upper -3 dB frequency |
| Cascode Gain Factor | $g_{m,cas}$ | $g_{m1} \cdot R_{cascode}$ | — | Reduced effective gain to mitigate Miller |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** In a typical 0.18 $\mu$m CMOS process, $C_{gs} \approx 5C_{gd}$. Once Miller multiplication kicks in, the Miller term $C_{gd}(1+g_m R_D)$ can become **5 to 20 times larger** than $C_{gs}$, completely dominating the high-frequency response.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Two-Stage Op-Amp Compensation:** Miller compensation intentionally uses a large capacitor across the high-gain second stage to achieve dominant-pole stability.
- **Cascode Amplifier Design:** Adding a cascode device reduces the effective $A_v$ seen by $C_{gd}$, dramatically extending bandwidth.
- **RF CMOS Design:** Neutralization capacitors (cross-coupled $C_{gd}$ cancellation) are used in LNAs to nullify the Miller Effect at GHz frequencies.
- **Switched-Capacitor Circuits:** Miller Effect is a parasitic concern in high-speed sample-and-hold circuits.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Miller's Theorem from Impedance Equivalence

**Setup:** Consider an inverting amplifier with input node $V_1$, output node $V_2$, voltage gain $A_v = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$ (a real, negative number), and a floating impedance $Z_f$ connected between $V_1$ and $V_2$.

**Goal:** Replace $Z_f$ by two grounded impedances $Z_1$ (at input) and $Z_2$ (at output) such that the currents $I_1$ entering $V_1$ and $I_2$ entering $V_2$ remain unchanged.

**Step 1:** Write the current flowing from $V_1$ to $V_2$ through $Z_f$:

$$I_f = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_f}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $V_2 = A_v \cdot V_1$ into the current expression:

$$I_f = \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z_f} = \frac{V_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z_f}$$

**Step 3:** The equivalent grounded impedance at the input $Z_1$ must draw the same current $I_f$ from a voltage $V_1$ applied to ground:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_1}{Z_1} = I_f = \frac{V_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z_f}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $Z_1$:

$$\frac{1}{Z_1} = \frac{1 - A_v}{Z_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_1 = \frac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the same logic at the output node. The current $I_2$ entering the output from the grounded equivalent $Z_2$ must equal the original current $I_f$ (now written as flowing into $V_2$):

$$I_2 = \frac{V_2}{Z_2} = \frac{A_v V_1}{Z_2}$$

**Step 6:** Also, $I_f$ in terms of $V_2$ is:

$$I_f = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_f} = \frac{V_2/A_v - V_2}{Z_f} = \frac{V_2(1 - A_v)}{A_v Z_f}$$

**Step 7:** Equate $I_2 = I_f$:

$$\frac{A_v V_1}{Z_2} = \frac{V_2(1 - A_v)}{A_v Z_f} = \frac{A_v V_1 (1 - A_v)}{A_v Z_f} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z_f}$$

**Step 8:** Solve for $Z_2$:

$$\frac{A_v}{Z_2} = \frac{1 - A_v}{Z_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_2 = \frac{A_v Z_f}{1 - A_v} = \frac{Z_f}{\frac{1}{A_v} - 1}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{Z_1 = \frac{Z_f}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{and} \quad Z_2 = \frac{A_v \cdot Z_f}{1 - A_v}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Miller Capacitance in Common-Source Amplifier

**Setup:** A common-source NMOS amplifier with:
- Transconductance $g_m$
- Drain load resistance $R_D$
- Gate-to-source capacitance $C_{gs}$
- Gate-to-drain (Miller) capacitance $C_{gd}$
- Source resistance $R_{sig}$ driving the gate

**Step 1:** Small-signal voltage gain from gate to drain:

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -g_m R_D$$

**Step 2:** Apply Miller's Theorem. The Miller multiplication factor is:

$$K_M = 1 - A_v = 1 - (-g_m R_D) = 1 + g_m R_D$$

**Step 3:** The effective capacitance at the input (gate) becomes:

$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd} \cdot (1 + g_m R_D)$$

**Step 4:** The dominant pole of the amplifier (assuming the output pole is at a much higher frequency) is:

$$\omega_{p,dom} = \frac{1}{R_{sig} C_{in,eff}} = \frac{1}{R_{sig}\left[C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)\right]}$$

**Step 5:** Express the -3 dB bandwidth:

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{sig}\left[C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)\right]}$$

**Step 6:** Numerical Example: Let $R_{sig} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $g_m = 1\text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 50\text{ fF}$, $C_{gd} = 10\text{ fF}$.

First, compute the gain:

$$A_v = -g_m R_D = -(1\text{ mA/V})(10\text{ k}\Omega) = -10$$

Then, the Miller multiplication factor:

$$K_M = 1 - (-10) = 11$$

The Miller capacitance:

$$C_M = C_{gd} \cdot K_M = (10\text{ fF})(11) = 110\text{ fF}$$

The effective input capacitance:

$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_M = 50\text{ fF} + 110\text{ fF} = 160\text{ fF}$$

Without Miller Effect (ideal), $C_{in,eff}$ would be $60\text{ fF}$ (just $C_{gs} + C_{gd}$). The Miller Effect increased the input capacitance by **267%**!

The -3 dB bandwidth:

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi (10 \times 10^3)(160 \times 10^{-15})} \approx 99.47\text{ MHz}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cascode Amplifier — Miller Effect Mitigation

**Setup:** A cascode amplifier where the gain from the input MOSFET's drain to the cascode source is unity, so $C_{gd}$ of the input device only sees $\approx 1\times$ multiplication.

**Step 1:** For a cascode, the small-signal voltage at the drain of the input transistor (M1) is approximately $V_{gs2}$ of the cascode device (M2), which is very small.

**Step 2:** The effective gain from M1's gate to M1's drain:

$$A_{v1} = \frac{V_{d1}}{V_{g1}} \approx -g_{m1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{g_{m2}} = -\dfrac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} \approx -1$$

(if both devices are matched)

**Step 3:** The Miller capacitance on M1's $C_{gd}$ is now:

$$C_{M,cascode} = C_{gd1} \cdot (1 - A_{v1}) = C_{gd1} \cdot (1 - (-1)) = 2 C_{gd1}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with a common-source amplifier where the gain is $-g_m R_D$:

$$C_{M,CS} = C_{gd} \cdot (1 + g_m R_D) \quad \text{vs.} \quad C_{M,cascode} = 2 C_{gd}$$

**Step 5:** The cascode dramatically reduces the Miller multiplication (e.g., from $11\times$ to $2\times$ in our numerical example), allowing the high-frequency response to be limited only by the intrinsic transistor transit time, not by Miller multiplication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is precisely why cascode amplifiers are the workhorse of high-frequency analog design. The cascode topology does not increase the gain significantly over a common-source stage, but it isolates the high-impedance output node from the Miller-sensitive input node.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| **Parameter** | **Common-Source (CS)** | **Common-Gate (CG)** | **Cascode** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Gain | $-g_m R_D$ (large, neg.) | $g_m R_D$ (large, pos.) | $-g_{m1} R_D$ (large, neg.) |
| $C_{gd}$ seen by Input | $C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$ | $\approx 0$ (source input) | $C_{gd1}(1 + g_{m1}/g_{m2})$ |
| Miller Severity | **Severe** | **None** | **Mild** |
| Dominant Pole | $\dfrac{1}{R_{sig} C_{in,eff}}$ (low) | $\dfrac{g_m}{C_L}$ (high) | $\dfrac{g_m}{C_L}$ (high) |
| Bandwidth | Low | High | High |
| Input Impedance | High | Low ($1/g_m$) | High |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Miller Effect Mechanism

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the feedback capacitor creates an apparent input capacitance through the Miller Effect.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVin["VIN Source"] --> nodeRsig["Rsig Signal Resistance"]
    nodeRsig --> nodeGate["Gate Node of MOSFET"]
    nodeGate --> nodeCgs["Cgs Capacitance to Ground"]
    nodeGate --> nodeCgd["Cgd Feedback Capacitance"]
    nodeCgd --> nodeDrain["Drain Node of MOSFET"]
    nodeGate --> nodeGm["Controlled Current Source gm * Vgs"]
    nodeGm --> nodeDrain
    nodeDrain --> nodeRD["RD Load Resistance"]
    nodeRD --> nodeVout["Vout Output Node"]
    nodeDrain --> nodeCdb["Cdb to Ground"]
    nodeDrain --> nodeCL["CL Load Capacitance"]
    nodeCL --> nodeGND["Ground Node"]
    nodeRD --> nodeGND
    nodeCgs --> nodeGND
    nodeCdb --> nodeGND
    nodeGND(["Ground Reference"])
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Miller Theorem Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Original Circuit: Floating Zf between Vin and Vout"] --> nodeB["Measure Voltage Gain Av = Vout / Vin"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Is Av Large and Negative?"}
    nodeC -- "Yes" --> nodeD["Z1 at Input = Zf / 1 - Av"]
    nodeC -- "Yes" --> nodeE["Z2 at Output = Av Zf / 1 - Av"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Equivalent Grounded Circuit at Input"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Equivalent Grounded Circuit at Output"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Simplified Small-Signal Analysis"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["Compute Effective Input Capacitance CM"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Determine Dominant Pole Frequency"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Compute 3dB Bandwidth"]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture — CS vs Cascode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeCS["Common Source Amplifier"]
        cs1["Vin via Rsig"] --> cs2["Gate of M1"]
        cs2 --> cs3["Cgs to GND"]
        cs2 --> cs4["Cgd to Drain"]
        cs2 --> cs5["gm * Vgs current source"]
        cs5 --> cs6["Drain of M1"]
        cs6 --> cs7["RD to VDD"]
        cs6 --> cs8["Vout Node"]
        cs6 --> cs9["Large Miller Capacitance on Cgd"]
    end
    subgraph nodeCas["Cascode Amplifier"]
        cas1["Vin via Rsig"] --> cas2["Gate of M1"]
        cas2 --> cas3["Cgs1 to GND"]
        cas2 --> cas4["Cgd1 to Drain of M1"]
        cas2 --> cas5["gm1 * Vgs1 current source"]
        cas5 --> cas6["Drain of M1 Source of M2"]
        cas6 --> cas7["Gate of M2 biased at Vbias"]
        cas6 --> cas8["Drain of M2 Vout"]
        cas6 --> cas9["Small Miller Capacitance on Cgd1"]
    end
    nodeCS --> nodeCompare
    nodeCas --> nodeCompare
    nodeCompare["CS: Severe Miller C, Low BW"] --> nodeOutcome["Cascode: Mild Miller C, High BW"]
```

### 4.4 Frequency Response Plot — Conceptual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF1["Frequency Axis f in Hz"] --> nodeF2["Low Frequency Region"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3["Midband DC Gain Av0 in dB"]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4["Corner Frequency f3dB CS amp very low due to Miller"]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5["Corner Frequency f3dB Cascode much higher"]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6["Roll off at -20 dB per decade"]
    nodeF6 --> nodeF7["Unity Gain Frequency fu"]
    nodeF7 --> nodeF8["CS amp fu small"] --> nodeF9["Cascode fu larger"]
```

### 4.5 Detailed Functional Architecture of a Two-Stage Op-Amp with Miller Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1["Input Differential Pair M1 M2"] --> nodeS2["High Gain Node A"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Miller Compensation Capacitor CC across High Gain Stage"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS4["Second Stage Common Source M6"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Output Node Vout"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS6["Load Capacitance CL"]
    nodeS3 -. "Pole Splitting" .-> nodeS7["Pole at A moves to lower frequency"]
    nodeS3 -. "Pole Splitting" .-> nodeS8["Pole at B moves to higher frequency"]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS9["Stability Achieved"]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** The same Miller Effect that degrades common-source amplifier bandwidth is **intentionally exploited** in two-stage op-amp design. A large compensation capacitor $C_C$ connected between the high-gain node and the output uses Miller multiplication to "split" the poles, ensuring stability (phase margin > 45°) when the op-amp is used in unity-gain feedback.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> Define the **Miller Effect**. Explain why the effective input capacitance of a common-source MOSFET amplifier is increased by a factor of $(1 - A_v)$, where $A_v$ is the voltage gain.

**Model Answer:**

The Miller Effect refers to the multiplication of the effective capacitance observed at the input of an inverting amplifier due to a feedback capacitor between input and output. When an input voltage $V_{in}$ changes by $\Delta V$, the output voltage changes by $A_v \Delta V$ in opposite polarity. Hence, the voltage across the feedback capacitor $C_f$ changes by:

$$\Delta V_f = V_{in} - V_{out} = \Delta V(1 - A_v)$$

Since the same current $I = C_f \cdot \dfrac{d(\Delta V_f)}{dt}$ flows through $C_f$, the input "sees" an effective capacitance:

$$C_{M} = C_f (1 - A_v)$$

In a common-source amplifier, the feedback capacitor is the gate-to-drain overlap capacitance $C_{gd}$, and $A_v = -g_m R_D$ is large and negative. So $C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$, which is dominated by the Miller term for moderate-to-high gain.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> State **Miller's Theorem**. For a common-source amplifier with $g_m = 2\text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, and $C_{gd} = 20\text{ fF}$, calculate the Miller capacitance.

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Miller's Theorem states that a floating impedance $Z_f$ between input and output of an amplifier with gain $A_v$ can be replaced by:
- $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}$ at the input
- $Z_2 = \dfrac{A_v \cdot Z_f}{1 - A_v}$ at the output

**Calculation:**

Step 1 — Voltage gain:

$$A_v = -g_m R_D = -(2\text{ mA/V})(5\text{ k}\Omega) = -10$$

Step 2 — Miller multiplication factor:

$$1 - A_v = 1 - (-10) = 11$$

Step 3 — Miller capacitance:

$$C_M = C_{gd} \cdot (1 - A_v) = 20\text{ fF} \times 11 = 220\text{ fF}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat small-signal equivalent circuit, derive an expression for the **Miller capacitance** in a common-source MOSFET amplifier. Show that the effective input capacitance becomes $C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Draw the small-signal high-frequency equivalent of the common-source amplifier with $R_{sig}$ driving the gate, $C_{gs}$ from gate to source, $C_{gd}$ from gate to drain, and $R_D \parallel C_L$ at the drain.

**Step 2:** The small-signal voltage gain at low frequencies (before $C_L$ becomes significant) is:

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -g_m R_D$$

**Step 3:** Identify $C_{gd}$ as the floating feedback capacitor between gate and drain.

**Step 4:** Apply Miller's Theorem. The Miller capacitance at the input is:

$$C_{M,input} = C_{gd} \cdot (1 - A_v) = C_{gd} \cdot (1 + g_m R_D)$$

**Step 5:** The total effective input capacitance at the gate is the sum of intrinsic $C_{gs}$ and the Miller-multiplied $C_{gd}$:

$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

**Step 6:** The dominant pole of the amplifier is therefore:

$$\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{R_{sig} \cdot C_{in,eff}} = \frac{1}{R_{sig}\left[C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)\right]}$$

**Conclusion:** The Miller Effect dramatically increases the input capacitance, lowering the dominant pole frequency and limiting the high-frequency gain of the common-source amplifier.

---

**(b)** A common-source amplifier has $g_m = 5\text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 4\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 100\text{ fF}$, $C_{gd} = 20\text{ fF}$, and is driven by a source resistance $R_{sig} = 2\text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate **(i)** the midband voltage gain, **(ii)** the Miller capacitance, **(iii)** the effective input capacitance, and **(iv)** the -3 dB bandwidth. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Midband Voltage Gain:**

$$A_v = -g_m R_D = -(5\text{ mA/V})(4\text{ k}\Omega) = -20$$

$$A_v \text{ (in dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(20) \approx 26.02\text{ dB}$$

**(ii) Miller Capacitance:**

$$1 - A_v = 1 - (-20) = 21$$

$$C_M = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = 20\text{ fF} \times 21 = 420\text{ fF}$$

**(iii) Effective Input Capacitance:**

$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_M = 100\text{ fF} + 420\text{ fF} = 520\text{ fF}$$

**(iv) -3 dB Bandwidth:**

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{sig} C_{in,eff}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (2 \times 10^3)(520 \times 10^{-15})}$$

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1.04 \times 10^{-9})} = \frac{1}{6.534 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 153.0\text{ MHz}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain how a **cascode amplifier** mitigates the Miller Effect compared to a common-source amplifier. Derive the effective Miller capacitance in both configurations and show that the cascode has superior high-frequency performance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Common-Source (CS) Amplifier:**
The input device $M_1$ sees its full drain load $R_D$ at the drain. The voltage gain from $M_1$'s gate to its drain is:

$$A_{v,CS} = -g_m R_D \quad (\text{large, e.g., } -20)$$

Miller capacitance:

$$C_{M,CS} = C_{gd}(1 - A_{v,CS}) = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

**Cascode Amplifier:**
The cascode device $M_2$ presents a **low input impedance** of $\approx 1/g_{m2}$ at its source. This is the load seen by $M_1$'s drain. Therefore, the voltage gain from $M_1$'s gate to its drain is:

$$A_{v,M1} = -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \approx -1 \quad (\text{when } g_{m1} = g_{m2})$$

Miller capacitance on $M_1$'s $C_{gd}$:

$$C_{M,cascode} = C_{gd1}(1 - A_{v,M1}) = C_{gd1}(1 - (-1)) = 2 C_{gd1}$$

**Comparison:**
- $C_{M,CS} = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D) = 21 C_{gd}$ (using earlier example)
- $C_{M,cascode} = 2 C_{gd}$

The cascode reduces the Miller capacitance by a factor of $\dfrac{21}{2} = 10.5\times$. The total gain is preserved at the cascode output, but $C_{gd}$ of $M_1$ no longer sees the high-impedance output node, so the Miller multiplication is minimal.

**Result:** The cascode configuration achieves the same DC gain as a common-source amplifier, but with a much higher -3 dB bandwidth and superior high-frequency performance.

---

**(b)** A cascode amplifier uses $M_1$ and $M_2$ with $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = 2\text{ mA/V}$, $C_{gs1} = 80\text{ fF}$, $C_{gd1} = 15\text{ fF}$, $R_D = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, and $R_{sig} = 1\text{ k}\Omega$. Determine **(i)** the overall voltage gain, **(ii)** the effective input capacitance, and **(iii)** the -3 dB bandwidth. Compare the bandwidth with a comparable common-source amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Overall Voltage Gain:**
The cascode gain is the same as a CS amplifier with the same $g_m$ and $R_D$:

$$A_{v,cascode} = -g_{m1} R_D = -(2\text{ mA/V})(5\text{ k}\Omega) = -10$$

**(ii) Effective Input Capacitance:**
The Miller multiplication factor on $M_1$ is:

$$1 - A_{v,M1} = 1 - \left(-\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}\right) = 1 - (-1) = 2$$

Therefore:

$$C_{M,cascode} = C_{gd1} \cdot 2 = 15\text{ fF} \times 2 = 30\text{ fF}$$

$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs1} + C_{M,cascode} = 80\text{ fF} + 30\text{ fF} = 110\text{ fF}$$

**(iii) -3 dB Bandwidth:**

$$f_{3dB,cascode} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{sig} C_{in,eff}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1 \times 10^3)(110 \times 10^{-15})} \approx 1.447\text{ GHz}$$

**Comparison with Common-Source Amplifier:**
For a CS amplifier with the same $g_m = 2\text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, and $C_{gd} = 15\text{ fF}$:

$$C_{M,CS} = 15\text{ fF} \times 11 = 165\text{ fF}$$

$$C_{in,eff,CS} = 80\text{ fF} + 165\text{ fF} = 245\text{ fF}$$

$$f_{3dB,CS} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1 \times 10^3)(245 \times 10^{-15})} \approx 649.7\text{ MHz}$$

**Conclusion:** The cascode amplifier achieves a bandwidth of **1.447 GHz** versus **649.7 MHz** for the common-source amplifier — a **2.23× improvement** in bandwidth, despite having the same DC gain. This demonstrates the power of the cascode topology in mitigating the Miller Effect.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Miller Effect Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $(1 - A_v)$:** Students frequently write $C_M = C_{gd}(1 + A_v)$ instead of $C_M = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$. Since $A_v$ is negative for a common-source amplifier, the **correct expression is $C_M = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$**. Marks lost: 1-2 per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Intrinsic $C_{gs}$:** The effective input capacitance is $C_{gs} + C_M$, **not just** $C_M$. Always sum both.
>
> 3. **Confusing the Dominant Pole Formula:** Some students use $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_L}$ (which is the **output-pole formula**) instead of $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{sig} C_{in,eff}}$ (the **input-pole formula** due to Miller multiplication). The Miller-induced input pole is almost always the dominant one.
>
> 4. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Capacitances in CMOS are typically in **femtofarads (fF)** or **picofarads (pF)**, resistances in **k$\Omega$**, and frequencies in **MHz/GHz**. Convert carefully.
>
> 5. **Miller vs. Miller Compensation:** Do not confuse the Miller Effect (a parasitic issue) with **Miller Compensation** (an intentional design technique using a feedback capacitor for stability). Both involve the same $(1 - A_v)$ factor but serve opposite purposes.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Miller Effect**

- **Definition:** The Miller Effect is the apparent multiplication of a feedback impedance by $(1 - A_v)$ at the input of an inverting amplifier.
- **Miller's Theorem Formulae:**
  - $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}$ (input equivalent)
  - $Z_2 = \dfrac{A_v Z_f}{1 - A_v}$ (output equivalent)
- **Miller Capacitance:** $C_M = C_f (1 - A_v)$
- **Common-Source Effective Input Capacitance:** $C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$
- **Dominant Pole:** $\omega_{p,dom} = \dfrac{1}{R_{sig} C_{in,eff}}$
- **-3 dB Bandwidth:** $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{sig} C_{in,eff}}$
- **Cascode Mitigation:** The cascode reduces the effective $C_{gd}$ Miller multiplication from $(1 + g_m R_D)$ to approximately $2\times$ by isolating the high-impedance output from the Miller-sensitive input device.
- **Bandwidth-Gain Trade-off:** Miller Effect creates a fundamental trade-off: increasing $g_m R_D$ (gain) decreases bandwidth, leaving the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** approximately constant.
- **Miller Compensation:** The same principle is **exploited intentionally** in two-stage op-amps to achieve dominant-pole stability.
- **Neutralization:** Cross-coupled capacitors in differential pairs can cancel the Miller Effect at a specific frequency.
- **Rule of Thumb:** If $g_m R_D > 10$, the Miller capacitance dominates $C_{in,eff}$ in a common-source amplifier.
- **Sign Convention:** For inverting amplifiers, $A_v < 0$, so $1 - A_v = 1 + \vert A_v \vert > 1$, leading to **amplification** of the feedback capacitance.
- **Exam Tip:** Always identify the feedback capacitor (typically $C_{gd}$ in CS amplifiers) and compute $C_M$ separately before summing with $C_{gs}$ to get the total $C_{in,eff}$.
- **Real-World Application Areas:** RF CMOS LNAs, two-stage op-amps, switched-capacitor filters, sample-and-hold circuits, and high-speed comparators all require careful Miller Effect analysis.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poles and Zeros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Poles and Zeros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the analysis of linear analog CMOS circuits, the **small-signal transfer function** $H(s)$ of any amplifier is a ratio of two polynomials in the complex frequency variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$. The roots of the **numerator polynomial** are called **Zeros** (frequencies at which the output vanishes for a finite, non-zero input), and the roots of the **denominator polynomial** are called **Poles** (frequencies at which the output becomes infinite for a finite, non-zero input).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEAET631 - Module 3):** Poles and zeros determine the **frequency response**, **stability**, and **transient behavior** of CMOS amplifiers. Mastering the association of poles to physical circuit nodes is essential for high-frequency analog design.

Mathematically, a generalized transfer function is expressed as:

$$H(s) = A_0 \cdot \frac{(s - z_1)(s - z_2)\cdots(s - z_m)}{(s - p_1)(s - p_2)\cdots(s - p_n)}$$

where:
- $z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_m$ are the **m zeros** of the system.
- $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ are the **n poles** of the system.
- $A_0$ is the **low-frequency (midband) gain** constant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Swing and Sink" Model

Imagine a long water channel with several **drain plugs** (poles) and **water gates** (zeros) along its length:

- **Poles = Drain Plugs:** Each plug drains energy from the system. When you pour water (signal) at a frequency that matches a plug's "drainage speed," the channel can no longer hold the water — the output "leaks" to zero, and the gain collapses. The lowest-frequency plug dictates the **dominant pole** and therefore the **upper limit of useful amplification** (the **$-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth**).

- **Zeros = Water Gates:** A gate can completely block the water from passing. At that specific frequency, the output is **zero** regardless of the input. Zeros usually arise from **feedforward paths** or the **Miller effect** in MOSFET amplifiers.

> [!NOTE]
> In the $s$-plane, **poles are marked with "$\times$"** and **zeros with "$\circ$"**. Poles in the **Left-Half Plane (LHP)** guarantee stability; poles in the **Right-Half Plane (RHP)** make the circuit oscillate uncontrollably.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Pole Frequency | $p_i$ | $\text{rad/s}$ or $\text{Hz}$ | Natural frequency of energy storage |
| Zero Frequency | $z_i$ | $\text{rad/s}$ or $\text{Hz}$ | Transmission null frequency |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| Time Constant | $\tau_i$ | $\text{seconds}$ | $\tau_i = R_i C_i$ |
| Quality Factor | $Q$ | dimensionless | Sharpness of resonance |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in CMOS Design

In modern CMOS VLSI (e.g., $65\,\text{nm}$, $28\,\text{nm}$ technology nodes), the intrinsic transistor capacitances ($C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{db}$) become comparable to or larger than the load capacitances. Consequently, **every node in a CMOS circuit introduces a pole**, and the **bandwidth of amplifiers shrinks drastically**. A circuit designer must therefore:
1. Identify pole locations to predict **gain roll-off**.
2. Identify zeros caused by feedforward capacitance ($C_{gd}$) — these can boost or distort the response.
3. Apply techniques like **Miller multiplication**, **cascode compensation**, and **source degeneration** to push poles to higher frequencies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 General Form of a Single-Stage CMOS Amplifier Transfer Function

Consider a single-stage Common-Source (CS) amplifier. Its voltage transfer function is typically a **second-order rational function**:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -G_{m0} \cdot \frac{R_L \,(1 - s C_{gd}/g_m)}{1 + s(R_L C_L) + s^2 R_L R_{gd} C_{gd} C_L}$$

For most hand-analysis in KTU exams, we approximate this as a **dominant-pole first-order system**:

$$H(s) \approx \frac{A_0}{1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_p}}$$

## 2.2 Pole-Zero Identification Rules (KTU Board Exam Favorites)

1. **Number of Poles = Number of Independent Energy-Storing Nodes** in the small-signal equivalent circuit.
2. **Number of Zeros $\leq$ Number of Capacitors** (each capacitor can contribute at most one zero).
3. **A zero occurs when the signal has two paths to the output that cancel each other** (e.g., direct feedthrough via $C_{gd}$ versus the transconductance path).
4. **Open-circuit time constant method:** Set the input source to zero and find $R_{i}$ seen by each capacitor $C_i$; then $\tau_i = R_i C_i$. Sum them all: $\tau_{tot} = \sum_i R_i C_i$. The dominant pole estimate is $\omega_{p,dom} \approx 1/\tau_{tot}$ when poles are well-separated.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Pole Derivation Logic

**Step 1:** Replace the MOSFET with its small-signal model ($g_m v_{gs}$ current source, $r_o$ output resistance).
**Step 2:** Identify all capacitors: $C_{gs}, C_{gd}, C_{db}, C_{sb}$, and load $C_L$.
**Step 3:** Apply KCL/KVL at the output node and the gate node.
**Step 4:** The denominator polynomial (characteristic equation) yields the poles.

For a CS stage with $C_{gs}$ at the input and $C_L$ at the output:

$$s^2 (R_S C_{gs} R_L C_L) + s(R_S C_{gs} + R_L C_L + g_m R_L R_S C_{gd}) + 1 = 0$$

Solving this quadratic gives the two poles. In the typical case where $g_m R_L \gg 1$ (large gain) and $C_{gs} \gg C_{gd}$, **Miller's theorem** simplifies the analysis:

$$C_{M,in} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) \approx C_{gd} \cdot g_m R_L$$

This converts the circuit to a **single-pole system** at low frequencies.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most-tested equations** for Module 3. Memorize them for the KTU End Semester Exam (ESE).

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | General Transfer Function | $H(s) = A_0 \dfrac{\prod (s - z_i)}{\prod (s - p_i)}$ | $A_0$ is the midband gain |
| 2 | $-3\text{ dB}$ Bandwidth | $\omega_{-3dB} = \omega_{p,dom}$ (for dominant pole) | Where $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = A_0/\sqrt{2}$ |
| 3 | Miller Capacitance (Input) | $C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$ | Input-side Miller multiplication |
| 4 | Miller Capacitance (Output) | $C_{M2} = C_{gd}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A_v}\right)$ | Output-side Miller multiplication |
| 5 | Open-Circuit Time Constant | $\tau_{i} = R_i C_i$ | Resistance seen by $C_i$ with input set to zero |
| 6 | Dominant Pole Estimate | $\omega_{p,dom} \approx \dfrac{1}{\sum_i R_i C_i}$ | Valid when pole magnitudes differ by $\geq 4\times$ |
| 7 | Right-Half-Plane Zero (Miller) | $z = \dfrac{g_m}{C_{gd}}$ | RHP zero in CS amplifier |
| 8 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBW} = A_0 \cdot \omega_{-3dB}$ | Constant for single-pole op-amp |
| 9 | Quality Factor (2nd order) | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{b/a}}{c/b}$ | Determines peaking in Bode plot |
| 10 | Pole of CS Amplifier (output) | $p_{out} = \dfrac{1}{R_L \,(C_L + C_{db})}$ | Dominant output pole |

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $s = j\omega$ for sinusoidal steady-state analysis.
- All poles must lie in the **left half of the $s$-plane** for **BIBO stability**.
- A pole at the **origin** ($s = 0$) represents a pure integrator (e.g., ideal op-amp open-loop).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pole-zero framework is used universally in:
- **Operational Amplifier Design:** Compensation capacitors (Miller compensation) are sized to place the dominant pole at $\sim 10\text{ Hz}$ so that the unity-gain bandwidth is below the second pole.
- **RF CMOS Front-Ends:** Low-Noise Amplifiers (LNAs) are designed with conjugate pole matching for maximum power transfer.
- **Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs):** Loop filters are designed as rational functions in $s$ to optimize phase margin.
- **Data Converters (ADC/DAC):** Sample-and-Hold circuits must have their poles above the Nyquist frequency to avoid aliasing distortion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Transfer Function of a Common-Source Amplifier with $C_{gd}$

We will derive the exact transfer function including the **Right-Half-Plane (RHP) zero** caused by $C_{gd}$.

### Circuit Setup

Consider a CS amplifier with:
- MOSFET $M_1$ with transconductance $g_m$, output resistance $r_o$.
- Load resistor $R_L$ (or equivalently, $r_o \parallel R_L$).
- Gate-to-source capacitance $C_{gs}$ (with source grounded).
- Gate-to-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ (the Miller element).
- Drain-to-body capacitance $C_{db}$ lumped with load capacitance $C_L$.

### Derivation Step 1: Write KCL at the Output Node

The output node (drain) sees:
- Current from $g_m v_{gs}$ flowing in (transconductance source).
- Current through $R_L$ flowing to ground.
- Current through $C_L$ flowing to ground.
- Current through $C_{gd}$ flowing from gate to drain.

Applying KCL at the drain node:

$$g_m v_{gs} + s C_{gd}(v_{gs} - v_{out}) = \frac{v_{out}}{R_L} + s C_L v_{out}$$

> Here, $v_{gs}$ is the small-signal gate-to-source voltage, and $v_{out}$ is the small-signal drain voltage. The current $sC_{gd}(v_{gs} - v_{out})$ flows from gate to drain, which is why it appears as a positive source on the drain side.

### Derivation Step 2: Rearrange to Isolate $v_{out}/v_{gs}$

Expand the equation:

$$g_m v_{gs} + s C_{gd} v_{gs} - s C_{gd} v_{out} = \frac{v_{out}}{R_L} + s C_L v_{out}$$

Group the $v_{gs}$ and $v_{out}$ terms:

$$(g_m + s C_{gd}) v_{gs} = \left( \frac{1}{R_L} + s C_L + s C_{gd} \right) v_{out}$$

Solve for the ratio:

$$\frac{v_{out}}{v_{gs}} = \frac{g_m + s C_{gd}}{\dfrac{1}{R_L} + s(C_L + C_{gd})}$$

> This already reveals one **zero** at $s = -g_m/C_{gd}$ (which, if we instead analyze input feedforward, becomes the well-known **RHP zero** at $s = +g_m/C_{gd}$). The **denominator** gives the **output pole**.

### Derivation Step 3: Express in Standard $H(s)$ Form

Multiply numerator and denominator by $R_L$:

$$H(s) = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{gs}} = -g_m R_L \cdot \frac{1 - s \dfrac{C_{gd}}{g_m}}{1 + s R_L (C_L + C_{gd})}$$

**Interpretation:**
- **DC Gain** $A_0 = -g_m R_L$ (negative sign indicates $180^\circ$ phase inversion).
- **Pole** at $p_1 = -\dfrac{1}{R_L (C_L + C_{gd})}$ (in LHP — stable).
- **Zero** at $z_1 = +\dfrac{g_m}{C_{gd}}$ (in **RHP** — this is problematic, see Section 4).

> [!NOTE]
> The minus sign in the numerator confirms that the zero lies in the **Right-Half Plane**. In Bode magnitude, the zero is "invisible" (it still boosts magnitude by $+20\text{ dB/dec}$), but in phase it causes a **$-90^\circ$** shift instead of $+90^\circ$. This makes the amplifier **less stable** in feedback configurations.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Open-Circuit Time Constant Method

Suppose a CMOS circuit has $n$ capacitors $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ at various nodes. To estimate the **dominant pole** without solving a full $n$-th order polynomial, we use the **Open-Circuit Time Constant (OCTC) method**.

### Step 1: Set the Input to Zero
Replace the input voltage source with a short circuit and the input current source with an open circuit.

### Step 2: For Each Capacitor, Find its "Driving-Point Resistance"
For capacitor $C_k$, find $R_{k0}$ = the resistance seen across the terminals of $C_k$ with all **other capacitors removed (open-circuited)**.

### Step 3: Compute the Time Constants
$$\tau_{k0} = R_{k0} \cdot C_k$$

### Step 4: Sum All Time Constants
$$\tau_{tot} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_{k0} C_k$$

### Step 5: Dominant Pole Estimate
$$\omega_{p,dom} \approx \frac{1}{\tau_{tot}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This estimate is **exact** for the dominant pole **only if** the poles are well-separated (i.e., $\vert p_1 \vert \ll \vert p_2 \vert \ll \cdots$). If poles are clustered, the estimate deviates.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pole-Zero Locations for Standard CMOS Stages

| Stage | Number of Poles | Pole Locations | Zero Location | Dominant Pole |
|:---|:-:|:---|:---|:---|
| **Common Source (CS)** | 2 (Gate + Drain) | $p_1 = 1/(R_{sig}C_{gs})$ at input, $p_2 = 1/(R_L C_L)$ at output | $z_1 = +g_m/C_{gd}$ (RHP) | $p_2$ (output) due to Miller effect |
| **Common Gate (CG)** | 1 | $p_1 = 1/(R_{out} C_L)$ | None | $p_1$ |
| **Source Follower (CD)** | 1 or 2 | $p_1$ at source node: $1/[R_{S}\, \vert\vert\, (1/g_m)\,(C_{gs} + C_L)]$ | May have RHP zero from $C_{gd}$ | $p_1$ |
| **Cascode (CS + CG)** | 2 | Both at high frequency (shielding reduces Miller) | RHP zero still exists | Higher than CS |

**Key Takeaway:** The **cascode** configuration is widely used in high-frequency CMOS design because the CG stage **shields** the input from the output, dramatically reducing the Miller multiplication of $C_{gd}$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A CS amplifier has $g_m = 5\,\text{mA/V}$, $R_L = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 100\,\text{fF}$, $C_{gd} = 20\,\text{fF}$, $C_L = 50\,\text{fF}$. Find the midband gain, the dominant pole, and the RHP zero.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Midband Gain:**
$$A_0 = -g_m R_L = -(5 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^3) = -10\,\text{V/V}$$

**Step 2 — Output Pole Frequency:**
$$p_1 = \frac{1}{R_L (C_L + C_{gd})} = \frac{1}{(2 \times 10^3)(50 \times 10^{-15} + 20 \times 10^{-15})}$$

$$p_1 = \frac{1}{(2000)(70 \times 10^{-15})} = \frac{1}{1.4 \times 10^{-10}} = 7.14 \times 10^9\,\text{rad/s}$$

Converting to Hz: $f_{p1} = p_1 / (2\pi) \approx 1.14\,\text{GHz}$.

**Step 3 — RHP Zero Frequency:**
$$z_1 = \frac{g_m}{C_{gd}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.5 \times 10^{11}\,\text{rad/s}$$

Converting: $f_{z1} \approx 39.8\,\text{GHz}$. This zero is far above the pole and usually does not affect the $-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth, but it **degrades phase margin** in closed-loop op-amps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 S-Plane Pole-Zero Plot (Conceptual Map)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **Left-Half-Plane (LHP)** and **Right-Half-Plane (RHP)** regions, with the locations of poles and zeros for a typical CMOS amplifier.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPlane["COMPLEX s-PLANE: sigma + j omega"]
        direction TB
        nodeOrigin["ORIGIN s = 0"]
        nodeLHP["LEFT-HALF PLANE\n sigma less than 0\n STABLE REGION"]
        nodeRHP["RIGHT-HALF PLANE\n sigma greater than 0\n UNSTABLE REGION"]
        nodeImag["IMAGINARY AXIS j omega\n marginally stable"]
    end

    subgraph PoleLocations["CMOS CS AMPLIFIER POLE-ZERO MAP"]
        pole1["x p1 = -7.14 GHz\n OUTPUT POLE\n LHP STABLE"]
        pole2["x p2 = -1.6 GHz\n INPUT POLE\n LHP STABLE"]
        zero1["o z1 = +39.8 GHz\n MILLER RHP ZERO\n CAUSES PHASE DEGRADATION"]
    end

    PoleLocations -- "lie on" --> SPlane
    nodeOrigin ~~~ nodeLHP
    nodeLHP ~~~ nodeImag
    nodeImag ~~~ nodeRHP
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The "$\times$" symbols (poles) are in the **LHP** — confirming **stability**.
- The "$\circ$" symbol (zero) is in the **RHP** — this is the Miller zero that complicates compensation.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Pole Formation in a CMOS Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput["Vin\n INPUT NODE"] --> nodeGateCap["Cgs\n GATE CAPACITANCE"]
    nodeInput --> nodeMillerCap["Cgd\n MILLER CAPACITANCE\n (FEEDFORWARD)"]
    nodeGateCap --> nodeTrans["gm Vgs\n TRANSCONDUCTANCE STAGE"]
    nodeMillerCap --> nodeTrans
    nodeTrans --> nodeOutput["Vout\n OUTPUT NODE"]
    nodeOutput --> nodeLoadCap["CL\n LOAD CAPACITANCE"]
    nodeLoadCap --> nodeGround["GROUND"]
    nodeTrans -. "POLE p1" .-> nodeOutput
    nodeGateCap -. "POLE p2" .-> nodeInput
    nodeMillerCap -. "ZERO z1 in RHP" .-> nodeTrans
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- The **input node** (gate) has capacitance $C_{gs}$, which together with the source resistance $R_{sig}$ creates the **input pole** $p_2 = 1/(R_{sig} C_{gs})$.
- The **output node** (drain) has capacitance $C_L$, which with the output resistance $R_L$ creates the **output pole** $p_1 = 1/(R_L C_L)$.
- The **Miller capacitor** $C_{gd}$ bridges input and output, creating a **feedforward path** that generates the **RHP zero**.

## 4.3 Frequency Response (Bode Plot) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BodeResponse["BODE MAGNITUDE PLOT FOR CS AMPLIFIER"]
        nodeDC["DC GAIN: 20 dB\n flat region up to p1"]
        nodeSlope1["-20 dB/dec ROLLOFF\n between p1 and z1"]
        nodeZero["GAIN LIFTS AGAIN\n at RHP zero z1"]
        nodeSlope2["FINAL SLOPE: 0 dB/dec\n gain is recovered above z1"]
    end
    nodeDC --> nodeSlope1
    nodeSlope1 --> nodeZero
    nodeZero --> nodeSlope2
```

**Important Phase Behavior:**
- Below $p_1$: phase is $-180^\circ$ (inverting amplifier).
- At $p_1$: phase decreases by another $-90^\circ \to$ total $-270^\circ$ (which means $-90^\circ$ margin from $-360^\circ$).
- At $z_1$ (RHP): phase **increases** by $+90^\circ$, recovering to $-180^\circ$ — but this is **bad for stability** because it means the phase has already crossed $-180^\circ$ before the zero can rescue it.

## 4.4 Compensation Strategy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeUncomp["UNCOMPENSATED CS AMP\n dominant pole at low frequency\n unstable when used in feedback"]
    nodeMiller["ADD MILLER COMPENSATION CAPACITATION Cc\n splits poles p1 and p2 apart\n creates new dominant pole p1_new = gm2 / Cc"]
    nodeCascode["ALTERNATIVE: CASCODE STAGE\n shields input from output\n eliminates Miller effect\n raises bandwidth by 3 to 5 times"]
    nodeNeutral["NEUTRALIZATION CAPACITORS\n Cnull = Cgd\n cancels the RHP zero by adding\n a feedforward LHP zero"]

    nodeUncomp --> nodeMiller
    nodeUncomp --> nodeCascode
    nodeUncomp --> nodeNeutral
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Neutralization** is a clever technique: by adding a capacitor of value exactly equal to $C_{gd}$ but connected in **cross-coupled** fashion, designers can cancel the RHP zero. This is widely used in **RF CMOS LNAs** and **wideband op-amps**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

### Question 1: Define poles and zeros of a transfer function. How are they identified from the $s$-plane representation?

**Model Answer:**
A **pole** is a value of the complex frequency $s$ at which the magnitude of the transfer function $H(s)$ becomes infinite (or the output is non-zero for zero input). Mathematically, poles are the roots of the denominator polynomial of $H(s)$.

A **zero** is a value of $s$ at which the transfer function $H(s)$ becomes zero, i.e., the output is zero for a non-zero input. Zeros are the roots of the numerator polynomial of $H(s)$.

In the $s$-plane, poles are denoted by "$\times$" and zeros by "$\circ$". For a system to be **stable**, all poles must lie in the **Left-Half Plane (LHP)**, i.e., their real parts must be negative.

### Question 2: What is the Miller effect, and how does it affect the pole locations in a Common-Source amplifier?

**Model Answer:**
The **Miller effect** (or Miller theorem) describes the multiplication of an impedance connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier. If a capacitor $C_{gd}$ is connected between gate and drain of a CS amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$, it appears as:

- An **input-side capacitance**: $C_{M,in} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$.
- An **output-side capacitance**: $C_{M,out} = C_{gd}(1 - 1/A_v)$.

Since $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$, the input capacitance is dramatically increased (typically by a factor of $g_m R_L$). This **lowers the input pole frequency** by the same factor, severely limiting the high-frequency response. The Miller effect is the **primary reason** CS amplifiers have low bandwidth, and it motivates the use of cascode configurations in high-speed design.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Module 3 — Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the voltage transfer function $H(s) = V_{out}/V_{in}$ of a Common-Source amplifier including the gate-to-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$. Clearly identify the pole(s) and zero, and comment on the stability implications of the zero's location. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the derived transfer function, given $g_m = 2\,\text{mA/V}$, $R_L = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 200\,\text{fF}$, $C_{gd} = 50\,\text{fF}$, and $C_L = 100\,\text{fF}$, calculate: (i) the midband gain, (ii) the dominant pole frequency in Hz, and (iii) the RHP zero frequency in Hz. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1:** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of the CS amplifier. The MOSFET is replaced by a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$ (often neglected for hand analysis). The load is $R_L$ in parallel with $C_L$, and $C_{gd}$ bridges the gate and drain.

**Step 2:** Apply KCL at the output (drain) node. The currents leaving the node are:
- Through $R_L$: $v_{out}/R_L$.
- Through $C_L$: $s C_L v_{out}$.
- Through $C_{gd}$ (from drain to gate): $s C_{gd}(v_{out} - v_{gs})$.

The currents entering from the transistor and $C_{gd}$:
- From $g_m v_{gs}$: $-g_m v_{gs}$ (note: $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source, so it leaves the drain node; we write $-g_m v_{gs}$ as a source term on the LHS).
- From $C_{gd}$ flowing into the drain from the gate: $+s C_{gd}(v_{gs} - v_{out})$.

Setting KCL (sum of currents leaving = 0):

$$-g_m v_{gs} - s C_{gd}(v_{gs} - v_{out}) + \frac{v_{out}}{R_L} + s C_L v_{out} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Group the $v_{out}$ terms:

$$(g_m + s C_{gd}) v_{gs} = \left( \frac{1}{R_L} + s C_L + s C_{gd} \right) v_{out}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the transfer function:

$$H(s) = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{gs}} = \frac{g_m + s C_{gd}}{\dfrac{1}{R_L} + s(C_L + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $R_L$ and rearrange:

$$H(s) = -g_m R_L \cdot \frac{1 - s \dfrac{C_{gd}}{g_m}}{1 + s R_L (C_L + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 6: Identification of poles and zeros:**
- **Midband gain:** $A_0 = -g_m R_L$ (negative = inverting).
- **Pole:** $p_1 = -\dfrac{1}{R_L (C_L + C_{gd})}$ — located in the **LHP** (stable).
- **Zero:** $z_1 = +\dfrac{g_m}{C_{gd}}$ — located in the **RHP** (the negative sign in the numerator $1 - s C_{gd}/g_m$ indicates a zero at $s = +g_m/C_{gd}$).

**Stability comment:** Although the pole is in the LHP (which means the open-loop amplifier is stable), the **RHP zero** introduces a $-90^\circ$ phase shift that **degrades the phase margin** when the amplifier is placed in a feedback loop. This is the central reason compensation capacitors are required in two-stage op-amp design.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given:** $g_m = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A/V}$, $R_L = 5 \times 10^3\,\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 200 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}$, $C_{gd} = 50 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}$, $C_L = 100 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}$.

**(i) Midband gain:**
$$A_0 = -g_m R_L = -(2 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^3) = -10\,\text{V/V}$$

In decibels: $20 \log_{10}(10) = 20\,\text{dB}$.

**(ii) Dominant pole frequency:**

$$p_1 = \frac{1}{R_L (C_L + C_{gd})} = \frac{1}{(5 \times 10^3)(100 \times 10^{-15} + 50 \times 10^{-15})}$$

$$p_1 = \frac{1}{(5 \times 10^3)(150 \times 10^{-15})} = \frac{1}{7.5 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.33 \times 10^9\,\text{rad/s}$$

Converting to Hz:
$$f_{p1} = \frac{p_1}{2\pi} = \frac{1.33 \times 10^9}{2\pi} \approx 212.2\,\text{MHz}$$

**(iii) RHP zero frequency:**

$$z_1 = \frac{g_m}{C_{gd}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-15}} = 4 \times 10^{10}\,\text{rad/s}$$

Converting to Hz:
$$f_{z1} = \frac{4 \times 10^{10}}{2\pi} \approx 6.37\,\text{GHz}$$

**Observation:** The zero is approximately **30 times** higher than the pole, so the $-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth is dominated by $p_1 \approx 212\,\text{MHz}$, but the RHP zero will still cause **phase erosion** well within the unity-gain bandwidth of typical feedback configurations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Open-Circuit Time Constant (OCTC) method** for estimating the dominant pole of a multi-pole CMOS amplifier. State the conditions under which the method gives an accurate estimate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS cascode amplifier has the following nodes with associated capacitances and open-circuit driving-point resistances:
- $C_1 = 50\,\text{fF}$, $R_1 = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$ (gate of input transistor)
- $C_2 = 30\,\text{fF}$, $R_2 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$ (drain of input transistor / source of cascode)
- $C_3 = 80\,\text{fF}$, $R_3 = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$ (drain of cascode / output node)

Use the OCTC method to estimate the dominant pole frequency and the corresponding $-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Explanation of OCTC Method

The **Open-Circuit Time Constant (OCTC) method** is a **simplified hand-analysis technique** for finding the dominant pole of an $n$-th order CMOS amplifier without solving an $n$-th order characteristic equation.

**Procedure:**

1. Replace the input source with its internal impedance (short for voltage source, open for current source).
2. For each capacitor $C_k$ in the circuit, find the **driving-point resistance** $R_{k0}$ = the resistance seen across the terminals of $C_k$ with **all other capacitors open-circuited** (since the OCTC method implicitly assumes that the other capacitors are at DC for resistance calculation).
3. Compute the time constant $\tau_{k0} = R_{k0} C_k$.
4. Sum all time constants: $\tau_{tot} = \sum_{k=1}^n R_{k0} C_k$.
5. The dominant pole is estimated as $\omega_{p,dom} \approx 1/\tau_{tot}$.

**Conditions for accuracy:**

- The poles must be **well-separated** in frequency: $\vert p_1 \vert \ll \vert p_2 \vert \ll \cdots \ll \vert p_n \vert$.
- A common rule of thumb is that adjacent pole ratios should be at least **$4\times$** for the estimate to be within ~10% of the true dominant pole.
- The method works best when one capacitor is much larger than the others (which defines the dominant pole).

**Advantage:** The OCTC method gives a very good estimate using only simple **resistance calculations** (no complex algebra), making it ideal for quick hand-analysis in board examinations.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Application

**Given:**
- $\tau_1 = R_1 C_1 = (4 \times 10^3)(50 \times 10^{-15}) = 2.0 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s}$
- $\tau_2 = R_2 C_2 = (2 \times 10^3)(30 \times 10^{-15}) = 6.0 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{s}$
- $\tau_3 = R_3 C_3 = (1.5 \times 10^3)(80 \times 10^{-15}) = 1.2 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s}$

**Step 1:** Sum the time constants:
$$\tau_{tot} = \tau_1 + \tau_2 + \tau_3 = 2.0 \times 10^{-10} + 6.0 \times 10^{-11} + 1.2 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\tau_{tot} = 3.8 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Dominant pole frequency:
$$\omega_{p,dom} = \frac{1}{\tau_{tot}} = \frac{1}{3.8 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 2.63 \times 10^9\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to Hz for the $-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth:
$$f_{-3dB} = \frac{\omega_{p,dom}}{2\pi} = \frac{2.63 \times 10^9}{2\pi} \approx 419\,\text{MHz}$$

**Interpretation:** Despite three internal nodes contributing poles, the dominant pole of this cascode amplifier is at approximately **$419\,\text{MHz}$**, which is significantly higher than a comparable CS amplifier (typically $200\text{–}300\,\text{MHz}$) — this validates the **cascode's bandwidth advantage** in CMOS design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign of the RHP zero:** Students often write the zero as $z = -g_m/C_{gd}$ (LHP). It is in the **RHP** because the numerator of $H(s)$ contains $(1 - s C_{gd}/g_m)$, giving $s = +g_m/C_{gd}$. Mistake costs **2 marks** typically.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in the gain:** The CS amplifier is inverting. Writing $A_0 = +g_m R_L$ loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Mixing $\text{rad/s}$ and $\text{Hz}$. Always convert using $\omega = 2\pi f$ and **state both** in the answer.
> 4. **OCTC misapplication:** Students sometimes forget to **open-circuit other capacitors** when calculating each $R_{k0}$. The method requires sequential analysis, not parallel.
> 5. **Skipping the stability comment:** Even if the derivation is correct, failing to comment on the **RHP zero's impact on phase margin** loses 1–2 marks in the "comment" sub-part.
> 6. **Confusing pole and zero notation:** "$\times$" = pole, "$\circ$" = zero. Reversing them in the $s$-plane sketch is an instant **1-mark deduction**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Definition Recap:**
  - Pole = root of denominator of $H(s)$; output diverges.
  - Zero = root of numerator of $H(s)$; output vanishes.
  - LHP poles = stable; RHP poles = unstable.

- **Miller Effect Recap:**
  - Input side: $C_{M,in} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$.
  - Causes **drastic bandwidth reduction** in CS amplifiers.
  - Mitigation: **cascode**, **neutralization**, **Miller compensation**.

- **RHP Zero (Miller Zero):**
  - Location: $z = +g_m/C_{gd}$ — always in RHP for CS stage.
  - Effect: phase degradation, instability in feedback.
  - Cancellation: add a **feedforward LHP zero** via a nulling resistor or capacitor.

- **OCTC Method:**
  - $\tau_{tot} = \sum R_{k0} C_k$.
  - $\omega_{p,dom} \approx 1/\tau_{tot}$.
  - Accurate only for **well-separated** poles.

- **Stage Comparison (One-Glance Summary):**
  - CS: high gain, low bandwidth, RHP zero → bad for high speed.
  - CG: low input impedance, no Miller effect, high bandwidth → used in cascode.
  - CD (Source Follower): unity gain, level-shifter, moderate bandwidth.
  - Cascode: high gain **and** high bandwidth (best of both worlds).

- **Bode Plot Slopes to Remember:**
  - Single pole: $-20\,\text{dB/dec}$ slope, $-90^\circ$ phase shift.
  - Two poles: $-40\,\text{dB/dec}$, $-180^\circ$ phase.
  - RHP zero: magnitude rises $+20\,\text{dB/dec}$ but phase drops $-90^\circ$ (opposite of LHP zero).

- **Conversion Formulas:**
  - $f (\text{Hz}) = \omega (\text{rad/s}) / (2\pi)$.
  - $\omega_{-3dB} \approx 1/\tau$ for single-pole systems.

- **Engineering Intuition Rule of Thumb:** For a CS amplifier, the **Gain-Bandwidth Product** $\text{GBW} = g_m / (2\pi C_L)$ is approximately **constant** regardless of $R_L$. To increase bandwidth, **increase $g_m$** (larger $W/L$) or **reduce $C_L$**.

- **Syllabus Alignment:** This topic is a **mandatory 14-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 3 (PEAET631). The OCTC method, Miller theorem, and pole-zero identification of CS/CG/CD stages are the **three most-tested sub-topics**. Practice at least **three numerical problems** before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Response Analysis of Common Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Response Analysis of Common Source

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Frequency Response Analysis of a Common Source (CS) amplifier** is the rigorous analytical study of the gain–frequency behavior of the most fundamental single-transistor CMOS amplifier stage. It quantifies how the magnitude and phase of the voltage gain $A_v(s)$ deviate from the ideal midband (DC) value $A_{v0}$ as the operating frequency increases, leading to the identification of **poles**, **zeros**, and the **3-dB cutoff frequency ($\omega_H$ or $f_H$)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is typically examined with a strong focus on:

- The **intrinsic parasitic capacitances** of the MOSFET: $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{db}$, $C_{sb}$.
- The application of **Miller's Theorem** to simplify the feedback capacitor $C_{gd}$.
- The **Open-Circuit Time Constant (OCTC)** and **Short-Circuit Time Constant (SCTC)** methods for estimating the dominant pole without solving the full network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to identify the poles of a CS stage using both **Miller's approximation** and the **OCTC method**, and compare the two for engineering accuracy.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Pipe & Bucket Model"

Imagine your CS amplifier as a **water delivery system**:

- The **input signal** is water flowing into a small bucket (representing the **gate capacitance** $C_{gs}$).
- The **output node** is a much larger tank (representing $C_L$ at the drain).
- A **thin pipe** connects the two buckets — this is the **feedback capacitor** $C_{gd}$ (Miller path).
- The water faucet (input source) has a fixed flow rate determined by the source resistance $R_S$.

When you pour water slowly (low frequency), both buckets have time to fill and empty together — the output faithfully follows the input (the **midband region**). As you pour faster (high frequency), the small bucket at the gate cannot fill instantaneously, and worse, the connecting pipe "steals" water backward through $C_{gd}$, dragging down the high-frequency response. Eventually, the system simply cannot keep up — this is the **cutoff frequency $\omega_H$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Cutoff Frequency:** The frequency at which the magnitude of the gain drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (or $-3$ dB) of its midband value. Beyond $\omega_H$, the amplifier is considered **non-functional** for that design's specification.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the standard metrics every KTU paper assumes you know by heart:

- **Threshold Frequency ($f_T$):** The unity-current-gain frequency of the transistor alone.
- **Transition Frequency ($f_T$ or $\omega_T$):** $\omega_T = g_m / C_{gs}$.
- **Miller Multiplier Factor:** $(1 - A_v)$ for the input side, $(1 - 1/A_v)$ for the output side.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Frequency Response Degrade in a CS Stage?

A CS amplifier contains **parasitic capacitances** that are physical and unavoidable. These are:

1. **Gate-to-Source Capacitance ($C_{gs}$):** Caused by the gate oxide over the channel.
2. **Gate-to-Drain Capacitance ($C_{gd}$):** Caused by the gate-drain overlap and the depletion region under the channel. This is the most troublesome because it bridges input and output.
3. **Source-to-Body Capacitance ($C_{sb}$):** Often tied to AC ground via the source.
4. **Drain-to-Body Capacitance ($C_{db}$):** Adds directly to the output load.

At low frequencies, the impedance $1/(j\omega C)$ of these capacitors is enormous — they act as **open circuits** and are effectively invisible. At high frequencies, their impedance becomes small, and they begin to **short-circuit the signal**, robbing the amplifier of its gain.

## 2.2 The Two Operating Regions of an Amplifier

| Region | Frequency Range | Behavior | Gain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DC / Midband** | $0 \le \omega \ll \omega_H$ | Capacitors are open; circuit is purely resistive. | $A_{v0} = -g_m R_D$ |
| **High Frequency** | $\omega \gg \omega_H$ | Capacitors short-circuit internal nodes. | $\vert A_v(\omega) \vert \to 0$ |
| **Transition** | $\omega \approx \omega_H$ | Roll-off begins; phase shifts accumulate. | $A_{v0} / \sqrt{2}$ at $\omega_H$ |

## 2.3 Miller's Theorem — The Cornerstone of CS Analysis

Because $C_{gd}$ connects the input node to the output node (a feedback path), it is **inconvenient to analyze directly**. Miller's Theorem transforms it into two grounded capacitors:

For a voltage gain $A_v$ from the side of $C_{gd}$ where the original is referenced:

$$C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) \quad \text{(referred to the input)}$$

$$C_{M2} = C_{gd}\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right) \quad \text{(referred to the output)}$$

Since $A_v$ is negative for a CS stage, the **input-side Miller capacitance is greatly amplified** — this is the famous **Miller Effect** and is the dominant cause of high-frequency gain roll-off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** The Miller Multiplier $(1 - A_v)$ for a CS stage becomes approximately $(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \approx g_m R_D$ for moderate gains. So a tiny $C_{gd} \approx 1\text{ fF}$ can appear as a $100\text{ fF}$ capacitor at the input if $g_m R_D = 100$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Midband Gain | $A_{v0} = -g_m R_D$ | CS stage with resistive load | V/V |
| 2 | Miller Input Cap | $C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v0} \vert)$ | Referred to gate | F |
| 3 | Miller Output Cap | $C_{M2} = C_{gd}(1 + 1/\vert A_{v0} \vert)$ | Referred to drain | F |
| 4 | Input Pole | $\omega_{p,in} = \dfrac{1}{R_S\,(C_{gs} + C_{M1})}$ | Dominant input pole | rad/s |
| 5 | Output Pole | $\omega_{p,out} = \dfrac{1}{R_D\,(C_{db} + C_{L} + C_{M2})}$ | Pole at drain node | rad/s |
| 6 | Dominant Pole (OCTC) | $\omega_H \approx 1 / \sum_i \tau_{i,oc}$ | Sum of all OC time constants | rad/s |
| 7 | OCTC at Input | $\tau_{in,oc} = R_S (C_{gs} + C_{gd})$ | Time constant at gate | s |
| 8 | OCTC at Output | $\tau_{out,oc} = R_D (C_{db} + C_L + C_{gd})$ | Time constant at drain | s |
| 9 | Transition Freq. | $\omega_T = g_m / C_{gs}$ | Transistor intrinsic figure of merit | rad/s |
| 10 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBW} = A_{v0} \cdot \omega_H$ | Design metric | rad/s |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Op-amp design:** The CS stage is the heart of every CMOS op-amp's gain stage. Frequency response analysis determines whether the op-amp remains stable when cascaded.
- **RF Front-Ends:** In radio receivers, the CS stage's $\omega_H$ defines the usable bandwidth before signal loss.
- **High-Speed ADCs:** Determines the maximum sampling rate achievable.
- **Data Converters:** Limits the slew rate and settling time in switched-capacitor circuits.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function of CS Amplifier Using Miller's Theorem

### Step 1 — Define the Midband Gain

For a CS amplifier with resistive load $R_D$ (ignoring body effect for clarity):

$$A_{v0} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}}\bigg|_{s=0} = -g_m R_D$$

This is the **DC / midband voltage gain** (negative sign indicates 180° phase inversion).

### Step 2 — Apply Miller's Theorem to $C_{gd}$

We split the bridging capacitor $C_{gd}$ into two grounded capacitors using the midband gain $A_{v0}$ (valid because the gain is approximately constant near $\omega_H$ for well-designed stages):

$$C_{gd,input} = C_{gd}(1 - A_{v0}) = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

$$C_{gd,output} = C_{gd}\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_{v0}}\right) = C_{gd}\left(1 + \frac{1}{g_m R_D}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Equivalent Capacitance at Each Node

At the **input (gate) node**, the total capacitance to AC ground is:

$$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_{gd,input} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

At the **output (drain) node**, the total capacitance to AC ground is:

$$C_{out,total} = C_{db} + C_L + C_{gd,output} = C_{db} + C_L + C_{gd}\left(1 + \frac{1}{g_m R_D}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply KCL at Each Node (Nodal Analysis)

**At the input node** (driven by source $v_s$ with source resistance $R_S$):

$$\frac{v_{gs} - v_s}{R_S} + v_{gs} \cdot sC_{in,total} = 0$$

Solving for $v_{gs}$:

$$v_{gs} = \frac{v_s}{1 + s R_S C_{in,total}}$$

**At the output node** (KCL: current from $g_m v_{gs}$ flows into $R_D \parallel C_{out,total}$):

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot \frac{R_D}{1 + s R_D C_{out,total}}$$

### Step 5 — Combine to Get the Transfer Function

$$A_v(s) = \frac{v_{out}}{v_s} = \frac{-g_m R_D}{(1 + s R_S C_{in,total})(1 + s R_D C_{out,total})}$$

Substituting $A_{v0} = -g_m R_D$:

$$\boxed{\,A_v(s) = \frac{A_{v0}}{\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_{p,in}}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_{p,out}}\right)}\,}$$

where:

$$\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{R_S\,[C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)]} \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_{p,out} = \frac{1}{R_D\,[C_{db} + C_L + C_{gd}(1 + \tfrac{1}{g_m R_D})]}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate the 3-dB Frequency

When the two poles are widely separated, the **dominant pole approximation** gives:

$$\omega_H \approx \min(\omega_{p,in},\, \omega_{p,out})$$

If they are close (within a factor of 4), the exact expression is:
