$$\omega_H = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\sqrt{\omega_{p,in}^2 + \omega_{p,out}^2 - \text{(correction terms)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The dominant pole is almost always the **input pole** because Miller multiplication inflates $C_{gd}$ dramatically. In high-output-impedance designs (current source load), however, the output pole can become dominant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Open-Circuit Time Constant (OCTC) Method

The OCTC method is the **KTU-favorite** because it gives a more accurate estimate of $\omega_H$ for two-pole systems without requiring pole-splitting analysis.

### Step 1 — General Principle

For a network with multiple capacitors, the dominant time constant is:

$$\tau_H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{i,oc} C_i$$

where $R_{i,oc}$ is the **resistance seen at the terminals of capacitor $C_i$** when all **other capacitors are replaced by open circuits**.

Then:

$$\omega_H = \frac{1}{\tau_H} = \frac{1}{\sum_i R_{i,oc} C_i}$$

### Step 2 — Apply to CS Amplifier

**Capacitor 1: $C_{gs}$** (between gate and source)
- Open $C_{gd}$, $C_{db}$.
- The resistance seen by $C_{gs}$ is $R_S$ (source resistance) since the source is AC ground.
- Therefore: $R_{1,oc} = R_S$

$$\tau_1 = R_S \cdot C_{gs}$$

**Capacitor 2: $C_{gd}$** (between gate and drain)
- Open $C_{gs}$, $C_{db}$.
- Looking into the gate node from $C_{gd}$: we see $R_S$.
- Looking into the drain node from $C_{gd}$: we see $R_D$.
- Since the two terminals of $C_{gd}$ are in different sub-circuits, the total resistance is the sum: $R_{2,oc} = R_S + R_D$.

$$\tau_2 = (R_S + R_D) \cdot C_{gd}$$

**Capacitor 3: $C_{db}$** (drain to body, body = AC ground)
- The resistance seen is simply $R_D$ (load resistance at the drain).

$$\tau_3 = R_D \cdot C_{db}$$

### Step 3 — Sum the Time Constants

$$\tau_H = R_S C_{gs} + (R_S + R_D) C_{gd} + R_D C_{db}$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\omega_H$

$$\boxed{\,\omega_H = \frac{1}{R_S C_{gs} + (R_S + R_D) C_{gd} + R_D C_{db}}\,}$$

### Step 5 — Comparison with Miller Method

| Aspect | Miller Approximation | OCTC Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Accuracy** | Approximate (assumes $A_v$ is constant) | More accurate (exact dominant pole) |
| **Best for** | Quick design estimates | Final design / KTU exam rigor |
| **Number of poles** | Two explicit poles | One effective dominant pole |
| **KTU Preference** | Often required to show both | Used as verification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Tip:** If $R_S \ll R_D$ (typical in CMOS), the term $(R_S + R_D) C_{gd} \approx R_D C_{gd}$ dominates $\tau_H$, which means **$C_{gd}$ is the bandwidth bottleneck** in most practical CS stages.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** A CS amplifier with $g_m = 2\text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 50\text{ fF}$, $C_{gd} = 10\text{ fF}$, $C_{db} = 20\text{ fF}$, $C_L = 0$.

**Required:** (a) Midband gain, (b) Poles using Miller, (c) $\omega_H$ using OCTC, (d) GBW.

### (a) Midband Gain

$$A_{v0} = -g_m R_D = -(2\times 10^{-3})(5\times 10^3) = -10\text{ V/V}$$

### (b) Poles Using Miller's Method

$$C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v0} \vert) = 10\text{ fF} \times 11 = 110\text{ fF}$$

$$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_{M1} = 50 + 110 = 160\text{ fF}$$

$$\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{R_S C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{(10^3)(160\times 10^{-15})} = 6.25\text{ Grad/s}$$

$$C_{M2} = C_{gd}\left(1 + \frac{1}{\vert A_{v0} \vert}\right) = 10 \times 1.1 = 11\text{ fF}$$

$$C_{out,total} = C_{db} + C_{L} + C_{M2} = 20 + 0 + 11 = 31\text{ fF}$$

$$\omega_{p,out} = \frac{1}{R_D C_{out,total}} = \frac{1}{(5\times 10^3)(31\times 10^{-15})} = 6.45\text{ Grad/s}$$

### (c) $\omega_H$ Using OCTC Method

$$\tau_H = R_S C_{gs} + (R_S + R_D) C_{gd} + R_D C_{db}$$

$$\tau_H = (10^3)(50\text{f}) + (6\times 10^3)(10\text{f}) + (5\times 10^3)(20\text{f})$$

$$\tau_H = 50\text{ ps} + 60\text{ ps} + 100\text{ ps} = 210\text{ ps}$$

$$\omega_H = \frac{1}{210\text{ ps}} = 4.76\text{ Grad/s} \quad (f_H \approx 758\text{ MHz})$$

### (d) Gain-Bandwidth Product

$$\text{GBW} = \vert A_{v0} \vert \cdot \omega_H = 10 \times 4.76\text{ Grad/s} = 47.6\text{ Grad/s}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{GBW} = \frac{g_m}{2 C_{gd}} = \frac{2\text{ mA/V}}{2 \times 10\text{ fF}} = 100\text{ Grad/s} \quad \text{(theoretical maximum)}$$

> [!TIP]
> The discrepancy between the two GBWs comes from finite $R_S$ and $C_{db}$ — to approach the theoretical GBW, you must minimize the source resistance and use a current source load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CS Amplifier Frequency Response Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Voltage Source Vs with Source Resistance Rs] --> B[Gate Node G]
    B --> C1[Parasitic Capacitance Cgs Gate to Source]
    B --> C2[Parasitic Capacitance Cgd Gate to Drain Miller Path]
    C1 --> D[Source Node AC Ground]
    C2 --> E[Drain Node D]
    E --> F[Output Node Vout]
    E --> C3[Parasitic Capacitance Cdb Drain to Body]
    E --> C4[Load Capacitance CL]
    F --> G[Load Resistance RD to VDD]
    H[Transconductance gm x Vgs Controlled Current Source] --> E
    B --> H
    I[Apply Miller Theorem] --> J[CM1 equals Cgd times one plus Av0 Referred to Input]
    I --> K[CM2 equals Cgd times one plus one over Av0 Referred to Output]
    J --> L[Input Pole omega p in]
    K --> M[Output Pole omega p out]
    L --> N[Transfer Function Av of s]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Three dB Frequency omega H via OCTC]
    N --> P[Gain Bandwidth Product GBW]
```

## 4.2 Pole-Zero Plot Description (Conceptual Bode Plot)

```
       |A_v| (dB)
        |
   20 dB|______________________________  Midband Gain (A_v0)
        |                              \
        |                               \
   17 dB| - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -\● ← 3 dB point (ω_H)
        |                                \ \
        |                                 \ \  -20 dB/decade
        |                                  \ \  (one pole)
        |                                   \ \
   -∞   |                                    \_\______
        |                                     \       \____
        |                                                  
        0          ω_H                               ω_T
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Small-Signal Model (Miller-Transformed)

```
   Rs                          R_D
    |                           |
  o--/\/\---+---------o----/\/\--o
  Vs        |         |         |
            |       [gmVgs]      |
            |         |          |
            o         o----------o  Vout
            |         |
          [Cgs+CM1]  [Cdb+CL+CM2]
            |         |
           GND       GND
```

This is the **decoupled equivalent circuit** after applying Miller's Theorem. The input and output nodes are now **independent**, and each node has a single-pole RC time constant. This simplification is the foundation of the **dominant-pole approximation** used in every CMOS design textbook.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C1`) to avoid any reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Miller's Theorem and its significance in CS amplifier frequency analysis. *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

**Model Answer:**

Miller's Theorem states that an impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes with voltage gain $A_v$ between them can be replaced by two impedances: $Z_1 = Z/(1 - A_v)$ to ground at the input node and $Z_2 = Z(1 - 1/A_v)$ to ground at the output node.

**Significance in CS amplifier:**
- It transforms the **gate-drain feedback capacitance** $C_{gd}$ into two grounded capacitors.
- The input-side equivalent capacitance is amplified by a factor of $(1 + \vert A_{v0} \vert)$, causing a dramatic reduction in the input pole frequency.
- Without Miller's Theorem, the transfer function of a CS stage would require solving a complex two-node simultaneous equation; with it, the circuit becomes a simple cascade of two first-order RC low-pass filters.

### Question 2: What is the Open-Circuit Time Constant (OCTC) method, and why is it used for CS amplifier analysis? *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer:**

The OCTC method is a technique to estimate the **dominant 3-dB frequency** of an amplifier by computing the sum of time constants formed by each capacitor and the **resistance seen at its terminals** when all other capacitors are opened.

**Reasons for use in CS amplifier:**
1. The CS stage has multiple parasitic capacitors ($C_{gs}, C_{gd}, C_{db}$), each contributing a pole. Direct pole calculation is tedious.
2. When the poles are not widely separated, the dominant-pole approximation fails. OCTC gives a more accurate estimate of $\omega_H$ without requiring full pole-zero analysis.
3. It provides excellent physical insight — the **largest $\tau$** identifies the **bandwidth-limiting node**.

The formula is:

$$\omega_H \approx \frac{1}{\sum_i R_{i,oc} C_i}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Full Analysis of CS Amplifier Frequency Response

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**A CS amplifier has the following parameters:** $g_m = 1\text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 500\text{ }\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 100\text{ fF}$, $C_{gd} = 20\text{ fF}$, $C_{db} = 50\text{ fF}$, $C_L = 200\text{ fF}$.

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Calculate the midband gain, the two poles using Miller's Theorem, and the 3-dB frequency assuming dominant-pole approximation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Midband Gain:**

$$A_{v0} = -g_m R_D = -(1\text{ mA/V})(10\text{ k}\Omega) = -10\text{ V/V}$$

**Step 2 — Miller Capacitances:**

$$C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v0} \vert) = 20\text{ fF} \times 11 = 220\text{ fF}$$

$$C_{M2} = C_{gd}\left(1 + \frac{1}{\vert A_{v0} \vert}\right) = 20 \times 1.1 = 22\text{ fF}$$

**Step 3 — Input Pole:**

$$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_{M1} = 100 + 220 = 320\text{ fF}$$

$$\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{R_S C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{(500)(320\times 10^{-15})} = 6.25\text{ Grad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Output Pole:**

$$C_{out,total} = C_{db} + C_L + C_{M2} = 50 + 200 + 22 = 272\text{ fF}$$

$$\omega_{p,out} = \frac{1}{R_D C_{out,total}} = \frac{1}{(10^4)(272\times 10^{-15})} = 367.6\text{ Mrad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Dominant Pole (Input is clearly lower):**

$$\omega_H \approx \omega_{p,in} = 6.25\text{ Grad/s} \quad (f_H \approx 995\text{ MHz})$$

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Calculate $\omega_H$ using the OCTC method and compare with the Miller result.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time Constant for $C_{gs}$:**

$$\tau_1 = R_S \cdot C_{gs} = (500)(100\text{ fF}) = 50\text{ ps}$$

**Step 2 — Time Constant for $C_{gd}$:**

$$\tau_2 = (R_S + R_D) \cdot C_{gd} = (500 + 10{,}000)(20\text{ fF}) = 210\text{ ps}$$

**Step 3 — Time Constant for $C_{db}$:**

$$\tau_3 = R_D \cdot C_{db} = (10{,}000)(50\text{ fF}) = 500\text{ ps}$$

**Step 4 — Sum and Invert:**

$$\tau_H = 50 + 210 + 500 = 760\text{ ps}$$

$$\omega_H = \frac{1}{760\text{ ps}} = 1.316\text{ Grad/s} \quad (f_H \approx 209.4\text{ MHz})$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Discussion:**

| Method | $\omega_H$ (Grad/s) | $f_H$ (MHz) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Miller (Dominant) | 6.25 | 995 |
| OCTC | 1.316 | 209.4 |

The OCTC method yields a **lower** $\omega_H$ because it accurately accounts for the cumulative effect of all three capacitors. The Miller dominant-pole method is **optimistic** because the input and output poles (6.25 Grad/s vs 0.368 Grad/s) are not widely separated — they are within a factor of 17, which is borderline for dominant-pole validity. In practice, **OCTC is the more reliable** estimate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice — Frequency Response of CS with Current Source Load

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**A CS amplifier is loaded by an ideal PMOS current source with output resistance $R_{out} = 100\text{ k}\Omega$.** The NMOS driver has $g_m = 500\text{ }\mu\text{A/V}$, $C_{gs} = 30\text{ fF}$, $C_{gd} = 5\text{ fF}$, $C_{db} = 10\text{ fF}$, $C_{L,ext} = 500\text{ fF}$, $R_S = 1\text{ k}\Omega$.

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Derive the transfer function and identify the dominant pole.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Midband Gain:**

$$A_{v0} = -g_m R_{out} = -(500\text{ }\mu\text{A/V})(100\text{ k}\Omega) = -50\text{ V/V}$$

**Step 2 — Miller Capacitances:**

$$C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 + 50) = 5 \times 51 = 255\text{ fF}$$

$$C_{M2} = C_{gd}\left(1 + \frac{1}{50}\right) = 5 \times 1.02 = 5.1\text{ fF}$$

**Step 3 — Input Pole:**

$$C_{in,total} = 30 + 255 = 285\text{ fF}$$

$$\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{(10^3)(285\text{ fF})} = 3.51\text{ Grad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Output Pole (note: $R_{out}$ is now huge!):**

$$C_{out,total} = C_{db} + C_{L,ext} + C_{M2} = 10 + 500 + 5.1 = 515.1\text{ fF}$$

$$\omega_{p,out} = \frac{1}{(10^5)(515.1\text{ fF})} = 19.4\text{ Mrad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Dominant Pole Identification:**

In this case, **$\omega_{p,out} \ll \omega_{p,in}$**, so the **output pole is dominant**! This is a critical insight: with a high-impedance current source load, the output node — not the input — limits the bandwidth.

$$\boxed{\,\omega_H \approx \omega_{p,out} = 19.4\text{ Mrad/s}\,}$$

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Compute GBW and discuss strategies to improve bandwidth.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — GBW:**

$$\text{GBW} = \vert A_{v0} \vert \cdot \omega_H = 50 \times 19.4\text{ Mrad/s} = 970\text{ Mrad/s} \approx 154\text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Strategies to Improve Bandwidth:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reduce $C_L$** | Direct pole boost | Limited by parasitic routing |
| **Cascode Transistor** | Reduces Miller effect by lowering gain seen by $C_{gd}$ | Reduces voltage headroom |
| **Use a buffer (source follower)** | Drives large $C_L$ without loading gain stage | Adds current draw |
| **Reduce $R_S$** | Raises input pole (only useful if input is dominant) | Requires stronger driver stage |
| **Inductive peaking** | Adds zero to cancel pole | Area cost, frequency-specific |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $R_D$ in the output pole formula when the load is a current source — **always use $R_{out}$ of the load, not the bias resistor**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that **Miller's Theorem uses the midband gain** $A_{v0}$, not the frequency-dependent gain. This is only accurate if the poles are well-separated.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In OCTC, the resistance seen by $C_{gd}$ is **$R_S + R_D$** (or $R_S + R_{out}$), **not** $R_S \times R_D / (R_S + R_D)$. This is because the two terminals of $C_{gd}$ are in separate sub-circuits.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting the units! $1\text{ fF} = 10^{-15}\text{ F}$. A direct substitution in Farads will give numerical answers off by 15 orders of magnitude.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Miller's Theorem:** $C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$ at the input; $C_{M2} = C_{gd}(1 - 1/A_v)$ at the output.
- **Midband Gain of CS:** $A_{v0} = -g_m R_D$ (or $-g_m R_{out}$ for current source load).
- **Input Pole** is dominant for **low-impedance output loads** ($R_D$ small).
- **Output Pole** is dominant for **high-impedance current source loads** (cascode, gain-boosted stages).
- **OCTC formula** for CS stage: $\tau_H = R_S C_{gs} + (R_S + R_{out}) C_{gd} + R_{out} C_{db}$ — and $\omega_H = 1/\tau_H$.
- **Transition Frequency:** $\omega_T = g_m / C_{gs}$ is the **intrinsic speed limit** of the transistor.
- **GBW Maximization:** Minimize $C_{gd}$ (use minimum-length devices cautiously), use cascode to suppress Miller, and keep $R_S$ as small as possible.
- **Zero in CS amplifier:** The CS stage is **minimum-phase** (no right-half-plane zero) for resistive loads; zeros only appear with inductive or transmission-line loads — typically not in basic KTU problems.
- **Bode Plot Slope:** A single-pole system rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** past $\omega_H$; a two-pole system rolls off at **−40 dB/decade** past the higher pole.
- **Phase Margin Implication:** Two closely-spaced poles degrade phase margin; this is the bridge to **op-amp stability** in Module 4.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source Follower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Source Follower

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Source Follower (SF)** — also termed the **Common-Drain (CD) amplifier** — is a single-transistor MOSFET amplifier configuration in which:

- The **input signal** is applied at the **Gate (G)** terminal.
- The **output signal** is taken from the **Source (S)** terminal.
- The **Drain (D)** is tied directly to the AC ground (i.e., to $V_{DD}$ for an NMOS device).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Because the Gate of a MOSFET draws essentially **zero DC current**, the Source Follower is invariably preceded by a **biasing network** (a current source $I_{BIAS}$ or a large drain-to-source resistor) to establish the quiescent operating point. Without biasing, the transistor simply turns OFF for $V_{IN} < V_{TH}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention:** The circuit is called a *follower* because the small-signal source voltage $v_s$ "follows" the gate voltage $v_g$ with a gain slightly less than unity. In other words, $v_{out}(t) \approx v_{in}(t)$, just attenuated by a factor $\approx g_m R_S / (1 + g_m R_S)$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of a **Source Follower like a voltage buffer on a water pipeline**:

- Imagine a tap (the **Gate**) that you can wiggle slightly with your hand to control water flow.
- The water level in a floating cup attached to the tap (the **Source**) rises and falls *in sync* with your hand movement, but slightly **lagging** and **dampened** because of the cup's own weight and friction.
- If a heavy load (low-impedance load) is suddenly connected to the cup, the level drops a little — but recovers almost immediately, because the tap "tries hard" to keep the level matched to the input.

In electronics terms:
- The **"floating cup"** is the output node at the Source.
- The **"heavy load"** is $R_S$ or $R_L$ — the source resistor.
- The **"tap stiffness"** is the transconductance $g_m$.
- A **stiffer tap (high $g_m$)** = better tracking = gain $\to 1$.

The circuit is used as a **level-shifter** in modern CMOS Op-Amps, mixers, and I/O pads because it can shift a DC voltage by $V_{GS}$ without inverting the signal.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard metrics are evaluated by KTU examiners for a Source Follower:
> - **Voltage Gain** $A_v$ : Just less than **1** (positive, non-inverting) — typically **0.7 to 0.95**.
> - **Input Impedance** $R_{in}$ : Practically **infinite** at low frequencies (gate is insulated).
> - **Output Impedance** $R_{out}$ : Low — approximately **$1/g_m$** (a few hundred ohms).
> - **Phase Shift** : **0°** (in-phase with the input) — non-inverting by design.
> - **DC Level Shift** : **$V_{GS} \approx 0.7$ V** below $V_{IN}$ (with proper bias).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why and How

The Source Follower is best understood as a **shunt-series feedback amplifier** (a.k.a. voltage-voltage feedback, since the output is a voltage and the feedback samples it as a voltage). The transistor's source is its own feedback path: as $V_{out}$ rises, $V_{GS}$ falls, which throttles the drain current and pulls $V_{out}$ back down.

### Step-by-step Operating Logic

1. **DC Bias Establishment (Why bias is mandatory):**
   A pure NMOS Source Follower with $V_D = V_{DD}$ and Source tied only to ground through $R_S$ has:
   
   $$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$
   
   Since the gate draws no current, $V_G$ floats. The KCL at the gate forces $I_G = 0$, so without an external bias path, the gate has no defined DC voltage and the device sits in cutoff. The bias current source $I_{BIAS}$ (or a separate bias network) forces a known $I_D$, which in turn sets the required $V_{GS}$.

2. **Small-Signal AC Action (How the signal propagates):**
   With the Source resistor $R_S$ acting as the load, the small-signal drain current is:
   
   $$i_d = g_m v_{gs} = g_m (v_g - v_s)$$
   
   The output voltage $v_s$ is developed across $R_S$ (or $R_S \parallel r_o$ for full accuracy):
   
   $$v_s = -i_d \cdot R_S \quad \text{(current flowing out of source node)}$$
   
   Combining the two gives the closed-loop gain:
   
   $$A_v = \frac{v_s}{v_g} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

3. **Body Effect (Why $g_{mb}$ appears):**
   The Source is *not* at AC ground (it is the output!). Hence $V_{SB}$ varies with signal, modulating the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ and producing a back-gate transconductance $g_{mb}$. This **reduces** the effective gain:
   
   $$A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S}$$

4. **High-Frequency Behavior (KTU Optional Extension):**
   At very high frequencies, the parasitic capacitances $C_{gs}, C_{gd}, C_{sb}, C_{db}$ introduce poles. The dominant pole is typically at the source node due to the Miller effect of $C_{gd}$:
   
   $$C_{eq,\,source} \approx C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 - A_v) + C_{sb}$$
   
   -3 dB bandwidth:
   
   $$f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{out,eff} \cdot C_{eq,\,source}}$$

---

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Output Buffers** in Op-Amps (e.g., the second stage of the Miller-compensated two-stage CMOS op-amp uses a Source Follower for rail-to-rail output drive).
- **Level Shifters** between low-voltage core logic and high-voltage I/O pads.
- **Impedance Transformers** — high-Z source to low-Z load.
- **Source-Terminated Transmission Lines** in 50 Ω on-chip drivers.
- **Sample-and-Hold Circuits** as a unity-gain buffer in switched-capacitor systems.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|--------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Drain Current (Sat.) | $I_D$ | $\frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | Amperes |
| 2 | Transconductance | $g_m$ | $\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ | Siemens (A/V) |
| 3 | Body Transconductance | $g_{mb}$ | $\eta \cdot g_m$, where $\eta = \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\Phi_F + V_{SB}}}$ | S; $\eta \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ |
| 4 | Output Resistance | $r_o$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Ohms |
| 5 | **Voltage Gain (Ideal)** | $A_v$ | $\frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | Dimensionless, $< 1$ |
| 6 | **Voltage Gain (w/ Body Effect)** | $A_v$ | $\frac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Voltage Gain (Full Model)** | $A_v$ | $\frac{g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb})(R_S \parallel r_o)}$ | Includes $r_o$ |
| 8 | **Input Resistance** | $R_{in}$ | $\infty$ | At low freq (gate is insulated) |
| 9 | **Output Resistance** | $R_{out}$ | $\frac{1}{g_m + g_{mb}} \parallel R_S$ | $\approx 1/g_m$ for large $g_m R_S$ |
| 10 | Source Current | $i_s$ | $g_m v_{gs} = g_m v_{in} A_v$ | Amps |
| 11 | DC Level Shift | $\Delta V$ | $V_{GS} = V_{TH} + \sqrt{\frac{2 I_{BIAS}}{\mu_n C_{ox} W/L}}$ | Volts |
| 12 | -3 dB Bandwidth | $f_H$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi R_{out,eff} C_{eq}}$ | Hertz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical-bar substitution:** All absolute values or parallel operators in this table use $\vert$ or $\parallel$ to avoid Markdown table-parser conflicts. KTU answer scripts may use plain $\vert$ or the slanted notation $//$ interchangeably.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic step is explicitly written out below. No shortcuts, no skips. Read line-by-line.

---

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Small-Signal Voltage Gain $A_v$ (Ideal, Without Body Effect)

### Step 1 — Replace the MOSFET with its small-signal model
At the Source node, the controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ flows **from Drain to Source** internally. When externalised at the Source terminal, the current is $-g_m v_{gs}$ (leaving the Source node downward through $R_S$ to ground).

### Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Source Node
Let $v_s$ be the source node voltage, $v_g$ the gate node voltage, and $R_S$ the source degeneration resistor.

$$
i_{R_S} = \frac{v_s}{R_S}
$$
$$
\text{KCL: } \frac{v_s}{R_S} + g_m (v_g - v_s) = 0
$$

**Textual logic:** The current entering the Source node from the MOSFET ($g_m v_{gs}$, conventionally drawn flowing into the source) plus the current leaving through $R_S$ must sum to zero (no other path exists at the Source).

### Step 3 — Expand and Group Terms
$$
\frac{v_s}{R_S} + g_m v_g - g_m v_s = 0
$$
$$
g_m v_g = g_m v_s - \frac{v_s}{R_S}
$$
$$
g_m v_g = v_s \left(g_m - \frac{1}{R_S}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $A_v = v_s / v_g$
$$
A_v = \frac{v_s}{v_g} = \frac{g_m}{g_m - \frac{1}{R_S}}
$$

### Step 5 — Rationalise the Denominator
$$
A_v = \frac{g_m}{\frac{g_m R_S - 1}{R_S}} = \frac{g_m R_S}{g_m R_S - 1}
$$

> **Wait — sign check!** The standard result is positive. The small-signal model convention draws $g_m v_{gs}$ flowing *out of* the drain, so at the source, the *inward* current is $-g_m v_{gs}$. Re-doing KCL correctly:

$$
\frac{v_s}{R_S} - g_m(v_g - v_s) = 0
$$
$$
\frac{v_s}{R_S} = g_m v_g - g_m v_s
$$
$$
v_s \left(\frac{1}{R_S} + g_m\right) = g_m v_g
$$
$$
\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_s}{v_g} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}}
$$

**Conclusion:** $A_v$ is **positive** (non-inverting) and bounded above by 1. As $g_m R_S \to \infty$, $A_v \to 1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Including Body Effect (Full Small-Signal Model)

When the Source is **not** at AC ground, the body terminal (tied to ground for NMOS in a p-substrate) creates a second controlled source: $g_{mb} v_{bs}$, with $v_{bs} = -v_s$.

### Step 1 — KCL with Both Controlled Sources
The total current flowing out of the Source node is:
- Through $R_S$ : $\dfrac{v_s}{R_S}$
- Through the $g_m v_{gs}$ source : $-g_m (v_g - v_s)$
- Through the $g_{mb} v_{bs}$ source : $-g_{mb} (0 - v_s) = g_{mb} v_s$

KCL:
$$
\frac{v_s}{R_S} - g_m (v_g - v_s) + g_{mb} v_s = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Expand
$$
\frac{v_s}{R_S} - g_m v_g + g_m v_s + g_{mb} v_s = 0
$$
$$
v_s \left(\frac{1}{R_S} + g_m + g_{mb}\right) = g_m v_g
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $A_v$
$$
A_v = \frac{g_m}{\frac{1}{R_S} + g_m + g_{mb}} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S}
$$

**Insight:** Body effect *reduces* the gain because $g_{mb}$ appears in the denominator. For $\eta = g_{mb}/g_m = 0.2$, the gain drops roughly 17% at the same $R_S$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Output Resistance $R_{out}$

Set $v_g = 0$ (input shorted to ground) and apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the Source node. Measure the resulting current $i_x$.

### Step 1 — Test Source Model
With $v_g = 0$, the dependent source $g_m v_{gs} = g_m (0 - v_x) = -g_m v_x$.
With Source at $v_x$, the body source is $g_{mb} v_{bs} = g_{mb}(0 - v_x) = -g_{mb} v_x$.

### Step 2 — KCL at Source Node
$$
i_x = \frac{v_x}{R_S} - (-g_m v_x) - (-g_{mb} v_x)
$$
$$
i_x = \frac{v_x}{R_S} + g_m v_x + g_{mb} v_x = v_x \left(\frac{1}{R_S} + g_m + g_{mb}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $R_{out}$
$$
\boxed{R_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{g_m + g_{mb}} \parallel R_S}
$$

If $R_S \gg 1/g_m$, then $R_{out} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m + g_{mb}}$.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Given:**
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$
- $W/L = 10/1 = 10$
- $I_{BIAS} = 100 \,\mu\text{A}$
- $R_S = 1 \,\text{k}\Omega$
- $V_{TH} = 0.5 \,\text{V}$
- $g_{mb}/g_m = 0.2$

**Find:** $A_v$ and $R_{out}$.

### Step 1 — Compute $g_m$
$$
g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \mu \times 10 \times 100 \mu}
$$
$$
g_m = \sqrt{200 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 \times 100 \,\mu\text{A}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-8}} \,\text{A/V}
$$
$$
g_m \approx 1.414 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{S} = 0.1414 \,\text{mA/V} = 141.4 \,\mu\text{A/V}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $g_m R_S$
$$
g_m R_S = 141.4 \mu \times 1\text{k} = 0.1414
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $A_v$ (with body effect)
$$
g_{mb} = 0.2 \times g_m = 28.28 \,\mu\text{A/V}
$$
$$
A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S} = \frac{0.1414}{1 + (0.1414 + 0.02828) \times 1}
$$
$$
A_v = \frac{0.1414}{1 + 0.1697} = \frac{0.1414}{1.1697} \approx 0.1209
$$

**Oops — too low!** This means $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ is *not* large enough for a high-gain follower. The designer must either **increase $I_D$** (boosting $g_m$) or **use a current source load** instead of a passive $R_S$.

> [!TIP]
> **Designer's Rule of Thumb:** For $A_v > 0.9$, require $g_m R_S > 9$, which implies a *very large* $R_S$ (typically replaced by a **PMOS current source** whose small-signal resistance $r_{o,P}$ is on the order of tens of kΩ).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit-Level Schematic (Mermaid Architecture Block Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the Source Follower, mapping signal and bias flows from input to output. This is the recommended visualisation for KTU board answers when a clean pen-and-ink schematic is not feasible.

```mermaid
    F --> M1[NMOS Transistor M1]
    C --> M1
    M1 --> G[Source Node = Output Node]
    G --> H[DC Blocking Capacitor Cout]
    H --> I[Load RL external]
    G --> J[Bias Current Source IBIAS to Ground]
    G --> K[Parasitic Capacitances Cgs Cgd Csb]
    K -. feedback .-> C
    G --> L[Output Voltage Vout]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Small-Signal Model"
        G[Gate Node vg = vin] --- GGM[g_m * vgs controlled source]
        B[Body tied to GND] --- GBM[g_mb * vbs controlled source]

        GBM --- S
        S --- RS[Resistor RS to GND]
        G -. vgs .-> GGM
        B -. vbs = -vs .-> GBM
    end
    style S fill:#f9d6d6,stroke:#a94442,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Signal Flow View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Vin] -->|high Z gate| N1[Voltage-to-Current Converter gm vgs]
    N1 -->|current -gm vgs| N2[Source Node Summer]
    N2 -->|voltage divider with RS| OUT[Vout = Vin - vgs]
    OUT -->|sensed back| N2
    N2 -->|level shift VGS| LV[DC Level Shifter]
    LV --> OUT
    style N1 fill:#d9edf7,stroke:#31708f,color:#000

```

## 4.4 Functional Description (Block-Level)

| Block | Function | Mathematical Mapping |
|-------|----------|---------------------|
| **Gate Input Stage** | Receives high-impedance signal, no DC loading | $I_G = 0$, $R_{in} = \infty$ |
| **Bias Network** | Sets quiescent $I_D$ | $I_D = I_{BIAS} = 100\,\mu\text{A}$ |
| **V-to-I Converter** | $g_m$ transconductance stage | $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$ |
| **Source Impedance** | $R_S$ develops output voltage | $v_s = i_d R_S$ |
| **Feedback Path** | Source to Gate (degeneration) | $v_{gs} = v_g - v_s$ |
| **Level Shifter** | DC offset of $\approx V_{GS}$ | $V_{out,DC} = V_{in,DC} - V_{GS}$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Why is biasing essential in a CMOS Source Follower? What happens if the gate is left floating?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The CMOS Source Follower requires an explicit bias network because the gate of a MOSFET draws **no DC current** ($I_G = 0$). If the gate is left floating, the DC potential at the gate is undefined — the KCL at the gate forces zero gate current through any external resistor, leaving the gate voltage indeterminate. As a result, the transistor either stays in **cutoff** (no bias current path) or drifts into an **unknown operating region**, distorting the output.
>
> A biasing network (typically a current source $I_{BIAS}$ or a separate bias voltage divider with a current mirror) sets a known quiescent **$I_D$**, which in turn fixes the required $V_{GS}$ and establishes a stable operating point in saturation. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**State the small-signal voltage gain of a Source Follower with body effect. Why is the gain less than 1?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The small-signal voltage gain of a Source Follower including body effect is:
>
> $$A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S}$$
>
> The gain is always **less than 1** because the Source voltage $v_s$ acts as a **negative feedback** to the controlling $v_{gs}$. As $v_g$ rises, $v_s$ rises in sympathy, reducing the net $v_{gs} = v_g - v_s$, which in turn reduces the drain current and limits further rise in $v_s$. This is a **shunt-series (voltage-voltage) feedback** mechanism with closed-loop gain bounded by 1. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) Derive the small-signal voltage gain of a CMOS Source Follower, including the body effect. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit (1 mark).**
Replace the NMOS with its small-signal model: a $g_m v_{gs}$ current source between Drain and Source, a $g_{mb} v_{bs}$ current source between Body (ground) and Source. Drain is AC-grounded; gate is at $v_g$; source is at $v_s$.

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the Source node (2 marks).**

$$
\frac{v_s}{R_S} - g_m(v_g - v_s) + g_{mb}(0 - v_s) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and group terms (2 marks).**

$$
v_s \left(\frac{1}{R_S} + g_m + g_{mb}\right) = g_m v_g
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A_v$ (2 marks).**

$$
A_v = \frac{v_s}{v_g} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S}
$$

**Final boxed result (within the 7-mark scheme, the final expression is mandatory).**

---

**(b) For a Source Follower with $g_m = 1 \,\text{mA/V}$, $g_{mb} = 0.2 \,\text{mA/V}$, and $R_S = 5 \,\text{k}\Omega$, calculate the voltage gain and the output resistance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m R_S$ (1 mark).**

$$
g_m R_S = 1 \text{ mA/V} \times 5 \text{ k}\Omega = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator with body effect (2 marks).**

$$
(g_m + g_{mb}) R_S = (1 + 0.2) \times 5 = 6
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A_v$ (2 marks).**

$$
A_v = \frac{5}{1 + 6} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_{out}$ (2 marks).**

$$
R_{out} = \frac{1}{g_m + g_{mb}} \parallel R_S = \frac{1}{1.2 \text{ mA/V}} \parallel 5 \text{ k}\Omega
$$
$$
R_{out} = 833 \,\Omega \parallel 5000 \,\Omega = \frac{833 \times 5000}{833 + 5000} \approx 714 \,\Omega
$$

**Final Answer:** $A_v \approx 0.714$, $R_{out} \approx 714 \,\Omega$.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) Explain the operation of a CMOS Source Follower as a level shifter. Compute the DC level shift for $I_D = 200 \,\mu\text{A}$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 20/1$, $V_{TH} = 0.5 \,\text{V}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Level Shifting (2 marks).**
A Source Follower shifts the DC level of a signal downward by $V_{GS}$. Since the source is $V_{GS}$ below the gate, a high DC voltage at the input (e.g., a low-voltage core signal referenced to $V_{DD}$) can be shifted down to a lower reference level (e.g., 0 V or a mid-rail) needed by the next stage. The output **follows** the input AC-wise but is **DC-offset** by $-V_{GS}$.

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{GS}$ using square-law model (2 marks).**

$$
I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2
$$
$$
200 \mu = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \mu \times 20 \times (V_{GS} - 0.5)^2
$$
$$
200 \mu = 1000 \mu \times (V_{GS} - 0.5)^2
$$
$$
(V_{GS} - 0.5)^2 = 0.2
$$
$$
V_{GS} - 0.5 = \sqrt{0.2} = 0.4472
$$
$$
V_{GS} = 0.9472 \,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — State the level shift (1 mark).**
The DC level shift is $\Delta V = V_{GS} \approx 0.947 \,\text{V}$ (the output is 0.947 V *below* the gate DC voltage).

**Step 4 — Verify saturation (2 marks).**
$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - V_S$. For saturation, $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 0.4472$ V. Assuming $V_{DD} = 3$ V and $V_S \approx 0.95$ V, $V_{DS} = 2.05$ V $\gg 0.447$ V. The transistor is **in saturation** as required. ✓

---

**(b) Compare the Source Follower with the Common-Source amplifier in terms of gain, phase, input impedance, and output impedance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| Parameter | Common-Source (CS) | Source Follower (SF) | Marks |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ | High (10 to 100+); $A_v = -g_m R_D$ | $\approx 1$; $A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | 2 |
| Phase | **180°** (inverting) | **0°** (non-inverting) | 1 |
| Input Impedance $R_{in}$ | Very high ($\infty$) | Very high ($\infty$) | 1 |
| Output Impedance $R_{out}$ | Moderate to high ($R_D \parallel r_o$) | Low ($\approx 1/g_m$) | 1 |
| DC Level Shift | None inherent | Shifts by $-V_{GS}$ (level shifter) | 1 |
| Use Case | Voltage gain stage | Buffer / level shifter / output driver | 1 |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The CS amplifier provides high voltage gain with signal inversion, making it suitable for the gain stage. The Source Follower provides near-unity gain with high input impedance and low output impedance, making it ideal as a **buffer**, **level shifter**, or **output driver** in cascaded CMOS designs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the body effect term $g_{mb}$** — Many students write $A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ and lose 2–3 marks. The Source is *not* AC-grounded, so $g_{mb}$ **must** appear.
> 2. **Confusing Source Follower with Common-Source** — The CS inverts; the SF does not. KTU examiners frequently test this in 3-mark questions.
> 3. **Omitting bias justification** — When asked "design a Source Follower," students often draw the circuit without explaining *why* the bias current source is needed. Always state: *"Biasing is mandatory because $I_G = 0$."*
> 4. **Sign error in $A_v$** — Some students derive a negative gain. The Source Follower is **non-inverting**; the gain is **positive**.
> 5. **Not mentioning $r_o$** — For long-channel or low-current designs, $r_o$ significantly affects both gain and output resistance. Mentioning it in a "full small-signal analysis" earns an extra mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 12-Point Source Follower Checklist — Read Before Every Exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** Source Follower = Common-Drain amplifier; input at gate, output at source.
- [x] **Gain:** $A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S}$ — always positive, always less than 1.
- [x] **Body Effect:** $g_{mb} = \eta g_m$ where $\eta \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$. **Always include in MOSFET small-signal analysis when Source is not AC-grounded.**
- [x] **Biasing is Mandatory:** Gate has infinite DC input impedance, so a current source $I_{BIAS}$ or a reference bias network is required to set $I_D$.
- [x] **Input Impedance:** $R_{in} = \infty$ (at low frequencies).
- [x] **Output Impedance:** $R_{out} = \frac{1}{g_m + g_{mb}} \parallel R_S \approx 1/g_m$ for large $R_S$.
- [x] **Phase:** 0° (non-inverting).
- [x] **Level Shifting:** Output DC is $V_{GS}$ below the gate DC. Used to shift signals between voltage domains.
- [x] **$g_m$ Formula:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ — memorize this.
- [x] **Current Source Load:** Replacing $R_S$ with a PMOS current source gives a much higher effective $R_S$ (=$r_{o,P}$), pushing $A_v$ closer to unity.
- [x] **Design Trade-off:** Higher $I_D$ → higher $g_m$ → higher $A_v$ and lower $R_{out}$, but at the cost of **power dissipation** and **headroom**.
- [x] **KTU Favourite Question Format:** "Derive $A_v$ with body effect" + "Compare with Common-Source amplifier" — this combination appears almost every cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Gate and Differential Pair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Common Gate and Differential Pair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Common-Gate (CG) Amplifier

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Common-Gate (CG) amplifier** is a single-transistor MOS configuration in which the **input signal is applied at the source terminal**, the **output is taken at the drain terminal**, and the **gate is tied to a small-signal ground (AC ground)**. The name "common-gate" arises because the gate terminal is common to both the input port (source) and the output port (drain) with respect to AC signals.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **water pipeline with a one-way valve**:
- Water flows *in* from one side (Source = water inlet).
- Water flows *out* the other side (Drain = water outlet).
- The valve-opening knob is fixed (Gate tied to a constant bias — no wiggle allowed).
- A small fluctuation in inlet pressure (input signal at source) creates a proportional fluctuation in outlet flow (output at drain).
- Because the inlet "sees" the water in the pipe, the input resistance is very **low** (water flows in easily without backing up). The output side, however, looks into a constricted pipe → **high output resistance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Property:** The CG stage is a **non-inverting, low-input-impedance, high-output-impedance** amplifier. It is the dual of the Common-Source (CS) stage.

---

### 1.2 The MOS Differential Pair

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **MOS differential pair** is a symmetric two-transistor circuit consisting of two matched MOSFETs (typically NMOS) whose sources are tied together and biased by a **tail current source** $I_{SS}$. The two inputs are applied to the gates of the two transistors ($v_{in1}$ and $v_{in2}$), and the outputs are taken differentially (between the two drains) or single-endedly (at one drain with respect to ground). It is the **fundamental building block of analog integrated circuits**, including operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs), comparators, and mixers.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a **seesaw (teeter-totter)** balanced on a pivot (the tail current source):
- Two children sit on either end (the two transistors M1 and M2).
- If both children push equally (common-mode input), the seesaw stays level — *no useful output*.
- If one child pushes harder than the other (differential input), the seesaw tilts — *producing a large differential output*.
- The seesaw is **very sensitive to differences** but **rejects common pushes** — exactly what an ideal differential amplifier should do!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Property:** The differential pair is a **transconductance stage** that converts an input **voltage difference** $(v_{in1} - v_{in2})$ into an output **current difference** $(i_{D1} - i_{D2})$. The **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is the figure of merit quantifying how well it ignores common-mode signals.

---

### 1.3 Why These Two Circuits Together?
- **CG amplifier**: provides excellent high-frequency response (no Miller effect), used in RF, broadband, and as the "upper" transistor in a cascode.
- **Differential pair**: provides the *input stage* for nearly every op-amp and ADC — it gives DC bias stability, rejects power-supply noise, and amplifies only the *difference* between two signals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Common-Gate Amplifier — Operation Principle

**Circuit Topology (NMOS CG with resistive load $R_D$):**
- Source: input node $v_{in}$
- Gate: tied to a DC bias $V_G$ (with bypass capacitor $C_B$ making it AC ground)
- Drain: output node $v_{out}$ taken across $R_D$

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **DC Biasing:** A constant DC voltage $V_{GS}$ is set so that M1 operates in **saturation**. The drain current is $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$.
2. **AC Input at Source:** A small AC signal $v_{in}$ is applied at the source. Since the gate is AC-grounded, the effective gate-source voltage becomes $v_{gs} = v_g - v_s = 0 - v_{in} = -v_{in}$ (note the negative sign).
3. **Drain Current Modulation:** The drain current changes by $i_d = g_m v_{gs} = -g_m v_{in}$.
4. **Output Voltage:** This drain current flows through $R_D$, producing $v_{out} = -i_d R_D = +g_m R_D \, v_{in}$ (**non-inverting**).

---

### 2.2 Small-Signal Parameters of the CG Stage

Using the **T-model** (preferred for CG analysis because the source is the input):

| Parameter | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Input resistance | $R_{in} = \dfrac{1}{g_m} \,\bigg\vert\vert\, \dfrac{r_o}{1+g_m r_o}$ | **Very low** (typically $\approx 1/g_m$) |
| Output resistance | $R_{out} = R_D \,\bigg\vert\vert\, [r_o + (R_S \cdot (1+g_m r_o))]$ | **High** |
| Voltage gain (with source resistance $R_S$) | $A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R_D}{r_o}}$ | Non-inverting; $\approx g_m R_D$ if $R_S \to 0$ |
| Current gain | $A_i = \dfrac{i_{out}}{i_{in}} \approx 1$ (ideally) | Unity — input current ≈ output current |
| Short-circuit transconductance | $G_m = g_m \cdot \dfrac{r_o}{r_o + R_D}$ | Useful for cascaded analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> For an **ideal** voltage source at the source (zero source resistance, $R_S = 0$), and ignoring $r_o$:
> $$A_v \approx g_m R_D$$
> This is **identical in magnitude** to the CS gain, but **non-inverting**.

---

### 2.3 Differential Pair — Operation Principle

**Circuit Topology:**
- M1 and M2: matched NMOS pair, sources tied at node S
- Tail current source $I_{SS}$ feeds node S
- $R_D$ loads on each drain
- Inputs: $v_{G1} = V_{GS} + v_{in1}$, $v_{G2} = V_{GS} + v_{in2}$

**Defining Input Quantities:**

$$v_{id} = v_{in1} - v_{in2} \quad \text{(Differential-Mode Input)}$$

$$v_{ic} = \frac{v_{in1} + v_{in2}}{2} \quad \text{(Common-Mode Input)}$$

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. **DC Balance:** When $v_{in1} = v_{in2}$, by symmetry $I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_{SS}/2$.
2. **Differential Input:** A pure $v_{id}$ causes one transistor to draw more current while the other draws less. The small-signal drain currents are:
   $$i_{d1} = +\frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}, \quad i_{d2} = -\frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}$$
3. **Common-Mode Input:** A pure $v_{ic}$ tries to change *both* transistors equally. The tail current source **opposes** this — only a tiny fraction leaks through (finite output resistance $R_{SS}$ matters here).
4. **Output:** Either single-ended ($v_{out1}$ or $v_{out2}$) or differential ($v_{out1} - v_{out2}$).

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Differential Pair)

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Overdrive voltage (DC bias) | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{SS}}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}}}$ | Each transistor carries $I_{SS}/2$ |
| Transconductance of pair | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot \dfrac{I_{SS}}{2}} = \sqrt{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot I_{SS}}$ | |
| Differential voltage gain (single-ended out) | $A_{vd} = \dfrac{v_{od}}{v_{id}} = \dfrac{g_m R_D}{2}$ | **Factor of 2** less than CS |
| Differential voltage gain (differential out) | $A_{vd,diff} = -g_m R_D$ | Differential output doubles the gain |
| Common-mode gain | $A_{vc} = -\dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}$ | $R_{SS}$ is tail-source output resistance |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_{vd}}{A_{vc}} \right\vert = \dfrac{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}{2}$ | In dB: $20 \log_{10}(\text{CMRR})$ |
| Common-mode input range | $-V_{th} < v_{ic,min} < V_{DD} - V_{GS} - V_{th} - V_{DS,sat}$ | Set by tail current source compliance |
| Differential input range (linear) | $\vert v_{id} \vert \leq \sqrt{2} \, V_{OV}$ | Beyond this, transistors enter triode |
| Differential output current | $i_{od} = i_{d1} - i_{d2} = g_m v_{id}$ | Total small-signal transconductance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** A **single-ended output** delivers $g_m R_D / 2$ per side; a **fully-differential output** delivers the full $g_m R_D$. Most KTU problems ask for single-ended unless stated otherwise.

---

### 2.5 Cascode Amplifier (CG + CS Cascade)

> [!NOTE]
> The **cascode** is a CS amplifier (M1) with a CG amplifier (M2) stacked on top. It combines the **high input resistance of CS** with the **high output resistance of CG**, dramatically boosting gain while **killing the Miller effect** on M1.

**Output resistance of cascode:**

$$R_{out,cascode} = r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2} \approx g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$$

**Voltage gain:**

$$A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{out,cascode} \approx -g_{m1} g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$$

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **CG amplifiers**: RF low-noise amplifiers (LNAs), input stages of broadband oscilloscopes, current conveyors, cascode stages, sample-and-hold circuits.
- **Differential pairs**: Every op-amp input stage (e.g., 741, two-stage CMOS op-amps), comparators (e.g., flash ADC front-ends), Gilbert cells (multipliers/mixers), sense amplifiers in SRAM.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Common-Gate Small-Signal Voltage Gain

**Given:** NMOS CG amplifier with source degeneration resistance $R_S$, drain load $R_D$, output resistance $r_o$ included, gate AC-grounded.

**Starting KCL at the Source Node (input):**
The small-signal current flowing *into* the source from the input source is $i_{in}$. This current must equal the current flowing through the transistor from source to drain (T-model: source current = $-i_d$ entering the T).

**Step 1 — T-Model Identification:**
In the T-model, the resistance looking into the source is $1/g_m$ (in series with $r_o$ at the drain). So the input resistance from the source is:

$$R_{in} = \frac{1}{g_m} \parallel \frac{r_o}{1 + g_m r_o} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} \quad \text{(ignoring } r_o \text{)}$$

**Step 2 — KCL at Source Node:**
Let $v_{in}$ be the source node voltage. The total current entering the source node from the signal source through $R_S$ equals the current flowing into the transistor:

$$\frac{v_{sig} - v_{in}}{R_S} = g_m v_{gs} = g_m(0 - v_{in}) = -g_m v_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_{in}$ in terms of $v_{sig}$:**

$$\frac{v_{sig}}{R_S} = v_{in}\left(\frac{1}{R_S} - g_m\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{in} = \frac{v_{sig}}{1 - g_m R_S}$$

Wait — the sign is wrong. Let's redo carefully using standard convention.

**Corrected Step 2 (using hybrid-π model):**
$v_{gs} = v_g - v_s = 0 - v_{in} = -v_{in}$. The dependent current source is $g_m v_{gs} = -g_m v_{in}$ (flowing from drain to source, i.e., into the source node). The current entering the source node from outside is $i_{in} = (v_{sig} - v_{in})/R_S$. KCL at the source:

$$i_{in} + g_m v_{gs} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{v_{sig} - v_{in}}{R_S} - g_m v_{in} = 0$$

$$\frac{v_{sig}}{R_S} = v_{in}\left(\frac{1}{R_S} + g_m\right)$$

$$v_{in} = \frac{v_{sig}}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

This is the **input voltage at the source** in terms of the signal source $v_{sig}$.

**Step 4 — Output Voltage at Drain:**
KCL at the drain: current from dependent source $g_m v_{gs} = -g_m v_{in}$ (flowing out of drain into the transistor) equals current through $R_D$ and $r_o$:

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot (R_D \parallel r_o) = -g_m(-v_{in}) R_D' = g_m v_{in} R_D'$$

where $R_D' = R_D \parallel r_o$.

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{sig}} = \frac{g_m v_{in} R_D'}{v_{sig}} = \frac{g_m R_D'}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}}$$

**Special Case — Ideal Voltage Source ($R_S = 0$):**

$$A_v = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) \approx g_m R_D$$

This is the **non-inverting** canonical CG gain. Note the sign: with the convention $v_{out}$ taken at the drain and $v_{in}$ at the source, when $v_{in}$ rises, $V_{GS}$ falls, $I_D$ falls, and the drop across $R_D$ falls, so $v_{out}$ rises — **non-inverting**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Pair Small-Signal Analysis (Half-Circuit Method)

**Given:** Matched pair with $I_{SS}$ tail current, $R_D$ loads, both $r_o$ ignored initially.

**Step 1 — Decompose Inputs into Common-Mode and Differential-Mode:**

$$v_{in1} = v_{ic} + \frac{v_{id}}{2}, \quad v_{in2} = v_{ic} - \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Pure Differential Input ($v_{ic} = 0$, $v_{in1} = +v_{id}/2$, $v_{in2} = -v_{id}/2$):**
By symmetry, the source node voltage does **not change** ($v_s = 0$) because the increase in $v_{GS1}$ is exactly matched by a decrease in $v_{GS2}$, and the tail current is constant. So the source node is a **virtual ground** for differential signals.

**Step 3 — Half-Circuit Reduction:**
Each transistor sees its own gate-source voltage and a grounded source. This is **identical to a Common-Source amplifier** with input $v_{id}/2$ and output at the drain.

**Step 4 — Small-Signal Drain Currents:**

For M1: $i_{d1} = g_m \cdot v_{gs1} = g_m \cdot \dfrac{v_{id}}{2}$

For M2: $i_{d2} = g_m \cdot v_{gs2} = g_m \cdot \left(-\dfrac{v_{id}}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{g_m v_{id}}{2}$

**Step 5 — Output Voltages (single-ended):**

$$v_{od1} = -i_{d1} R_D = -\frac{g_m R_D}{2} v_{id}$$

$$v_{od2} = -i_{d2} R_D = +\frac{g_m R_D}{2} v_{id}$$

**Differential Output:**

$$v_{od} = v_{od1} - v_{od2} = -g_m R_D \cdot v_{id}$$

$$\boxed{A_{vd} = \frac{v_{od}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_D \quad \text{(fully differential output)}}$$

**Single-Ended Output (e.g., $v_{od1}$):**

$$A_{vd,\text{se}} = \frac{v_{od1}}{v_{id}} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{2}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Common-Mode Gain and CMRR

**Step 1 — Pure Common-Mode Input ($v_{id} = 0$, $v_{in1} = v_{in2} = v_{ic}$):**
Both gates move together. The source node is no longer virtual ground; it follows the input with attenuation. Each transistor sees a gate-source voltage of $v_{gs} = v_{ic} - v_s$, and the tail current source (with finite $R_{SS}$) allows a small current variation.

**Step 2 — KCL at the Tail Node:**
The total drain current is $i_{d1} + i_{d2} = 2 i_{d1}$ (by symmetry). This must equal the small-signal current through the tail source:

$$2 g_m (v_{ic} - v_s) = -\frac{v_s}{R_{SS}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_s$:**

$$2 g_m v_{ic} - 2 g_m v_s = -\frac{v_s}{R_{SS}}$$

$$2 g_m v_{ic} = v_s \left(2 g_m - \frac{1}{R_{SS}}\right)$$

$$v_s = \frac{2 g_m R_{SS} \cdot v_{ic}}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}$$

**Step 4 — Effective Gate-Source Voltage:**

$$v_{gs} = v_{ic} - v_s = v_{ic} \left(1 - \frac{2 g_m R_{SS}}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}\right) = \frac{v_{ic}}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}$$

**Step 5 — Output Voltage (single-ended):**

$$v_{oc} = -g_m v_{gs} R_D = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}} \cdot v_{ic}$$

$$\boxed{A_{vc} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}}$$

**Step 6 — CMRR:**

$$\text{CMRR} = \left|\frac{A_{vd,\text{se}}}{A_{vc}}\right| = \left|\frac{-g_m R_D / 2}{-g_m R_D / (1 + 2 g_m R_{SS})}\right|$$

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \frac{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}{2}}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}{2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** To make CMRR large, we need $2 g_m R_{SS} \gg 1$, which means a **high-impedance current source** (large $R_{SS}$, e.g., a cascode current mirror or long-channel transistor).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A CMOS differential pair uses NMOS transistors with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 50/0.5$, and tail current $I_{SS} = 200\ \mu\text{A}$. Loads are $R_D = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, and the tail current source has $R_{SS} = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$. Find:
(a) $g_m$ of each transistor
(b) Differential gain (single-ended output)
(c) Common-mode gain
(d) CMRR in dB

**Solution (a):** Each transistor carries $I_{SS}/2 = 100\ \mu\text{A}$.

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot I_D} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 100\mu \cdot 100 \cdot 100\mu}$$

$$= \sqrt{2 \cdot 10^{-4} \cdot 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.414 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{S} = 0.141\ \text{mA/V}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$A_{vd,\text{se}} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{2} = -\frac{0.141\ \text{mA/V} \cdot 10\ \text{k}\Omega}{2} = -\frac{1.414}{2} = -0.707\ \text{V/V}$$

**Solution (c):**

$$A_{vc} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}} = -\frac{0.141 \cdot 10}{1 + 2 \cdot 0.141 \cdot 1000} = -\frac{1.414}{1 + 282} = -\frac{1.414}{283} \approx -0.005\ \text{V/V}$$

**Solution (d):**

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}{2} = \frac{283}{2} = 141.5$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(141.5) = 20 \cdot 2.151 = 43.0\ \text{dB}$$

---

### 3.5 Cascode Output Resistance Derivation

**Goal:** Show that a cascode (M1 = CS, M2 = CG) has $R_{out} \approx g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$.

**Test Source Method:** Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output (drain of M2) and measure $i_x$.

**Step 1:** The current through $r_{o2}$ is $i_{r_{o2}} = v_x / r_{o2}$ (approximately, ignoring $r_{o1}$ contribution for first pass). The voltage at the source of M2 (which is the drain of M1) is approximately:

$$v_{s2} = v_x - g_{m2} v_{gs2} r_{o2} \approx v_x - g_{m2}(-v_{s2}) r_{o2} \quad \text{(since } v_{g2} = 0, v_{gs2} = -v_{s2}\text{)}$$

**Step 2:** Solve iteratively. Approximation: $v_{s2} \approx v_x / (g_{m2} r_{o2})$.

**Step 3:** The current through $r_{o1}$ (from drain M1 to ground) is $v_{s2} / r_{o1} \approx v_x / (g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2})$.

**Step 4:** The current $i_x$ entering the drain of M2 is the sum:

$$i_x \approx \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_x}{g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}}$$

For $g_{m2} r_{o1} \gg 1$:

$$\boxed{R_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} \approx g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}}$$

This is the **boosted output resistance** — the cascode's defining feature.

### 4.1 Common-Gate Amplifier Topology (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVin["V_IN Signal Source"] --> nodeRs["Source Resistance R_S"]
    nodeRs --> nodeSrc["Source Node M1"]

    nodeSrc --> nodeDrn["Drain M1"]

    nodeRd --> nodeVdd["V_DD Supply"]

    nodeTail --> nodeSrc
    nodeG --- nodeGnd["Small Signal Ground"]
```

**Functional Reading of the Diagram:**
- Input enters at the **source**, gate is **AC ground**, output exits at the **drain** — three independent signal paths converging on transistor M1.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DP["Differential Pair Core"]

        nodeM2["M2 NMOS Right Branch"]

    end
    nodeIn1["V_IN1 Input to Gate M1"] --> nodeM1
    nodeIn2["V_IN2 Input to Gate M2"] --> nodeM2

    nodeM1 --> nodeRD1["Load R_D1"]
    nodeRD1 --> nodeVdd["V_DD Positive Supply"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeRD2["Load R_D2"]
    nodeRD2 --> nodeVdd
    nodeM1 --> nodeOut1["V_OUT1 Single Ended Output"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeOut2["V_OUT2 Single Ended Output"]
    nodeOut1 --- nodeOutDiff["V_OD = V_OUT1 minus V_OUT2 Differential Output"]

```

---

### 4.3 Cascode Amplifier — Functional Stacking

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeVin["V_IN to Gate of M1"] --> nodeM1["M1 Common Source Lower Transistor"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeMid["Intermediate Node Drain M1 equals Source M2"]
    nodeMid --> nodeM2["M2 Common Gate Upper Transistor"]
    nodeBg["DC Bias V_BIAS2 Bypassed to AC Ground"] -.-> nodeM2
    nodeM2 --> nodeVout["V_OUT at Drain of M2"]
    nodeVout --> nodeRd["Load R_D"]
    nodeRd --> nodeVdd["V_DD"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeTail["Tail Current Source I_SS"]
    nodeTail --> nodeVss["V_SS"]
```

**Architecture Insight:** Signal enters at the gate of M1 (CS: high input impedance), passes through M1's drain to M2's source. M2 acts as a buffer (CG: low input impedance presented to M1's drain — isolating the high-impedance drain from the Miller effect). Output emerges at M2's drain with **multiplied output resistance**.

---

```mermaid
    nodeDM --> nodeDMGain["A_VD Differential Mode Gain Large"]
    nodeDMGain --> nodeDMOut["V_OD Desired Output Amplified"]

    nodeDMOut --- nodeFinal
```
