```mermaid
    stepE --> stepF["WRITE HOL S K0 1 PLUs STAUZ DIV S SQUARED 1 PLUs STAUP"]
    stepF --> stepG["SOLVE FOR GAIN CROSSOVER OMEGA C FROM MAG HOL JOMEGA C EQUALS 1"]
    stepG --> stepH{"IS OMEGA P MUCH GREATER THAN OMEGA C"}
    stepH -->|"YES"| stepI["USE 2ND ORDER APPROXIMATION ZETA AND OMEGA N"]
    stepH -->|"NO"| stepJ["USE FULL 3RD ORDER ANALYSIS OR COMPENSATE"]
    stepI --> stepK["COMPUTE PM ARCTAN OMEGA C TAUZ MINUS ARCTAN OMEGA C TAUP"]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL{"IS PM BETWEEN 45 AND 60 DEGREES"}
    stepL -->|"YES"| stepM["LOOP STABLE PROCEED TO LAYOUT"]
    stepL -->|"NO"| stepN["ADJUST R1 C1 C2 AND RECOMPUTE"]
    stepN --> stepE
    classDef action fill:#DAEEF3,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef warning fill:#F8CBAD,stroke:#C00000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class startA,stepM terminal
    class stepB,stepC,stepD,stepE,stepF,stepG,stepI,stepK action
    class stepH,stepL decision
    class stepJ,stepN warning
```

### 4.3 Pole-Zero Map of the Open-Loop Transfer Function

The following conceptual pole-zero map shows the location of the two integrators (double pole at origin), the compensating zero at $-\omega_z = -1/\tau_z$, and the high-frequency pole at $-\omega_p = -1/\tau_p$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA["IMAG AXIS J OMEGA"] --> axisB["REAL AXIS SIGMA"]
    p0["DOUBLE POLE AT ORIGIN FROM TWO INTEGRATORS"] --- p1["ZERO AT MINUS 1 DIV TAUZ PHASE BOOST ELEMENT"]
    p1 --- p2["POLE AT MINUS 1 DIV TAUP HIGH FREQ ATTENUATION"]
    p2 --- p3["STABLE REGION: LEFT HALF PLANE"]
    classDef pole fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef zero fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class p0,p2 pole
    class p1 zero
    class p3 safe
```

**Design rule:** The order on the negative real axis must be:
$$\text{Origin (2 poles)} \rightarrow \text{Zero } (-\omega_z) \rightarrow \text{Spurious Pole } (-\omega_p)$$
The "root-locus-style" placement — pole-zero-pole — is the signature of a well-designed CP-PLL.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Why is a passive loop-filter resistor $R_1$ essential for the stability of a charge-pump PLL?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In a CP-PLL, the open-loop transfer function inherently contains **two integrators** (one from the VCO, one from the filter capacitor), giving an $s^2$ term in the denominator. This makes the loop **unconditionally unstable** ($-180°$ phase at all frequencies, with gain that does not roll off). The series resistor $R_1$ in the loop filter introduces a **left-half-plane zero** at $\omega_z = 1/(R_1C_1)$, which provides **positive phase boost** near the unity-gain crossover, raising the phase margin to a stable positive value (typically 45°–60°). Without $R_1$, the phase margin would be exactly $0°$ and the loop would oscillate. (3 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1 mark lost if not mentioned):** Students often forget to explicitly link the **zero location** to the **PM calculation**: $PM = \arctan(\omega_c\tau_z) - \arctan(\omega_c\tau_p)$. The resistor is not "decoration" — its value directly determines damping.

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is phase margin, and what is the typical design target for a PLL in CMOS circuit design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Phase margin (PM)** is the additional phase lag that the open-loop transfer function can tolerate at the gain-crossover frequency $\omega_c$ before becoming unstable (reaching $-180°$). Mathematically:

$$PM = 180° + \angle H_{OL}(j\omega_c)$$

It quantifies the **distance from instability** and correlates directly with the **damping factor** $\zeta$ of the closed-loop transient response. The typical design target for a CMOS PLL is **PM = 45° to 60°**, with **60°** being the industry-standard optimum (corresponds to $\zeta \approx 1$, i.e., critically-damped, minimal overshoot). A PM below $\sim 40°$ results in unacceptable ringing; above $\sim 70°$ the response becomes sluggish. (3 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Examiners deduct a half-mark if the student says "phase margin greater than 0" without specifying the **range** or the **reasoning** behind the 60° target (linkage to damping/overshoot).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the open-loop transfer function $H_{OL}(s)$ of a charge-pump PLL with a second-order passive loop filter, clearly identifying each block's contribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**
- **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** PFD + CP block — write the current equation $\overline{I_{cp}} = (I_p/2\pi) \cdot \Delta\theta$ and identify the gain $K_{PD} = I_p/(2\pi)$.
- **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Loop filter — derive the impedance of the second-order RC network $Z(s) = (1 + sR_1C_1)/[s(C_1 + C_2) + s^2R_1C_1C_2]$ and apply the approximation $C_1 \gg C_2$ to obtain $Z(s) = (1 + s\tau_z)/[sC_T(1 + s\tau_p)]$.
- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** VCO — write $\theta_{VCO}(s) = (K_{VCO}/s) V_{ctrl}(s)$.
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Divide by N and multiply forward-path blocks to obtain the canonical third-order form:
  $$H_{OL}(s) = \frac{I_p K_{VCO}}{2\pi N C_T} \cdot \frac{1 + s\tau_z}{s^2(1 + s\tau_p)} = K_0 \cdot \frac{1 + s\tau_z}{s^2(1 + s\tau_p)}$$

**(b)** For a PLL with $I_p = 200\,\mu A$, $K_{VCO} = 2\pi \times 500\,Mrad/s/V$, $N = 200$, $C_1 = 200\,pF$, $C_2 = 20\,pF$, $R_1 = 5\,k\Omega$, calculate the natural frequency $\omega_n$, damping factor $\zeta$, and phase margin. Is the loop stable? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**
- **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute time constants and $C_T$:
  $\tau_z = R_1C_1 = 5\,k\Omega \times 200\,pF = 1\,\mu s$
  $\tau_p \approx R_1C_2 = 5\,k\Omega \times 20\,pF = 0.1\,\mu s$
  $C_T = 220\,pF$
- **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute $K_0$:
  $K_0 = (200 \times 10^{-6})(2\pi \times 500 \times 10^6)/(2\pi \times 200 \times 220 \times 10^{-12}) = 2.273 \times 10^{12}\,rad^2/s^2$
- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Natural frequency and damping:
  $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_0} = 1.508 \times 10^6\,rad/s$
  $\zeta = (\tau_z/2)\omega_n = (0.5\,\mu s)(1.508 \times 10^6) = 0.754$
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Crossover frequency and PM:
  $\omega_c \approx \omega_n(\zeta + \sqrt{\zeta^2+1}) = 1.508 \times 10^6 \times 2.108 = 3.179 \times 10^6\,rad/s$
  $PM = \arctan(3.179) - \arctan(0.3179) = 72.55° - 17.63° = 54.92°$
  $\Rightarrow$ **PM $\approx 55°$, loop is STABLE with good damping ($\zeta = 0.75$).**
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion: PM is within the 45°–60° design range; loop stable.

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a)** With the help of a pole-zero map, explain why a second-order PLL with only an integrating capacitor (no series resistor) is **unstable**. How does the introduction of $R_1$ stabilize the loop? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**
- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Without $R_1$, the loop filter is a pure integrator: $Z(s) = 1/(sC_1)$. The open-loop TF is $H_{OL}(s) = K_0/s^2$. This is a **double integrator** with constant $-180°$ phase and a $-40\,dB/dec$ gain slope that crosses unity at some $\omega_c$. At $\omega_c$, the phase is exactly $-180°$, giving $PM = 0°$ — the loop is **marginally unstable** (sustained oscillations).
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Pole-zero map: two poles at the origin $(s = 0, 0)$, no zero. The closed-loop poles lie on the imaginary axis (right at the stability boundary).
- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Adding $R_1$ creates a **LHP zero** at $-\omega_z = -1/(R_1C_1)$, which contributes **positive phase** up to $+90°$ near the crossover. This raises the phase margin to a positive value.
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** The closed-loop poles migrate into the LHP, ensuring stable, damped response. The resistor $R_1$ therefore **trades phase margin for damping**, and is indispensable.

**(b)** Explain the **Bode stability criterion** as applied to PLLs. Discuss the trade-off between loop bandwidth $\omega_c$ and phase margin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**
- **Step 1 (2 marks):** State the Bode criterion — a closed-loop system is stable if the open-loop gain crosses 0 dB with a **positive phase margin** (and a positive gain margin).
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Application to PLLs — at $\omega_c$, evaluate $PM = \arctan(\omega_c\tau_z) - \arctan(\omega_c\tau_p)$. Stability requires $PM > 0°$.
- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Trade-off — **higher $\omega_c$** means faster locking, better tracking of input jitter, but PM decreases (because both $\arctan$ terms approach $\pm 90°$, their difference shrinks, and the spurious pole $\omega_p$ becomes relatively closer to $\omega_c$, eroding PM).
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Conversely, **lower $\omega_c$** improves PM and reduces VCO noise folding but slows down lock acquisition. Typical rule: $\omega_c \approx 0.05\,\omega_{ref}$ to $0.1\,\omega_{ref}$ balances speed and stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas (1.5 marks per item, max 3 lost):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/(2\pi)$ in $K_{PD}$** — students often write $K_{PD} = I_p$ instead of $I_p/(2\pi)$. The phase detector converts *phase* (radians) to *current*, and the PFD output is normalized over a full $2\pi$ cycle.
> 2. **Ignoring the spurious pole $\tau_p$** — many students compute PM using only the zero, leading to PM = $\arctan(\omega_c\tau_z)$, which **overestimates** stability by 10–20°.
> 3. **Forgetting to divide by N** — the divider attenuates the feedback by N, which directly scales the loop bandwidth. A PLL with N = 100 has a 100× slower loop than one with N = 1, all else being equal.
> 4. **Not justifying the second-order approximation** — when $\omega_p < 4\omega_c$, the third-order term becomes significant, and a first-order PM calculation may give an error of $> 10°$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **CP-PLL = Type-II system** with **two integrators** in the open loop — **unstable** without a stabilizing zero.
- ☐ The **series resistor $R_1$** creates a **LHP zero** at $\omega_z = 1/(R_1C_1)$ — this is the **key stability mechanism**.
- ☐ The **shunt capacitor $C_2$** creates a high-frequency pole at $\omega_p \approx 1/(R_1C_2)$ — place it **well above $\omega_c$** (typically $\omega_p > 3\omega_c$).
- ☐ **PFD + CP gain**: $K_{PD} = I_p/(2\pi)$ A/rad — note the **$2\pi$** normalization.
- ☐ **Canonical open-loop TF**: $H_{OL}(s) = K_0(1 + s\tau_z)/[s^2(1 + s\tau_p)]$.
- ☐ **Phase margin formula**: $PM = \arctan(\omega_c\tau_z) - \arctan(\omega_c\tau_p)$ — evaluated at the **unity-gain crossover**.
- ☐ **Target PM** = **45° to 60°**; design optimum is **60°** (critically damped, $\zeta \approx 1$).
- ☐ **Damping factor** $\zeta = (\tau_z/2)\omega_n = (R_1C_1/2)\sqrt{K_0}$ — tuned by $R_1$.
- ☐ **Natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_0} = \sqrt{I_p K_{VCO}/(2\pi N C_T)}$ — set by $I_p$, $K_{VCO}$, $N$, $C_T$.
- ☐ **Trade-off rule**: $\omega_c \approx 0.05\,\omega_{ref}$ to $0.1\,\omega_{ref}$ balances speed and noise.
- ☐ **Divider $N$** appears in the denominator — larger $N \Rightarrow$ slower loop, smaller loop bandwidth.
- ☐ For the KTU exam: always write the **units**, always **state assumptions** ($C_1 \gg C_2$, $\omega_p \gg \omega_c$), and always **conclude with a stability statement** (PM value + stable/unstable verdict).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Ideal Effects in PLL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Non-Ideal Effects in PLL # ⚠️ 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) — A Quick Recap A **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** is a closed-loop negative-feedback system that locks the phase (and therefore frequency) of a Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) to an incoming reference signal. In CMOS design, a charge-pump PLL (CP-PLL) is the workhorse building block used for clock generation, frequency synthesis, clock/data recovery, and on-chip timing. The canonical architecture is: > **PFD → Charge Pump → Loop Filter (passive/active) → VCO → Feedback Divider (÷N) → PFD** In the **ideal** world, once locked, the PLL produces a clean output whose average frequency is exactly **N · f_REF**, with zero static phase error and zero spurious tones. In the **real** (CMOS) world, every block introduces imperfections that corrupt this picture. These are called **Non-Ideal Effects**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > *Non-ideal effects in a PLL refer to all the deviations from the ideal locked behavior caused by physical limitations in the PFD, charge pump, loop filter, VCO, and frequency divider. These include dead-zone, charge-pump current mismatch, leakage, finite VCO tuning range, VCO nonlinearity, reference spurs, phase noise, and finite acquisition/lock range.* ## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Crew-Rower Synchronization" Imagine an eight-member rowing team trying to keep all oars landing in the water **at the same instant** as a metronome (the reference). - The **PFD** is the coach's eyes — it sees who is early and who is late. - The **Charge Pump** is the coach's voice shouting "row harder!" (UP) or "ease off!" (DOWN). - The **Loop Filter** is the captain's brain — averaging shouts over time so he doesn't overreact to one stroke. - The **VCO** is the rowers — their stroke rate is controlled by the captain. - The **Divider** is the rowers' perception of "every 4th beat, sync with the metronome." **Non-idealities** correspond to real-world issues: - Coach has slow reaction (PFD **dead-zone / reset delay**) - "Row harder!" voice is louder than "ease off!" voice (**charge-pump current mismatch**) - The captain's brain leaks thoughts over time (**loop-filter leakage**) - The rowers don't speed up perfectly linearly with the captain's voice (**VCO gain variation / non-linearity**) - The metronome is heard as a small echo even when it shouldn't be (**reference spurs**) - The rowers introduce tiny random stroke-jitters (**VCO phase noise / jitter**) ## 1.3 Why These Effects Matter in CMOS Design In modern CMOS (sub-22nm), supply rails are low (≤ 1 V), transistor matching is imperfect, and clock frequencies are multi-GHz. A single non-ideality can render an otherwise perfect architecture unusable: - **CP mismatch** → static phase offset → deterministic jitter - **Dead zone** → random jitter near lock - **VCO non-linearity** → loop gain variation → instability - **Phase noise** → timing jitter → bit-error rate degradation in serializers - **Reference spurs** → out-of-band interference in RF transceivers > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** In the ESE (End Semester Exam), questions on "non-ideal effects" almost always test (a) the *cause*, (b) the *mathematical consequence on phase/voltage*, and (c) the *standard circuit trick used to fix it*. Master all three columns. --- # 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Big Picture — Taxonomy of Non-Ideal Effects We classify all non-idealities by the block where they originate: | # | Block | Non-Ideal Effect | Primary Symptom | |---|-------|------------------|-----------------| | 1 | PFD | Dead Zone, Reset Delay | Random jitter, gain nonlinearity | | 2 | Charge Pump | UP/DN current mismatch, Charge injection, Clock feedthrough, Leakage, Finite compliance | Static phase error, reference spurs | | 3 | Loop Filter | Capacitor leakage, Resistor mismatch | Reference spur increase, drift | | 4 | VCO | K_VCO nonlinearity, Phase noise, Limited tuning range, Pushing, Pulling | Jitter, frequency drift, instability | | 5 | Divider | Modulus-dependent delay | Fractional spurs | | 6 | Loop-Level | Reference spurs, Cycle slipping, False lock, Limited lock range | Spectral tones, slow lock, false stable states | ## 2.2 Deep Dive — Effect-by-Effect ### 2.2.1 PFD Dead Zone The **dead zone** is a range of phase error around zero in which the PFD produces *no* correction pulses — the PLL momentarily behaves as an **open loop**. **Why does it exist?** The PFD has internal logic gates and a finite reset path. When the phase error is smaller than the reset pulse width, the UP and DN pulses are too short to fully turn on the charge-pump switches. The output current of the CP averages to ~0, no charge is dumped on the filter, and the loop is "blind." **Consequence:** Near lock, the loop gain collapses → random jitter (≈ tens of ps). **Fix:** Add a **delay in the reset path** so that UP and DN always produce a guaranteed minimum pulse width (even when in lock). ### 2.2.2 Charge-Pump Current Mismatch Ideally, I_UP = I_DN = I_CP. In CMOS: $$I_{UP} = I_{DN} = I_{CP} \quad \text{(ideal)}$$ In reality, due to transistor mismatch (ΔV_TH, Δ(W/L)), channel-length modulation, and finite output impedance, the up and down currents differ: $$I_{UP} = I_{CP} + \frac{\Delta I}{2}, \qquad I_{DN} = I_{CP} - \frac{\Delta I}{2}$$ **Consequence in steady state:** To keep V_ctrl (and hence ω_out) constant over a reference period T_REF, the net charge delivered per period must be zero: $$I_{UP} \cdot t_{UP} - I_{DN} \cdot t_{DN} = 0$$ Since the PFD produces t_UP = t_DN = Δt in lock, a current mismatch requires **a non-zero static phase error Δφ_static** so that the *mismatch* in current is compensated by *mismatch* in pulse width: $$\Delta\phi_{static} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\Delta I}{I_{CP}} \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{T_{REF}}$$ **Fix:** Use **cascode current mirrors**, large device sizes, and **common-mode feedback** in the CP. ### 2.2.3 Charge-Pump Leakage Even when both UP and DN are OFF, a small leakage current I_leak flows through the CP. The loop must continually source this leakage to keep V_ctrl constant, requiring a *steady* phase error: $$\Delta\phi_{leak} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{I_{leak}}{I_{CP}} \cdot \frac{T_{REF}}{\Delta t_{min}}$$ This is the dominant static phase error mechanism in **nanometer CMOS** where gate leakage is significant. ### 2.2.4 VCO Non-Idealities **(a) VCO Gain (K_VCO) Variation** The VCO is described by: $$\omega_{out}(t) = \omega_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_{ctrl}(t)$$ But K_VCO is a function of V_ctrl itself (because C_var and the transconductor are nonlinear). Across the tuning range: $$K_{VCO,min} \leq K_{VCO} \leq K_{VCO,max}$$ This causes the **closed-loop bandwidth** ω_n to vary, jeopardizing stability (loop must be designed for worst-case K_VCO). **(b) VCO Phase Noise** The VCO is a noisy source. Its output phase fluctuates as: $$\phi_{n,VCO}(t) \quad \text{with PSD} \quad S_{\phi}(f) = \frac{2 \cdot 10^{(a)}}{\left(\frac{f}{f_0}\right)^2} + \text{higher-order terms}$$ The closed-loop PLL *low-pass filters* VCO phase noise inside the loop bandwidth and *high-pass filters* reference noise — the VCO phase noise dominates at offset frequencies **above** the loop bandwidth. **(c) VCO Pushing and Pulling** - **Pushing**: Δω_out / ΔV_DD (supply sensitivity) - **Pulling**: Δω_out due to load variation (e.g., from a power amplifier) **(d) Limited Tuning Range** The VCO must cover the full frequency range required by the divider ratio. If not, **false lock** occurs. ### 2.2.5 Reference Spurs Even with a perfect CP, mismatches and charge injection cause a small periodic ripple on V_ctrl at the reference frequency. This frequency-modulates the VCO, producing **sidebands** at f_REF (and its harmonics) called **reference spurs**: $$\text{Spur Level (dBc)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{ripple}}{2 K_{VCO} \cdot V_{pp,VCO}}\right) \quad \text{or via the formula:}$$ $$\text{Spur} \approx 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f_{BW}}{f_{REF}}\right) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{leak} + I_{mismatch}}{I_{CP}}\right)$$ where f_BW is the loop bandwidth. **Narrower loop bandwidth ⇒ lower spurs.** ### 2.2.6 Loop Acquisition — Lock, Pull-in, Hold-in, Capture - **Lock range (Δω_L)**: Frequency range over which the loop *remains* locked once locked. - **Hold-in range (Δω_H)**: Theoretically the same as the lock range for a 2nd-order loop. - **Pull-in range (Δω_P)**: Frequency range over which the loop will *eventually* lock from an unlocked state (slower, requires cycle slipping). - **Capture range (Δω_C)**: Frequency range over which the loop locks *without* cycle slipping (faster). For a 2nd-order PLL: $$\Delta\omega_L = K_{VCO} \cdot V_{ctrl,max}$$ $$\Delta\omega_P \approx \sqrt{2 \cdot K_{VCO} \cdot K_{PD} \cdot \zeta \omega_n}$$ $$\Delta\omega_C \approx K_{VCO} \cdot V_{ctrl,max} \quad \text{(for high-gain loops)}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains the **must-memorize equations** for KTU ESE 2024 Scheme questions on this topic. | # | Concept | Equation | Units | Remarks | |---|---------|----------|-------|---------| | 1 | VCO tuning | $\omega_{out} = \omega_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_{ctrl}$ | rad/s | $\omega_0$ = free-running freq | | 2 | Natural frequency (2nd order) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot C \cdot R}}$ | rad/s | $N$ = divider ratio | | 3 | Damping factor | $\zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_{PD} K_{VCO} C}{N}}$ | — | Design knob for stability | | 4 | Lock range | $\Delta\omega_L = K_{VCO} \cdot V_{ctrl,range}$ | rad/s | Set by VCO tuning range | | 5 | Static phase error (CP mismatch) | $\phi_\epsilon = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{\Delta I}{I_{CP}} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta t_{min}}{T_{REF}}$ | rad | Direct cause of deterministic jitter | | 6 | Static phase error (leakage) | $\phi_\epsilon = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{I_{leak}}{I_{CP}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{REF}}{\Delta t_{min}}$ | rad | Dominant in deep-submicron | | 7 | Reference spur | $P_{spur} \approx 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{ripple}}{V_{ctrl,FS}}\right)$ | dBc | Narrower loop ⇒ lower spur | | 8 | Phase-noise transfer (VCO) | $S_{\phi,out}^{VCO}(f) = S_{\phi,VCO}(f) \cdot \vert H_{VCO}(j2\pi f) \vert^2$ | rad²/Hz | High-pass to the VCO noise | | 9 | Phase-noise transfer (REF) | $S_{\phi,out}^{REF}(f) = S_{\phi,REF}(f) \cdot \vert H_{REF}(j2\pi f) \vert^2$ | rad²/Hz | Low-pass to reference noise | | 10 | Pull-in time (approx) | $T_P \approx \dfrac{(\Delta\omega)^2}{2 \zeta \omega_n^3}$ | s | Second-order loop | > **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations are used daily in chip design to size charge-pump currents, choose loop-filter components, budget jitter for SerDes links, and write datasheets for frequency synthesizers in 5G/Wi-Fi transceivers. --- # 🧮 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Static Phase Error Due to Charge-Pump Current Mismatch **Problem Setup.** A 2nd-order CP-PLL is in lock. PFD produces matched UP and DN pulse widths, Δt, around the lock point. Charge-pump UP current = I_CP + ΔI/2, DOWN current = I_CP − ΔI/2. Derive the static phase error φ_ε. **Step 1 — Charge balance over one reference period.** For V_ctrl to remain *constant* in lock, the net charge delivered to the loop-filter capacitor in one period must be zero: $$\Delta Q_{net} = I_{UP} \cdot t_{UP} - I_{DN} \cdot t_{DN} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Account for the static phase offset.** Because the currents differ, the PFD must bias the loop *off-center* to balance charge. In lock, the phase detector produces UP pulses of width t_UP and DN pulses of width t_DN. Define: $$t_{UP} = \Delta t_{min} + t_{\epsilon}, \qquad t_{DN} = \Delta t_{min} - t_{\epsilon}$$ where **t_ε** is the extra pulse width needed to compensate the current mismatch, and **Δt_min** is the minimum pulse width (set by the reset delay). **Step 3 — Substitute into charge balance:** $$\begin{aligned} (I_{CP} + \tfrac{\Delta I}{2})(\Delta t_{min} + t_\epsilon) - (I_{CP} - \tfrac{\Delta I}{2})(\Delta t_{min} - t_\epsilon) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Expand the products:** $$\begin{aligned} I_{CP}\Delta t_{min} + I_{CP}t_\epsilon + \tfrac{\Delta I}{2}\Delta t_{min} + \tfrac{\Delta I}{2}t_\epsilon \\ \quad - I_{CP}\Delta t_{min} + I_{CP}t_\epsilon + \tfrac{\Delta I}{2}\Delta t_{min} - \tfrac{\Delta I}{2}t_\epsilon &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Cancel terms:** $$\begin{aligned} 2 I_{CP} t_\epsilon + \Delta I \cdot \Delta t_{min} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Solve for t_ε:** $$t_\epsilon = -\frac{\Delta I \cdot \Delta t_{min}}{2 I_{CP}}$$ **Step 7 — Convert t_ε to static phase error φ_ε.** The phase error (in radians) is related to the pulse-width offset by the reference period: $$\begin{aligned} \phi_\epsilon &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{t_\epsilon}{T_{REF}} \\ &= 2\pi \cdot \left(-\frac{\Delta I \cdot \Delta t_{min}}{2 I_{CP} \cdot T_{REF}}\right) \\ &= -\frac{\pi \cdot \Delta I \cdot \Delta t_{min}}{I_{CP} \cdot T_{REF}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 8 — Final compact form:** $$\boxed{\;\phi_\epsilon = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\Delta I}{I_{CP}} \cdot \frac{\Delta t_{min}}{T_{REF}}\;}$$ > **Interpretation:** Larger mismatch, longer reset delay, or longer reference period → larger static phase offset. This is **deterministic jitter** — it does not average out. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Natural Frequency and Damping of a 2nd-Order CP-PLL **Setup.** A standard passive lead-lag loop filter (R series, C to ground). PFD/CP combined gain K_PD = I_CP / (2π) (A/rad). VCO gain K_VCO (rad/s/V). Divider ratio N. **Step 1 — Open-loop transfer function (linearized).** The loop consists of K_PD, the filter impedance Z(s) = R + 1/(sC), the VCO integrator 1/s, and the divider 1/N: $$G(s) = \frac{K_{PD} \cdot \left(R + \frac{1}{sC}\right) \cdot \frac{K_{VCO}}{s}}{N}$$ **Step 2 — Simplify:** $$G(s) = \frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO}}{N C} \cdot \frac{1 + sRC}{s^2}$$ **Step 3 — Characteristic equation: 1 + G(s) = 0:** $$s^2 + \frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO} R}{N} s + \frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO}}{N C} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Compare to standard 2nd-order form** s² + 2ζω_n s + ω_n² = 0: $$\begin{aligned} \omega_n^2 &= \frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO}}{N C} \\ 2 \zeta \omega_n &= \frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO} R}{N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Final formulas:** $$\boxed{\;\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO}}{N C}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO} C}{N}}\;}$$ > **Design Insight:** The damping ζ is controlled by R, while ω_n is set by C. K_VCO is set by the VCO, and is **non-ideal** (varies with V_ctrl). This is why designers use a **bandwidth 1/10 to 1/20 of f_REF** — to keep ζ > 0.7 even at K_VCO,max. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reference Spur Estimation (Simplified Behavioral Model) **Step 1 — Ripple amplitude on V_ctrl.** If the static phase error is φ_ε and the loop-filter impedance at f_REF is Z(f_REF), the ripple amplitude is approximately: $$V_{ripple} \approx \frac{I_{CP} \cdot \Delta t_{min}}{C} \cdot \phi_\epsilon \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi f_{REF}}$$ **Step 2 — This ripple FM-modulates the VCO:** $$\omega_{out}(t) = \omega_0 + K_{VCO} V_{ctrl}(t)$$ For a sinusoidal ripple V_ripple cos(2π f_REF t), the peak frequency deviation is: $$\Delta f_{peak} = K_{VCO} \cdot V_{ripple}$$ **Step 3 — FM sideband amplitude (Carson's rule, small-modulation limit):** For small modulation index m = Δf_peak / f_REF, the sideband amplitude relative to the carrier is: $$\frac{A_{sideband}}{A_{carrier}} \approx \frac{m}{2} = \frac{K_{VCO} V_{ripple}}{2 f_{REF}}$$ **Step 4 — Spur level in dBc:** $$\boxed{\;P_{spur} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{K_{VCO} V_{ripple}}{2 f_{REF}}\right) \text{ dBc}\;}$$ > **Engineering takeaway:** Narrower loop bandwidth → larger C → smaller V_ripple → **lower reference spurs**. This is the eternal trade-off: bandwidth ↔ spur. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Each Non-Ideality Manifests Mathematically | Non-Ideality | Effect on Phase Plane | Effect on Frequency Response | Effect on Spectrum | |--------------|-----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------| | PFD Dead Zone | Reduces K_PD near zero | Loss of loop gain at low error | Raised noise floor near carrier | | CP Mismatch | Static phase offset | DC phase error | Deterministic jitter (DJ) | | CP Leakage | Slow drift in φ | DC phase error | Reference spurs | | K_VCO variation | Loop bandwidth drift | ω_n, ζ vary with V_ctrl | Possible peaking/oscillation | | VCO Phase Noise | Random walk of φ | High-pass transfer to output | Close-in + far-out phase noise | | Reference Spurs | Periodic φ ripple | Discrete spurs at f_REF | Strong tones at f_REF, 2f_REF… | | Divider Delay | Adds lag in loop | Reduces phase margin | Spurs at f_REF/N | --- # 🗺️ 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of CP-PLL Highlighting Non-Ideal Injection Points ```mermaid flowchart LR A[REF fREF] -->|clock| PFD1[PFD] PFD1 -->|UP pulse
dead-zone and reset delay| CP1[Charge Pump] CP1 -->|Imismatch and Ileak| LF1[Loop Filter R and C] LF1 -->|Vctrl with ripple| VCO1[VCO] VCO1 -->|KVCO nonlinearity and phase noise| OUT1[fOUT N times fREF] OUT1 --> DIV1[Divide by N] DIV1 --> PFD1 LF1 -.->|Vripple| SPUR1[Reference Spurs on spectrum] VCO1 -.->|phi noise| JIT1[Jitter at output] CP1 -.->|static phase offset| PE1[Deterministic Jitter] classDef idealBlock fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 classDef nonIdealBlock fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 classDef outputBlock fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 class PFD1,CP1,LF1,VCO1,DIV1 nonIdealBlock class OUT1,SPUR1,JIT1,PE1 outputBlock ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Mermaid Block — How Non-Idealities Propagate ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([PLL Input REF]) --> PFDnode PFDnode[Phase Frequency Detector] -->|has dead zone| DeadZoneEffect[Dead Zone Effect] PFDnode -->|produces UP and DN| CPnode DeadZoneEffect -->|causes jitter| OutputNode[Final Output] CPnode[Charge Pump] -->|mismatch| MismatchEffect[Current Mismatch] CPnode -->|leakage| LeakEffect[Leakage Current] CPnode -->|charge injection| ChargeInject[Charge Injection] MismatchEffect -->|static phase error| OutputNode LeakEffect -->|reference spurs| OutputNode ChargeInject -->|glitches on Vctrl| OutputNode LFnode[Loop Filter] -->|ripple| RippleEffect[Vctrl Ripple] RippleEffect -->|FM mod| OutputNode VCOnode[VCO] -->|KVCO varies| Nonlinearity[K VCO Nonlinearity] VCOnode -->|phase noise| PhaseNoise[VCO Phase Noise] Nonlinearity -->|loop BW drift| OutputNode PhaseNoise -->|close in and far out noise| OutputNode DIVnode[Divider by N] -->|modulus delay| DivDelay[Modulus Delay] DivDelay -->|fractional spurs| OutputNode OutputNode[PLL Output and Spectrum] --> End([Final Clock fOUT]) ``` ## 4.3 Static Phase Error Origin — Physical Explanation Block ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A[In ideal PLL] I1[I UP equals I DN] --> N1[Net charge per period equals 0] N1 --> Z1[Static phase error equals 0] end subgraph B[In real PLL] I2[I UP not equal I DN] --> N2[Net charge not 0 for equal pulse widths] N2 --> Z2[PFD produces unequal UP and DN widths] Z2 --> S2[Non zero static phase error phi epsilon] end A -.contrasted with.-> B ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Red blocks represent non-ideal blocks, blue blocks are outputs/symptoms. In every CMOS PLL design review at industry level, engineers create exactly this kind of "non-ideality map" to trace each spur and jitter component to its root cause. --- # 🎯 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]` **What is meant by the "dead zone" of a Phase Frequency Detector (PFD) in a charge-pump PLL? Explain its effect on PLL performance and how it can be eliminated.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **dead zone** of a PFD is the small range of phase error around zero in which the detector fails to generate proper UP and DN correction pulses. This happens because the internal NAND-reset path of the PFD has a finite propagation delay, and when the phase error is smaller than this delay, the output pulses become too narrow to fully switch ON the charge-pump transistors. Consequently, the average charge-pump current for small phase errors is nearly zero, and the loop behaves as if open. **Effect on performance:** The closed-loop gain collapses near lock, causing **increased random jitter** and a raised noise floor in the output spectrum. **Elimination:** A **delay element** (typically an inverter chain of ~1–2 ns) is intentionally inserted in the PFD reset path. This guarantees a minimum UP and DN pulse width (Δt_min) even at zero phase error, ensuring the CP switches on fully and the loop gain remains linear. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]` **List any four non-ideal effects of a charge-pump PLL and state one consequence for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Non-Ideal Effect | Consequence | |---|------------------|-------------| | 1 | Charge-pump current mismatch (I_UP ≠ I_DN) | Static phase offset in lock → deterministic jitter | | 2 | CP leakage current | Slow drift in V_ctrl → reference spurs | | 3 | VCO gain (K_VCO) non-linearity | Loop bandwidth and damping vary with V_ctrl → instability risk | | 4 | PFD dead zone | Random jitter near lock | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **A charge-pump PLL is designed with the following parameters:** - VCO gain K_VCO = 2π × 800 MHz/V - Charge-pump current I_CP = 100 μA (with mismatch ΔI = 5 μA) - Reference frequency f_REF = 50 MHz (T_REF = 20 ns) - PFD minimum pulse width Δt_min = 2 ns - Loop filter: R = 5 kΩ, C = 50 pF - Divider ratio N = 10 - Leakage current of CP: I_leak = 1 μA ### **(a)** Calculate the static phase error due to (i) current mismatch and (ii) leakage current. What is the **total** static phase error? (7 marks) **Step 1 — Static phase error due to mismatch:** $$\begin{aligned} \phi_{\epsilon, mismatch} &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{\Delta I}{I_{CP}} \cdot \frac{\Delta t_{min}}{T_{REF}} \\ &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{5\,\mu A}{100\,\mu A} \cdot \frac{2\,ns}{20\,ns} \\ &= 2\pi \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.1 \\ &= 2\pi \cdot 0.005 \\ &= 0.0314 \text{ rad} \\ &\approx 1.8^\circ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Static phase error due to leakage:** $$\begin{aligned} \phi_{\epsilon, leak} &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{I_{leak}}{I_{CP}} \cdot \frac{T_{REF}}{\Delta t_{min}} \\ &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{1\,\mu A}{100\,\mu A} \cdot \frac{20\,ns}{2\,ns} \\ &= 2\pi \cdot 0.01 \cdot 10 \\ &= 0.628 \text{ rad} \\ &\approx 36^\circ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Total static phase error (sum, since both contribute in the same direction):** $$\phi_{\epsilon, total} = 0.0314 + 0.628 = 0.660 \text{ rad} \approx 37.8^\circ$$ > **Conclusion:** Leakage dominates the static phase error in this design. To minimize, we must **reduce I_leak** (e.g., use longer-channel CP transistors, low-leakage process options) or **increase Δt_min** (use a longer reset delay in the PFD). --- ### **(b)** Determine the natural frequency ω_n, damping factor ζ, and closed-loop bandwidth of the PLL. Comment on stability. (7 marks) **Step 1 — PFD/CP combined gain:** $$K_{PD} = \frac{I_{CP}}{2\pi} = \frac{100\,\mu A}{2\pi} = 15.92\,\mu A/rad$$ **Step 2 — Natural frequency:** $$\begin{aligned} \omega_n &= \sqrt{\frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot C}} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{15.92 \times 10^{-6} \times 2\pi \times 800 \times 10^{6}}{10 \times 50 \times 10^{-12}}} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{15.92 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.027 \times 10^{9}}{5 \times 10^{-10}}} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{80{,}030}{5 \times 10^{-10}}} \\ &= \sqrt{1.6006 \times 10^{14}} \\ &\approx 1.265 \times 10^{7} \text{ rad/s} \end{aligned}$$ $$\omega_n \approx 12.65 \text{ Mrad/s} \;\;(\Rightarrow f_n \approx 2.01 \text{ MHz})$$ **Step 3 — Damping factor:** $$\begin{aligned} \zeta &= \frac{R}{2} \sqrt{\frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO} C}{N}} \\ &= \frac{5000}{2} \sqrt{\frac{15.92 \times 10^{-6} \times 2\pi \times 800 \times 10^{6} \times 50 \times 10^{-12}}{10}} \\ &= 2500 \sqrt{\frac{15.92 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.027 \times 10^{9} \times 50 \times 10^{-12}}{10}} \\ &= 2500 \sqrt{4.001 \times 10^{-7}} \\ &= 2500 \times 6.32 \times 10^{-4} \\ &\approx 1.58 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Closed-loop bandwidth (approx f_{-3dB} ≈ 2 ζ ω_n):** $$f_{BW} \approx 2 \zeta f_n = 2 \times 1.58 \times 2.01 \text{ MHz} \approx 6.35 \text{ MHz}$$ **Step 5 — Stability comment:** - ζ ≈ 1.58 is **overdamped** (target is 0.7 < ζ < 1.0 for fast lock without ringing). - Loop bandwidth (6.35 MHz) is much smaller than f_REF/10 = 5 MHz — wait, actually it is comparable. We can state: **bandwidth is about 1/8 of f_REF**, which is at the upper end of recommended design. Good reference tracking but moderate spur suppression. > **Conclusion:** Loop is stable. To improve spur performance, reduce C (and hence f_n) by 3–5×. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]` **Explain the following non-ideal effects in a PLL with diagrams and equations:** **(a)** PFD dead zone and charge-pump current mismatch — causes, consequences, and remedies. (7 marks) **(b)** VCO phase noise, K_VCO non-linearity, and reference spurs — origin, impact on output spectrum, and standard circuit techniques to mitigate them. (7 marks) ### **(a) Part (a) — PFD Dead Zone & CP Current Mismatch** **PFD Dead Zone:** A PFD uses a tri-state output (UP, DN, or Hi-Z). After a phase comparison, both UP and DN go high momentarily, triggering a reset NAND gate. If the phase error is smaller than the **reset pulse width**, the UP and DN output pulses do not reach a high enough amplitude or duration to switch ON the CP current sources — the average current is zero, and the loop is "blind." **Consequence:** Near lock, loop gain drops to zero → the PLL cannot correct small phase errors → **random jitter** appears at the output, observable as a raised noise floor on a phase-noise analyzer. **Remedy:** Insert a **delay buffer** (typically 2–4 inverter stages) in the reset path. This guarantees a minimum pulse width Δt_min ≈ 1–2 ns even when the phase error is zero, ensuring the CP always sees a usable pulse. **Charge-Pump Current Mismatch:** The CP consists of two current sources (UP and DN) switched by the PFD outputs. In CMOS, transistor mismatch (ΔV_TH, ΔW/L, mobility variation) makes: $$I_{UP} = I_{CP} + \frac{\Delta I}{2}, \quad I_{DN} = I_{CP} - \frac{\Delta I}{2}, \quad \Delta I \neq 0$$ **Consequence:** To maintain V_ctrl constant, the PFD shifts the static operating point off zero phase, producing a **static phase error φ_ε**: $$\phi_\epsilon = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\Delta I}{I_{CP}} \cdot \frac{\Delta t_{min}}{T_{REF}}$$ This phase error directly manifests as **deterministic jitter** at the output. **Remedies:** - Use **cascode current mirrors** to increase output impedance - Use **large transistor sizes** to reduce ΔV_TH mismatch - Use a **dummy switch** at the output to cancel charge injection - Use **common-mode feedback** to keep V_ctrl in the high-impedance region of the CP - Add a **unity-gain buffer** between CP and loop filter to prevent charge sharing ### **(b) Part (b) — VCO Phase Noise, K_VCO Non-linearity, Reference Spurs** **VCO Phase Noise:** The VCO is an autonomous oscillator whose phase is perturbed by thermal noise (in the resonator, transconductor, and tail current source) and flicker (1/f) noise. The output spectrum is no longer a pure tone but has sidebands characterized by the phase-noise profile S_φ(f). Near the carrier, phase noise typically follows a **1/f²** (or 1/f³) profile. **Impact:** Above the loop bandwidth, VCO phase noise **passes directly to the output**; inside the loop bandwidth, it is suppressed by the loop. The VCO phase noise is therefore the dominant close-in noise source for offsets greater than the loop bandwidth. **Mitigation:** - Use a high-Q LC tank (or low-phase-noise ring topology) - Use a tail-current filter (large L, large C at the tail) - Use large device sizes and good bias decoupling - Add a **regulator** to power the VCO (reduces supply pushing) **K_VCO Non-linearity:** An ideal VCO has constant K_VCO. In a ring VCO, K_VCO depends on the delay cell's bias current, V_ctrl, and supply — it is highly nonlinear. In an LC VCO, the varactor's C-V curve is nonlinear. **Impact:** Closed-loop bandwidth ω_n and damping ζ vary with V_ctrl. The loop designed for nominal K_VCO may become unstable at the extremes of tuning. **Mitigation:** - Use a **linearized varactor** (e.g., switched-capacitor bank + small analog varactor) - Add **amplitude control** (AGC) to keep VCO swing constant - Use digital calibration to pre-set K_VCO close to design value - Design for **worst-case K_VCO** (largest value) — sets minimum phase margin **Reference Spurs:** Periodic disturbance on V_ctrl (caused by CP mismatch, leakage, charge injection, or clock feedthrough) at frequency f_REF frequency-modulates the VCO, creating discrete sidebands at offsets f_REF (and harmonics) — these are **reference spurs**, measured in dBc. **Impact:** A strong reference spur can violate the spectral mask of a wireless standard (e.g., 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6E). **Mitigation:** - **Narrow the loop bandwidth** (lower f_n) → V_ctrl ripple is filtered more - Use a **larger C in the loop filter** to reduce ripple amplitude - Use a **3rd- or 4th-order loop filter** to provide additional attenuation at f_REF - **Match CP currents** with careful layout and large devices - Use **differential CP topology** to cancel common-mode ripple --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in KTU ESE on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing "static phase error" with "phase offset at the PFD input"** — they are the *same* thing in lock, but the static phase error is the *result* of non-ideality, not a design choice. > 2. **Mixing up "dead zone" and "lock range"** — dead zone is a *PFD non-ideality*; lock range is a *loop-level specification*. Don't write them in the same sentence as if they are interchangeable. > 3. **Forgetting the factor 2π in the phase-error equation.** Many answers drop the 2π and write the wrong units. Always include the conversion from time to phase. > 4. **Drawing the PFD output as one signal instead of UP + DN.** The PFD has two outputs, and the *difference* is what feeds the CP. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy PFD diagrams. > 5. **Writing "VCO phase noise = jitter"** — phase noise is in the *frequency domain* (dBc/Hz), jitter is in the *time domain* (ps). Show the integration step (or at least mention it). > 6. **Stating "higher K_VCO = better"** — wrong. Higher K_VCO increases loop bandwidth, worsens spurs, and increases VCO sensitivity to noise on V_ctrl. K_VCO is a *design parameter*, not a figure of merit. > 7. **Forgetting that ζ < 0.7 ⇒ underdamped ⇒ ringing during lock.** A loop with low ζ *can* lock but takes long, with overshoot. The minimum acceptable is usually 0.707 (Butterworth). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Non-Ideal Effects in PLL** - ✅ A PLL's **closed-loop bandwidth** must be set between 1/20 and 1/10 of f_REF to balance jitter transfer vs. spur suppression. - ✅ **Dead zone** is fixed by adding a **reset-path delay** in the PFD → guarantees a minimum CP pulse width Δt_min. - ✅ **CP current mismatch** produces a **static phase error** proportional to ΔI/I_CP × Δt_min/T_REF. - ✅ **CP leakage** also produces a static phase error, scaling with I_leak/I_CP × T_REF/Δt_min — dominant in sub-22nm CMOS. - ✅ **K_VCO non-linearity** → loop parameters (ω_n, ζ) vary with V_ctrl → design for worst-case K_VCO,max. - ✅ **VCO phase noise** is **high-pass filtered** by the loop (dominant at offsets > f_BW); **reference noise** is **low-pass filtered** (dominant at offsets < f_BW). - ✅ **Reference spurs** come from periodic V_ctrl ripple → narrower loop bandwidth reduces them. - ✅ **Lock range ≈ Hold-in range** for 2nd-order PLL = K_VCO × V_ctrl,max. - ✅ **Pull-in range ≤ Lock range** and requires **cycle slipping** — a slow acquisition mechanism. - ✅ **Charge injection** is mitigated by **dummy switches**; **clock feedthrough** by **differential CP topology**. - ✅ Always size CP transistors large and use **cascode mirrors** to combat mismatch and channel-length modulation. - ✅ The PLL output frequency is **f_OUT = N × f_REF** — but only after lock, when the static phase error is fixed. - ✅ For **fractional-N PLLs**, the divider-delay variation produces **fractional spurs** at multiples of f_REF/2^M (where M is the accumulator width). - ✅ **Leakage-induced spurs** scale with **I_leak/I_CP × f_REF/f_BW** — narrowing the bandwidth helps but slows the loop. - ✅ The **trade-off matrix** to memorize: bandwidth ↔ spur; K_VCO ↔ stability; I_CP ↔ Δt_min ↔ phase error; N ↔ phase noise gain. ---

topics.title: Applications of PLL - Frequency Multiplication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET631 - CMOS CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications of PLL - Frequency Multiplication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Frequency Multiplication using a Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a frequency synthesis technique in which a PLL is configured such that its Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) is forced to oscillate at an integer multiple of the input reference frequency. This is achieved by inserting a **frequency divider** (with division ratio $N$) in the feedback path of the PLL. When the loop is in lock, the frequency fed back to the **Phase Frequency Detector (PFD)** equals the input reference frequency, causing the VCO to operate at exactly $N$ times the input frequency.

Mathematically, the fundamental relationship governing this operation is:

$$f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$$

where $f_{ref}$ is the input reference frequency, $N$ is the integer division ratio (typically $\geq 1$), and $f_{out}$ is the synthesized output frequency at the VCO.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Frequency multiplication is one of the most commercially significant applications of PLLs. It forms the backbone of every modern **Frequency Synthesizer**, RF transceiver, and clock generation circuit used in microprocessors, mobile phones, and wireless communication systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **grandfather clock** with a perfectly balanced pendulum. Now suppose you want to make a *faster* clock that ticks exactly 10 times for every single tick of the grandfather clock. You could try to mechanically gear it — but gears wear out and drift.

Instead, imagine a **smart child** who listens to the grandfather clock's tick. The child spins a wheel as fast as possible, and a *helper* compares the wheel's speed to the grandfather clock. If the wheel is too fast, the helper gently applies a brake; if too slow, the helper pushes it. The helper adjusts until **exactly 10 spins** of the wheel occur for **every 1 tick** of the grandfather clock.

The child is the **VCO**, the helper is the **Phase Frequency Detector + Loop Filter**, the grandfather clock is the **reference**, and the "rule of 10" is the **frequency divider ($N = 10$)** placed in the feedback path. The child's wheel spins at 10× the grandfather clock — this is frequency multiplication.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics

The following are the standard, exam-relevant performance metrics for a PLL-based frequency multiplier:

- **Lock Range ($\Delta\omega_L$ or $\Delta f_L$)**: The range of frequencies over which the PLL can maintain lock once locked. Also called the *tracking range* or *hold-in range*.
- **Capture Range ($\Delta\omega_C$ or $\Delta f_C$)**: The range of frequencies over which the PLL can acquire lock from an unlocked state. Always **smaller** than the lock range.
- **Pull-in Time**: The time required for the PLL to acquire lock.
- **Spurious Output**: Unwanted frequency components at the output, typically at $f_{ref}$ and its harmonics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Always Bold in KTU Answers):**
> - Lock Range: $\Delta\omega_L = \omega_{VCO,max} - \omega_{VCO,min}$
> - Capture Range: $\Delta\omega_C \leq \Delta\omega_L$
> - Output Frequency: $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The fundamental principle of PLL-based frequency multiplication rests on a single observation: **a PLL, when locked, forces the feedback signal to match the reference signal in both frequency and phase.** By manipulating the feedback signal through a divider, we control the VCO's effective output.

**Step-by-Step Logic Flow:**

1. **Reference Input:** A clean, stable reference signal of frequency $f_{ref}$ is applied to one input of the Phase Frequency Detector (PFD).

2. **Divided Feedback:** The VCO output at frequency $f_{out}$ is fed into a **frequency divider** (a digital counter that divides by an integer $N$). The divider's output is $f_{out}/N$.

3. **Phase Comparison:** The PFD compares $f_{ref}$ with $f_{out}/N$ and produces an error voltage proportional to the phase/frequency difference.

4. **Loop Filtering:** The **Low-Pass Loop Filter (LPF)** smooths this error voltage, removing high-frequency noise and PFD ripples. The output of the LPF is a slowly varying control voltage $V_c$.

5. **VCO Tuning:** $V_c$ tunes the VCO such that its oscillation frequency is **driven toward** $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$.

6. **Lock Condition:** At lock, the feedback signal exactly matches the reference:
   $$f_{ref} = \frac{f_{out}}{N} \implies f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$$

7. **Steady State:** The PFD output becomes (nearly) a DC value, and the VCO runs stably at the multiplied frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a PLL for frequency multiplication rather than a simple harmonic generator?**
> - A nonlinear diode generates harmonics, but the output is *weak*, *noisy*, and *not tunable* over a wide range.
> - A PLL frequency multiplier produces a *clean*, *stable*, *tunable*, and *high-purity* output locked to a precise reference.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Significance** |
|------------------------|--------------------------|-----------|------------------|
| Output Frequency | $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$ | Hz | Fundamental relationship of frequency multiplication |
| VCO Tuning Constant | $K_{VCO} = \dfrac{d\omega_{VCO}}{dV_c}$ | rad/(s·V) | Sensitivity of VCO frequency to control voltage |
| PFD / Phase Detector Gain | $K_{PD} = \dfrac{V_d}{\Delta\phi}$ | V/rad | Voltage produced per radian of phase error |
| Loop Filter Transfer Function | $F(s)$ | dimensionless | Sets loop dynamics; commonly 1st-order RC |
| Open-Loop Gain | $G(s) = K_{PD} \cdot F(s) \cdot \dfrac{K_{VCO}}{s}$ | 1/s | Determines bandwidth, stability |
| Lock Range (Tracking Range) | $\Delta\omega_L = K_{PD} \cdot F(0) \cdot K_{VCO}$ | rad/s | Range PLL can track while locked |
| Capture Range (Pull-in Range) | $\Delta\omega_C \approx \Delta\omega_L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_2}{\tau_1}}$ | rad/s | Range over which lock can be acquired |
| Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot \tau_1}}$ | rad/s | Sets loop transient response speed |
| Damping Factor | $\zeta = \dfrac{\tau_2}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot \tau_1}}$ | dimensionless | Controls overshoot and ringing |
| 3-dB Loop Bandwidth | $\omega_{BW} \approx 2\zeta\omega_n$ | rad/s | Sets noise vs. speed trade-off |
| Frequency Division Ratio | $N = \dfrac{f_{out}}{f_{ref}}$ | dimensionless | Integer $\geq 1$; often programmable |
| Lock Condition (Phase) | $\phi_{out} = \phi_{ref}$ (mod $2\pi$) | rad | At lock, phase error is constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** The above table uses `\vert` instead of `|` to denote magnitude/absolute values (e.g., $\vert \Delta\omega \vert$) so that the markdown table parser is **not broken** by vertical pipe characters. This is a mandatory convention in KTU 2024 scheme digital answer sheets.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Frequency multiplication via PLL is the **heart of every modern frequency synthesizer**. Some critical applications include:

- **Cellular base stations (4G/5G):** Local Oscillator (LO) generation at GHz frequencies from a low-frequency crystal reference (e.g., 26 MHz TCXO → 2.6 GHz LO).
- **Microprocessor Clock Generation:** A 100 MHz crystal is multiplied to 3–5 GHz CPU clock using a PLL inside the chipset.
- **Digital Radio / SDR:** Channel selection requires precise, agile frequency synthesis.
- **Radar Systems:** Coherent frequency generation for pulse-Doppler radar.
- **FM Broadcast Transmitters:** 88–108 MHz carrier generation from a stable crystal.
- **Test & Measurement Equipment:** Function/signal generators use PLL frequency synthesis for high spectral purity.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **division ratio $N$** in modern PLLs is implemented using a **programmable digital counter** (often a Dual-Modulus Prescaler in high-frequency designs). By changing $N$, the output frequency can be digitally programmed — this is the basis of a **Direct Digital Frequency Synthesizer's analog counterpart**, the **Integer-N PLL Synthesizer**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Frequency Multiplication Relationship

**Goal:** Derive $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$ from the basic PLL equations.

**Step 1 — Define the input (reference) signal** at the PFD input:
$$v_{ref}(t) = V_R \sin(\omega_{ref} t + \phi_{ref})$$

**Step 2 — Define the VCO output signal** (before divider):
$$v_{VCO}(t) = V_O \cos(\omega_{out} t + \phi_{out})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the frequency divider in the feedback path.** The divider produces an output whose frequency is $N$ times lower:
$$v_{fb}(t) = V_O \cos\!\left(\frac{\omega_{out}}{N} \cdot t + \frac{\phi_{out}}{N}\right)$$

**Step 4 — At phase lock**, the frequency of $v_{fb}$ must equal the frequency of $v_{ref}$ (this is the defining property of a locked PLL):
$$\frac{\omega_{out}}{N} = \omega_{ref}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the output angular frequency:**
$$\omega_{out} = N \cdot \omega_{ref}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to ordinary frequency in Hz** (divide both sides by $2\pi$):
$$\boxed{f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}}$$

**Step 7 — Additionally, at lock**, the phase error settles to a constant value (zero in the ideal case):
$$\frac{\phi_{out}}{N} - \phi_{ref} = \phi_e = \text{constant (ideally } 0\text{)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Open-Loop Transfer Function and Closed-Loop Behavior

**Goal:** Show how the divider ratio $N$ appears in the loop dynamics and affects the natural frequency $\omega_n$.

**Step 1 — Identify each block's transfer function in the Laplace domain:**

- Phase Detector: produces voltage $V_d = K_{PD} \cdot \phi_e$ → transfer function: $K_{PD}$
- Loop Filter: $F(s)$
- VCO: integrates control voltage to produce phase → transfer function: $\dfrac{K_{VCO}}{s}$

**Step 2 — Write the open-loop transfer function** $G(s)$ for a PLL with a divider of ratio $N$ in the feedback path:

$$
G(s) = \frac{V_{c}(s)}{\phi_{e}(s)} = K_{PD} \cdot F(s) \cdot \frac{K_{VCO}}{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the closed-loop transfer function** using standard negative feedback theory with the divider acting as a feedback factor of $1/N$:

$$
H(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + \dfrac{G(s)}{N}} = \frac{N \cdot G(s)}{N + G(s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute a common 1st-order RC loop filter** $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + s\tau_1}$ and define $\tau_2$ for higher-order filters. For a 2nd-order PLL with a passive lead-lag filter:

$$
F(s) = \frac{1 + s\tau_2}{1 + s\tau_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the characteristic equation** $1 + G(s)/N = 0$:

$$
1 + \frac{1}{N} \cdot K_{PD} \cdot \frac{1 + s\tau_2}{1 + s\tau_1} \cdot \frac{K_{VCO}}{s} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply through by $s(1 + s\tau_1)$:**

$$
s(1 + s\tau_1) + \frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N}(1 + s\tau_2) = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Expand and collect terms** in standard 2nd-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$
s^2\tau_1 + s + \frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N} + s \cdot \frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO} \cdot \tau_2}{N} = 0
$$

**Step 8 — Divide by $\tau_1$ to normalize the $s^2$ coefficient:**

$$
s^2 + s\left(\frac{1}{\tau_1} + \frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO} \cdot \tau_2}{N \cdot \tau_1}\right) + \frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot \tau_1} = 0
$$

**Step 9 — Match coefficients** with the standard 2nd-order polynomial:

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot \tau_1}
$$

$$
2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{1}{\tau_1} + \frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO} \cdot \tau_2}{N \cdot \tau_1}
$$

**Step 10 — Solve for the natural frequency:**

$$
\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot \tau_1}}}
$$

**Step 11 — Solve for the damping factor:**

$$
\boxed{\zeta = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{N \cdot \tau_1}{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\tau_1} + \frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO} \cdot \tau_2}{N \cdot \tau_1}\right) = \frac{\tau_2}{2}\sqrt{\frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot \tau_1}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** As the multiplication factor $N$ increases, $\omega_n$ **decreases** (loop becomes slower) and $\zeta$ also changes. This is why very high multiplication ratios require careful loop design — the loop bandwidth shrinks, making the PLL more sluggish but with better phase noise performance at far-out offsets.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Lock Range and Capture Range

**Step 1 — Lock Range (Tracking Range):** When the PLL is locked, the maximum frequency deviation the VCO can track is determined by the maximum DC control voltage swing $\Delta V_c$ that the PFD + loop filter can deliver:

$$
\Delta\omega_L = K_{VCO} \cdot \Delta V_{c,max} = K_{VCO} \cdot K_{PD} \cdot \Delta\phi_{max}
$$

For a tri-state PFD (commonly used in CMOS PLLs), $\Delta\phi_{max} = 2\pi$ rad, giving:

$$
\boxed{\Delta\omega_L = 2\pi \cdot K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}
$$

**Step 2 — Capture Range:** The capture range is narrower than the lock range. It is the range over which the PLL can *acquire* lock from an unlocked state. The beat-frequency dynamics during capture lead to the empirical result:

$$
\boxed{\Delta\omega_C \approx \sqrt{\frac{\Delta\omega_L}{\tau_1}}}
$$

or equivalently, in terms of the loop filter time constant $\tau_1$:

$$
\Delta\omega_C \approx \Delta\omega_L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $\Delta\omega_C < \Delta\omega_L$?** During capture, the PLL must slew the VCO from a large initial frequency error to the lock point. The loop filter's low-pass nature attenuates the high-frequency beat-note between $f_{ref}$ and $f_{out}/N$, slowing down acquisition. Once locked, the loop only needs to track small perturbations, so the lock range is much larger.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem Style)

**Problem:** A PLL frequency multiplier uses a reference of $f_{ref} = 100$ kHz. The VCO has a tuning constant $K_{VCO} = 2\pi \times 10$ kHz/V. The PFD gain is $K_{PD} = 0.5$ V/rad. The loop filter is a 1st-order RC with $\tau_1 = 1$ ms. The divider ratio is $N = 64$.

**Find:** (a) Output frequency, (b) Natural frequency, (c) Lock range.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Output frequency:
$$f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref} = 64 \times 100 \text{ kHz} = 6.4 \text{ MHz}$$

**(b)** Natural frequency:
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot \tau_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 2\pi \times 10{,}000}{64 \times 0.001}}$$
$$= \sqrt{\frac{31{,}415.93}{0.064}} = \sqrt{490{,}873.97} \approx 700.6 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} \approx 111.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**(c)** Lock range (using $K_{PD} = 0.5$ V/rad, $K_{VCO} = 2\pi \times 10$ kHz/V, $\Delta\phi_{max} = 2\pi$):
$$\Delta\omega_L = K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO} \cdot 2\pi = 0.5 \times 2\pi \times 10{,}000 \times 2\pi$$
$$= 2\pi^2 \times 10{,}000 \approx 1.97 \times 10^5 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\Delta f_L \approx 31.4 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always convert $K_{VCO}$ from Hz/V to rad/s/V by multiplying by $2\pi$ **before** plugging into $\omega_n$ formulas. A common student error is to mix Hz and rad/s units in the same equation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PLL Multiplier vs. Other Techniques

| **Parameter** | **PLL Frequency Multiplier** | **Harmonic Generator (Diode)** | **DDS (Direct Digital Synthesis)** |
|---------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Spectral Purity | High (locked to crystal) | Poor (broad harmonics) | Excellent |
| Frequency Range | Wide (programmable) | Limited to harmonics of input | Limited by clock rate |
| Power Consumption | Moderate–High | Low | High (fast DAC needed) |
| Frequency Switching Speed | Slow (loop bandwidth limit) | N/A (fixed) | Very fast |
| CMOS Integration | Excellent | Poor | Excellent |
| Spurs / Harmonics | At $f_{ref}$ and multiples | All harmonics present | At clock and DAC images |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | High |

### 4.3 Mermaid Acquisition Sequence Diagram (Lock Process)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Reference Source
    participant PFD as Phase Frequency Detector
    participant LF as Loop Filter
    participant VCO as VCO
    participant DIV as Divider N
    participant OUT as Multiplied Output

    SRC->>PFD: Apply fref
    Note over PFD,VCO: Initial state: VCO free-running at f0
    VCO->>DIV: fout
    DIV->>PFD: fout / N
    PFD->>LF: Large error voltage Vd
    LF->>VCO: Adjust Vc to slew fout
    Note over VCO: fout slewing toward N times fref
    PFD->>LF: Decreasing Vd as phase aligns
    LF->>VCO: Fine-tune Vc
    Note over PFD,VCO: Loop reaches LOCK
    PFD->>LF: Vd constant (DC)
    LF->>VCO: Vc steady
    VCO->>OUT: Stable fout = N times fref
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of a PLL frequency multiplier," always show the **divider block explicitly inside the feedback path** between the VCO output and the PFD. Marks are commonly lost if the divider is omitted or placed outside the loop.

### 4.4 Physical Description of the Circuit (for Drawing Questions)

For drawing-based exam questions, the PLL frequency multiplier consists of the following cascaded blocks:

1. **Crystal Oscillator / Reference Source:** Generates a stable sinusoidal reference at $f_{ref}$.
2. **Phase Frequency Detector (PFD):** A digital circuit (typically using two D-flip-flops and an AND gate) that outputs UP and DOWN pulses proportional to the phase/frequency difference.
3. **Charge Pump (CP):** Converts PFD pulses into current pulses charging/discharging the loop filter.
4. **Loop Filter:** A 2nd-order passive RC network that integrates the charge pump current to produce the VCO control voltage.
5. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** A CMOS ring oscillator or LC oscillator whose frequency is a linear function of $V_c$.
6. **Frequency Divider (÷N):** A digital counter (ripple, synchronous, or dual-modulus prescaler) that divides the VCO output frequency by $N$.
7. **Output Buffer:** Buffers the VCO output to drive external loads.
